topic: Terraform Associate 004 - Objective 7: Maintain Infrastructure with Terraform
key_points: terraform show provides a comprehensive overview of all managed infrastructure in the current Terraform configuration; terraform state show requires a resource address and outputs human-readable detailed state of a specific resource; terraform state list lists all resources currently managed in a Terraform workspace without detailed attributes; TF_LOG environment variable enables detailed logging for Terraform; TF_LOG_PATH environment variable specifies the file path where Terraform logs will be saved
confusion: Distinguishing between terraform show, terraform state show, and terraform state list and their appropriate use cases

key_points: Child modules do not have access to root variables defined in terraform.tfvars by default; values must be explicitly passed via module blocks; Calling a module means including its contents into the configuration with specific input variable values using a module block; The label immediately after the module keyword is a local name used by the calling module to reference that module instance; terraform init installs modules into the current working directory and initializes the Terraform working directory; tolist() function converts a set to a list in Terraform; Terraform reports check failures as warnings but continues with the plan; terraform fmt modifies files in the current working directory only and does not automatically affect subdirectories; terraform init must be run before creating a new resource with Terraform
confusion: terraform fmt scope: it only affects the current working directory, not all subdirectories automatically

key_points: Variable sets in HCP Terraform allow cloud credentials and common tags to be defined once and shared across all workspaces in a project; Creating a pull request on the branch of a workspace's linked repository automatically triggers a speculative plan on that workspace

tool: Chrome MV3 Service Worker Restart Race Condition
description: Chrome MV3 service workers are killed after ~30s of inactivity and restarted on demand. When a message arrives immediately after restart, module-level state populated from chrome.storage.session during the previous lifetime hasn't been re-hydrated yet, so synchronous reads return stale or empty data. The fix is to expose the internal hydration promise (_hydratePromise) on the module's public API so the message handler can await it before reading state.
comparison: chrome.storage.session survives SW idle-kill but in-memory JS variables do not — the mismatch is the root cause of the race condition
use_cases: MV3 service worker message handling; async state hydration on SW restart; exposing _hydratePromise on public API; making handleGetContext async to await RC_Context._hydratePromise before calling getChunks()

tool: Chrome MV3 Async Message Handlers
description: chrome.runtime.onMessage handlers that call sendResponse asynchronously must either return true to keep the channel open or be declared async. Changing a handler to async function is safe and does not break the return true pattern. Mixing async handlers with sendResponse is the idiomatic MV3 pattern for awaiting internal state before replying.
comparison: Non-async handler forgetting return true causes port to close before reply arrives and caller gets undefined; async handler with return true keeps channel open correctly
use_cases: MV3 message handler async patterns; awaiting internal state before sending response; keeping message channel open with return true

tool: 429 Error Shape Mismatch — Proxy String vs Anthropic Object
description: When two different backends both return HTTP 429, their error body shapes can differ. Anthropic returns {error:{type,message}} (nested object) while a Cloudflare Worker proxy returns {error:'insufficient_tokens'} (flat string). Code checking body.error.message works for Anthropic but silently gets undefined for the proxy string, swallowing the specific error code. The fix is to branch on which call path is active before parsing.
comparison: Anthropic: body.error.message (nested object); Cloudflare proxy: body.error as string — typeof body.error is the reliable discriminator, not HTTP status alone
use_cases: multi-backend error parsing; discriminating error shapes by typeof; throwing named codes DEMO_LIMIT_REACHED or INSUFFICIENT_TOKENS based on call path

tool: Named Error Codes vs Generic HTTP Status Propagation
description: Propagating raw HTTP status codes to UI copy requires a switch on numbers and makes copy fragile when a status is reused for multiple conditions. Named string error codes (RATE_LIMITED, DEMO_LIMIT_REACHED, INSUFFICIENT_TOKENS) thrown at the API layer and caught by the UI layer allow each condition to have distinct user-facing copy, independent of HTTP semantics. Each code maps one-to-one to an ERROR_COPY entry.
comparison: Raw HTTP status codes are fragile and ambiguous (e.g. 429 reused for rate limit, trial exhausted, token depletion); named string codes are explicit and map cleanly to UI copy
use_cases: API error propagation to UI; named error code lookup in ERROR_COPY; splitting on colon to extract code and message

tool: HMAC-SHA256 Webhook Verification with SubtleCrypto
description: Gumroad and most webhook providers sign POST bodies with a shared secret using HMAC-SHA256 and include the hex digest in a request header. Verification requires importing the secret as a CryptoKey via crypto.subtle.importKey, computing the HMAC signature, hex-encoding the result, and doing a timing-safe byte-by-byte XOR comparison to prevent timing oracle attacks. Using === string comparison leaks timing information proportional to the matching prefix length.
comparison: SubtleCrypto provides HMAC signing but not timing-safe comparison — manual XOR loop is required; if GUMROAD_WEBHOOK_SECRET is unset, importKey silently creates a valid key for 'undefined', accepting all wrong-signature requests
use_cases: webhook signature verification; Cloudflare Worker crypto; preventing timing oracle attacks; guarding against missing env vars before importKey

tool: Webhook Replay Attack Prevention via Idempotency Keys
description: A valid signed webhook can be replayed by a malicious actor or re-delivered by provider retry logic, causing duplicate purchases to be credited. The standard defense is idempotency: store the unique sale ID in KV after first successful processing and reject subsequent requests with the same ID. Returning 200 on duplicates is intentional — returning 4xx causes the provider to keep retrying.
comparison: Returning 4xx on duplicate causes provider retry loops; returning 200 with {duplicate:true} stops retries cleanly. KV read errors during idempotency check should fail open to avoid blocking legitimate purchases during KV outage.
use_cases: webhook idempotency with Cloudflare KV; preventing duplicate purchase credits; handling provider retry logic; KV key pattern sale:<saleId> with 1-year TTL

tool: Environment Variable Guard — Hard Fail vs Silent Degradation
description: When a Worker depends on a secret, the absence of the binding causes env.SECRET to be undefined at runtime. JavaScript string coercion makes 'undefined' a valid wrong key value, silently accepting all requests or creating a different HMAC. The correct behavior is to hard-fail at the top of the handler: check the env var is truthy before any crypto and return 500 with a server_misconfiguration body.
comparison: Silent degradation allows misconfigured secrets to appear valid and pass crypto checks; hard fail surfaces configuration errors immediately during deployment testing rather than in production
use_cases: Cloudflare Worker secret validation; guarding GUMROAD_WEBHOOK_SECRET before crypto; detecting missing KV namespace bindings at runtime

tool: Delta-Based Analytics Writes to Avoid Over-Counting
description: When a function is called multiple times during a session and each call writes a cumulative total to storage, the total inflates unless the write is idempotent. The pattern for session-scoped counters is to track _lastRecordedValue in memory alongside the live counter, compute delta = current - lastRecorded, write only the delta to storage, then update _lastRecorded = current. The session marker should fire only when the counter transitions from 0 to 1, not on every write.
comparison: Storing accumulated total directly in a handler called every turn produces triangular-number inflation: a 3-turn session writes 1+2+3=6 instead of 3; delta-based writes are resilient to repeated calls and don't require locking
use_cases: session-scoped turn counters in Chrome extensions; analytics delta writes; preventing over-counting in repeated handler calls

tool: Content Script Silent Failure — Missing Failure Signal
description: A content script catch block that only logs and returns leaves the sidebar in its last known state with no indication that extraction failed. The sidebar waits for a READING_CHUNK message and has no independent timeout or health-check. Every early-return path including catch blocks and quality-gate failures must call sendFailureChunk() before returning so the sidebar can render its fallback CTA instead of staying silent.
comparison: Original catch blocks around getStrategy() and strategy.extract() logged errors but silently returned, leaving the sidebar frozen with no user feedback; sendFailureChunk() on every exit path enables proper fallback rendering
use_cases: content script error signaling; sidebar fallback CTA on extraction failure; ensuring all exit paths call failure signal

tool: Cloudflare Worker KV Fail-Open Policy
description: In a paid proxy flow, KV stores hold entitlement state. If a KV read throws due to outage or misconfigured binding, rejecting the user request makes the product unavailable whenever KV is degraded. Fail-open means wrapping every KV read in try/catch, logging the error, and allowing the request to proceed as if the user has a valid entitlement. A response header X-Entitlement-Source: fallback enables monitoring without breaking user experience.
comparison: Fail-open on KV read allows requests during outages; fail-open does NOT apply to KV writes — failed writes after a successful Anthropic response should be logged but the user already received their response so nothing to roll back
use_cases: KV entitlement checks in paid proxy; resilient Cloudflare Worker design; monitoring fail-open events via response headers

tool: SSE Stream Tee + ctx.waitUntil() for Optimistic Deduction Correction
description: When proxying a streaming Anthropic response, the actual token count is only known at the end of the stream in message_delta usage events. To avoid blocking the stream, deduct a flat estimate up front, forward the live stream to the client immediately via branch A of a ReadableStream tee, and consume branch B in a ctx.waitUntil() background task that parses SSE events to get actual usage. After the stream completes, correct the balance to estimated minus actual.
comparison: Only ctx.waitUntil() keeps the Worker alive post-response; a bare setTimeout or unawaited promise would be killed when the response completes
use_cases: streaming Anthropic proxy with token correction; ReadableStream tee for dual consumption; ctx.waitUntil() for post-response background tasks; optimistic deduction with SSE usage parsing

tool: chrome.storage.session vs chrome.storage.local in MV3
description: chrome.storage.local persists across browser restarts and extension reloads and is the right store for user config, API keys, highlights, and accumulated analytics. chrome.storage.session is scoped to the browser session: it survives SW idle-kill and restart within the same session but is cleared on browser close. It is the right store for ephemeral in-session data like the rolling context window.
comparison: storage.local survives browser restart; storage.session survives SW idle-kill but not browser close; in-memory JS module variables survive neither SW kill nor browser restart and must be re-read from session storage on SW restart
use_cases: choosing storage tier in MV3 extensions; ephemeral context window storage; persisting user config and analytics across restarts

tool: Exposing Internal Promises on Module Public APIs
description: When a module performs async initialization and stores the result in module-level variables, callers that run immediately after the module loads may read before init completes. The clean fix without making all getters async is to expose the initialization promise itself as a named export (_hydratePromise). Callers that need to guarantee init is complete can await it once before their first read; callers that only run after init can skip the await.
comparison: Making all getters async forces every caller to change; exposing _hydratePromise as a named export lets only the callers that need it await init, leaving others unchanged. Underscore prefix signals internal implementation detail.
use_cases: async module initialization patterns; hydrating module state from storage; single-await pattern before first read; MV3 service worker module design

tool: Structured Three-Part AI Response Parsing with Fallback
description: Asking the model to emit explicit section markers ([OBSERVATION], [INSIGHT], [QUESTION]) makes splitting a response into UI sections deterministic. The fallback for responses without markers is sentence-boundary splitting on '. ' distributing sentences across three slots. Two quality guards in every system prompt prevent the insight restating the article verbatim and the question having a correct answer.
comparison: Marker-based parsing is deterministic but breaks if the model wraps markers in markdown formatting; sentence-boundary fallback handles short follow-up turns without markers
use_cases: structured AI response parsing; three-part UI section rendering; fallback sentence-boundary splitting; prompt engineering to prevent markdown wrapping of markers

tool: Session-Scoped State Reset on New Conversation
description: In-memory variables that accumulate session state (_sessionTurnCount, _lastRecordedTurns, _sessionPersona, _conversationLog) must be explicitly reset at the start of each new conversation, not just on page load. If the user starts a new conversation mid-session, state from the previous conversation carries over and corrupts counters and analytics. The reset point is _handleDiscussCta, the one user action that definitively starts a new conversation.
comparison: Resetting on page navigation is incorrect because navigation triggers a new READING_CHUNK but not necessarily a new conversation; resetting on user intent (_handleDiscussCta) is the correct trigger
use_cases: conversation state reset in Chrome extensions; preventing counter corruption across conversations; tying state reset to user intent not navigation events

tool: vm.createContext (Node.js vm module)
description: When a JavaScript array is created inside a vm.createContext sandbox via a [] literal, its prototype chain belongs to the vm context's Array, not the host Node.js Array. In Node 22, assert.deepStrictEqual(vmArray, []) throws 'Values have same structure but are not reference-equal' because deepStrictEqual detects the prototype mismatch even for empty arrays. Arrays produced by calling .filter() or .map() on a host-context array come back as host arrays and compare cleanly — only arrays born entirely inside the vm are affected.
comparison: Arrays originating inside the vm sandbox fail deepStrictEqual comparison against host [] literals due to prototype mismatch; arrays derived from host-context arrays via filter/map do not have this problem.
use_cases: Testing code that runs inside vm sandboxes; Asserting on values returned from sandboxed scripts; Node.js unit testing with vm module isolation

tool: Write / Edit file tools
description: The Write and Edit file tools silently truncate output at roughly 28000 bytes — the write appears to succeed with no error returned but the file on disk is shorter than the content sent. The truncation happens mid-line with no warning, making it hard to detect until the file fails to parse. The Read tool can also show stale cached content from earlier in the same session, making the file look complete when the disk copy is actually truncated.
comparison: Silent truncation with no error vs bash heredoc approach which reliably writes full content without size limits.
use_cases: Writing large files (>25 KB) in agentic coding sessions; Debugging unexpected parse errors in generated files; Recovering from silent write failures

tool: bash heredoc (cat >> file << 'ENDOFFILE')
description: cat >> file << 'ENDOFFILE' appends content to a file verbatim with no shell expansion because the delimiter is quoted. Quoting the ENDOFFILE sentinel prevents the shell from expanding $variables or backticks inside the block. For a complete rewrite, write with heredoc to /tmp/scratch then cp /tmp/scratch destination. Raw Unicode characters inside a heredoc may cause misparsing of the ENDOFFILE marker due to encoding issues.
comparison: More reliable than file write tools for large files; avoids the ~28 KB truncation limit; requires manual verification with xxd or tail afterward.
use_cases: Writing files larger than ~25 KB in agentic sessions; Reliable fallback when file tools truncate; Complete file rewrites via temp file pattern

tool: git show HEAD:path
description: git show HEAD:relative/path/to/file prints the file's content at the last commit to stdout. Piping to a temp file and then copying avoids the git checkout permission error that can occur when the working-tree file is locked by the mounted filesystem (Operation not permitted). The pattern git show HEAD:path > /tmp/copy && cp /tmp/copy path works even on read-only-mounted paths where git's atomic rename strategy fails.
comparison: Safer than git checkout for recovering files on read-only-mounted or locked paths; requires confirming the desired version exists in git log before overwriting.
use_cases: Recovering truncated files from git history; Restoring files on read-only-mounted filesystems; Bypassing git checkout permission errors

tool: buildSystemPrompt — Approach A (article highlight injection)
description: When a user returns to an article they've read before, previously starred passages (rcHighlights[] in chrome.storage.local) are injected into buildSystemPrompt as a 'Cross-session context —' block. Up to three highlights are chosen most-recent-first using .slice(-3), each truncated to 200 characters to control token cost. The model is instructed to let them inform its observation and insight without quoting them directly back. URL canonicalization strips tracking params, fragments, and trailing slashes before comparison.
comparison: Compared to Approach B (domain-level summary) which was rejected for storage complexity and irrelevant context risk, and Approach C (full conversation replay) rejected for token cost; Approach A is lightweight and article-specific.
use_cases: Personalizing AI reading assistant responses for returning users; Cross-session context injection in browser extensions; Token-efficient user history incorporation in prompts

tool: buildSystemPrompt — Approach D (preference signal)
description: rcHighlightStats accumulates a count of how many times the user has saved each section type (observation/insight/question) across all sessions. If total saves >= 3 AND one type holds a strict majority (>50% of all saves), a single-sentence preference note is appended to the system prompt. A 3/3/0 split intentionally produces dominantSection = null because the gate requires strictly more than 50%, not exactly half. The min-3 threshold avoids noise from the very first interaction.
comparison: Uses strict majority gate rather than plurality to prevent early saves from permanently biasing the prompt before sufficient signal accumulates.
use_cases: Long-run user preference detection across sessions; Adaptive prompt weighting based on user behavior; Signal-gated personalization to avoid noise from sparse data

tool: _canonicalizeUrl helper
description: A _canonicalizeUrl(url) helper normalises both stored and current URLs before comparison by using new URL() to parse, deleting known tracking query params (utm_*, fbclid, gclid, ref, source), stripping the hash, stripping the trailing slash, and lowercasing the result. A regex fallback handles malformed URLs that new URL() rejects. new URL() is not available in all JavaScript environments and must be explicitly injected as an extra global in vm sandbox contexts.
comparison: Required because save-time URLs may differ from revisit-time URLs due to UTM params, fragments, or trailing slashes; without canonicalization, the same article would not match across sessions.
use_cases: Cross-session article highlight matching; URL normalization for storage key consistency; Deduplication of URLs with tracking parameters

tool: buildSystemPrompt layer ordering
description: buildSystemPrompt composes the final system prompt by string-appending layers in a fixed order where no layer may modify content added by a prior layer. The structural invariant is unconditionally always the last thing appended, making it impossible for any future layer addition to accidentally bury the response-format instruction. Cross-session layers (4a highlights, 4b preference) slot between the trajectory layer and the follow-up suffix.
comparison: Append-only fixed-order composition prevents accidental layer interaction; contrast with dynamic reordering approaches that risk burying critical structural instructions.
use_cases: Composing multi-layer system prompts safely; Enforcing response format instructions in AI assistants; Extensible prompt architecture for feature additions

tool: vm.createContext global injection
description: vm.createContext creates a minimal sandbox that does not include Node.js globals like URL, fetch, TextEncoder, etc. If the script under test calls new URL(str) it will throw ReferenceError: URL is not defined unless URL is passed in the extraGlobals object when calling loadScript. The chrome mock and any global stubs the script depends on must all be injected explicitly. Additionally, get(keys, cb) {} method shorthand in object literals is parsed as a getter accessor definition in JavaScript and does not accept parameters — always use get: function(keys, cb) {} colon syntax in mock objects.
comparison: vm sandbox is more isolated than require/import-based loading but requires explicit injection of all globals; nothing leaks in from the host Node context automatically.
use_cases: Unit testing browser extension scripts in Node.js; Injecting mock globals into vm sandboxes; Avoiding ReferenceError for globals in sandboxed test environments

tool: buildSystemPrompt token budget adjustment
description: buildSystemPrompt estimates prompt token count as characters divided by 4 and logs a console.warn if it exceeds the budget. The budget was raised from 400 to 600 tokens to accommodate new cross-session layers (up to 3 highlights x 200 chars each ~150 tokens of headroom plus the preference sentence). The warning is monitoring-only and never truncates or throws, so raising the threshold is a documentation and observability change rather than a behavioral one. Estimation heuristic: 200 characters ~= 50 tokens at chars/4.
comparison: Monitoring-only budget enforcement vs hard truncation; raising the threshold has no behavioral impact but correctly reflects expected prompt size after new layer additions.
use_cases: Observability for prompt size growth; Token cost estimation for new prompt layers; Budget tracking in multi-layer prompt systems

tool: ADR — Approach A + D architecture decision
description: Approach B (domain-level summary across all articles on a site) was rejected for requiring aggregation across multiple articles adding storage query complexity and risking irrelevant context injection. Approach C (per-article context replay of entire prior conversations) was rejected due to token cost and risk of the model getting stuck in prior conversation frames. A + D together give a lightweight signal (a few hundred tokens) that personalizes the response without constraining it: A anchors to what the user found interesting in this specific article, D nudges section weighting based on long-run preference patterns. Both layers are append-only, read-only, and fail silently when storage is empty.
comparison: A+D preferred over B (too complex, irrelevant context risk) and C (too expensive, frame-lock risk); A+D keeps the fast path for first visits completely unchanged.
use_cases: Designing cross-session memory for AI reading assistants; Selecting lightweight personalization strategies over heavy context replay; Architectural decision-making for prompt personalization layers

tool: Write Tool (Cowork / Claude Code)
description: The Write tool silently truncates files beyond roughly 191 lines (~7 KB). It returns success but the file on disk is incomplete. The truncation always happens at the same byte boundary, making it a hard limit rather than flakiness. The only reliable workaround for large files is to write them via python3 inside a bash call: open the path, call f.write(content), close.
comparison: Write tool vs bash python3 write: Write tool is convenient but has a hard ~150-191 line limit with silent success reporting; python3 bash write has no such limit and is reliable for large files.
use_cases: Writing large generated files; Debugging silent file truncation issues; Any file expected to exceed ~150 lines in automated tooling

tool: Node vm.createContext with Cloudflare Workers ESM
description: Cloudflare Workers use `export default { async fetch() {} }` (ESM), which Node's vm module cannot evaluate directly. The fix is a source transform: replace `export default ` with `self.workerExport = ` via regex before passing the source to vm.Script. The sandbox object is assigned to both `self` and `window`. All Fetch API globals must be injected into the sandbox explicitly from Node 18+ globalThis.
comparison: Direct vm eval vs regex-transformed eval: Direct eval fails on ESM export syntax; transformed eval captures the export as a sandbox property and works correctly.
use_cases: Testing Cloudflare Workers in Node without a live runtime; Unit testing ESM worker fetch handlers; Sandboxed evaluation of worker source code

tool: Mutable Fetch Proxy Pattern
description: When testing a worker loaded once into a vm sandbox, individual tests cannot swap the fetch mock by reloading or by reassigning sandbox.fetch because the worker captured the original reference at eval time. The solution is a mutable proxy: define `let fetchProxy = defaultMock` in the test file, inject `fetch: (...args) => fetchProxy(...args)` into the sandbox, then assign `fetchProxy = myMock` inside each test. The indirection ensures every call goes through the current value of fetchProxy at call time.
comparison: Direct fetch injection vs mutable proxy injection: Injecting `fetch: fetchProxy` directly captures the value at injection time making per-test reassignment ineffective; the wrapper function indirection allows per-test mock swapping without reloading the worker.
use_cases: Per-test fetch mocking in vm sandbox worker tests; Avoiding expensive worker reloads between tests; Simulating different Anthropic API responses per test case

tool: Per-User KV Entitlement Model
description: Replaces a single global KV counter (50/day across all users) with three separate KV namespaces: FREE_TRIAL, TOKEN_BALANCE, and UNLIMITED, each keyed by extension ID. Priority is checked in order: UNLIMITED → TOKEN_BALANCE → FREE_TRIAL → reject. A user can hold multiple entitlements simultaneously; the highest-tier one wins. Separating namespaces keeps reads cheap and avoids read-modify-write races between tiers.
comparison: Global daily counter vs per-user namespace model: Global counter is simple but cannot support tiered entitlements or per-user tracking; per-user namespace model supports multiple simultaneous entitlement tiers, is cheaper to read (early exit on highest tier), and avoids race conditions.
use_cases: Monetised Cloudflare Worker APIs; Tiered access control with free trial, token balance, and unlimited tiers; Extension or plugin entitlement management

tool: Fail-Open KV Policy in Cloudflare Workers
description: Every KV read in the worker is wrapped in try/catch. If a namespace call throws due to transient KV outage or binding misconfiguration, the worker allows the request through anyway and sets the response header `X-Entitlement-Source: fallback`. This degrades a KV outage to 'everyone gets through' rather than 'everyone is blocked'. The trade-off is potential bypass of paid limits during prolonged outages, considered acceptable versus blocking all legitimate paying users.
comparison: Fail-open vs fail-closed KV error handling: Fail-closed blocks all users on outage including paying customers; fail-open allows all users through on outage risking limit bypass but preserving paying user experience.
use_cases: Production Cloudflare Workers with KV-backed entitlements; High-availability API gateways where blocking paying users is unacceptable; Systems where brief abuse risk is tolerated over availability loss

tool: Optimistic Token Deduction with Post-Response Correction
description: The worker deducts a flat estimate (TOKENS_PER_REQUEST, default 2500) from the token balance before forwarding to Anthropic. After the Anthropic response streams back, the actual token count is read from SSE events and the balance is corrected: `correctedBalance = tokensRemaining + tokensDeducted - actualTokens`. This avoids a second KV read by using values already in memory from the initial entitlement check. The correction write happens in ctx.waitUntil().
comparison: Pre-deduction flat estimate vs exact post-response deduction: Exact deduction requires waiting for full response before responding to client; optimistic deduction allows immediate streaming to client with async correction, but correction can be lost if the worker is terminated early.
use_cases: Token-metered streaming API workers; Cloudflare Workers forwarding to Anthropic or similar LLM APIs; Any monetised streaming proxy needing balance management

tool: SSE Stream tee() for Dual Consumption in Cloudflare Workers
description: A ReadableStream can only be consumed once. To both forward the Anthropic SSE stream to the client and parse it for token usage, call `response.body.tee()` which returns `[branchA, branchB]`. Branch A is passed directly to the client response. Branch B is consumed in a ctx.waitUntil() promise that reads chunks, decodes them, and extracts `message_start` and `message_delta` usage events. Backpressure on branch B does not stall branch A delivery to the client, but tee() buffers data internally until both branches have consumed it.
comparison: Single-consumer stream vs tee'd dual-consumer stream: Single consumer forces a choice between forwarding to client or parsing for usage; tee() enables both simultaneously with runtime-managed buffering, at the cost of potential unbounded buffer growth if branch B is slow.
use_cases: Streaming LLM proxy workers needing usage telemetry; Any Cloudflare Worker that must both forward and inspect a response body; Token accounting on streamed SSE responses

tool: ctx.waitUntil()
description: Once a Cloudflare Worker's fetch handler returns a Response, the runtime may terminate the isolate. Any async work that needs to run after the response is sent must be registered with `ctx.waitUntil(promise)`. The runtime keeps the isolate alive until all waitUntil promises settle. This is the correct pattern for fire-and-forget side effects such as correcting KV balances after streaming. Errors in waitUntil promises are silent unless a .catch() is attached.
comparison: Inline async work vs ctx.waitUntil: Inline work before returning the response delays the client; waitUntil allows immediate response return while bookkeeping continues asynchronously, but errors are silent and work can be lost on early termination.
use_cases: Post-response KV writes in Cloudflare Workers; Fire-and-forget logging or analytics after response; Token balance correction after streaming completes

tool: HMAC-SHA256 Webhook Verification with SubtleCrypto
description: Cloudflare Workers have no Node crypto module. Webhook HMAC verification uses the Web Crypto API (SubtleCrypto) exclusively. Steps: (1) crypto.subtle.importKey with HMAC/SHA-256, (2) crypto.subtle.sign, (3) convert ArrayBuffer to lowercase hex string via Uint8Array mapping, (4) timing-safe comparison against the webhook header signature. Both importKey and sign are async and require await.
comparison: Node crypto HMAC vs SubtleCrypto HMAC: Node crypto provides synchronous hmac helpers; SubtleCrypto is fully async, requires explicit key import, and uses ArrayBuffer outputs requiring manual hex conversion.
use_cases: Gumroad webhook signature verification in Cloudflare Workers; Any webhook HMAC verification in edge/serverless environments without Node crypto; Security-critical request authentication in Workers

tool: Timing-Safe String Comparison (XOR Accumulation)
description: Standard string equality (`===`) short-circuits on the first differing character, leaking information about how many leading characters match via timing side-channel. The safe pattern accumulates XOR differences across the full string without early exit: `let result = 0; for (let i = 0; i < a.length; i++) result |= a.charCodeAt(i) ^ b.charCodeAt(i); return result === 0`. Length must be verified first; this pattern is only safe when both strings are the same length.
comparison: Standard === comparison vs XOR accumulation: === leaks timing information on mismatch position; XOR accumulation always runs the full loop regardless of where differences occur, preventing timing side-channel attacks.
use_cases: HMAC signature comparison for webhooks; Any security-sensitive string equality check where timing attacks are a concern; Cryptographic token or secret comparison

tool: Gumroad Webhook Payload Parser
description: Gumroad sends webhook POST bodies as `application/x-www-form-urlencoded`, not JSON. Custom product fields use bracket notation in the form key: `custom_fields[extension_id]`. Parse with `new URLSearchParams(await request.text())` then read with `params.get('custom_fields[extension_id]')` — the bracket characters are literal parts of the key name, not array syntax. The product permalink is available as `params.get('permalink')` and is the canonical product identifier.
comparison: request.json() vs URLSearchParams(request.text()): request.json() throws on Gumroad webhooks because Content-Type is x-www-form-urlencoded; URLSearchParams correctly parses the form-encoded body including bracket-notation custom fields.
use_cases: Gumroad purchase webhook handling in Cloudflare Workers; Provisioning entitlements on sale events; Extracting custom field data from Gumroad webhook payloads

tool: 90-Day Inactivity Reset Logic for Free Trials
description: The free trial reset rule requires two independent conditions to both be true: (1) used < total (user is not exhausted) AND (2) lastActivity is older than 90 days. Exhausted users are never reset regardless of inactivity, preventing abuse via deliberate dormancy to recycle free credits. The lastActivity timestamp is updated on every request including those from exhausted users, measuring inactivity from the most recent attempt rather than from exhaustion.
comparison: Reset on inactivity alone vs dual-condition reset: Resetting exhausted users on inactivity creates a recycling loophole; requiring non-exhaustion as a condition alongside inactivity closes the loophole while still releasing stale non-exhausted allocations.
use_cases: Free trial entitlement management in monetised APIs; Preventing free credit abuse through dormancy cycling; KV-backed usage tracking with inactivity-based cleanup

tool: git show HEAD:<path> for File Recovery
description: `git checkout HEAD -- <file>` can be blocked by filesystem permissions in sandboxed environments. The workaround pipes blob content to stdout first then copies it: `git show HEAD:<relative-path> > /tmp/restore.js && cp /tmp/restore.js <destination>`. The `git show` command reads the object from the git database without touching the working tree, bypassing the permission check on the destination path.
comparison: git checkout HEAD vs git show HEAD piped to cp: git checkout writes directly to the working tree and can be blocked by destination path permissions; git show reads from the git object database to stdout, allowing the content to be written via a temp file with cp.
use_cases: File recovery in sandboxed or permission-restricted environments; Restoring files when direct working tree writes are blocked; Extracting historical file content without modifying the working tree

tool: wrangler .dev.vars
description: When running `wrangler dev` locally, secrets are not available from deployed KV bindings. Wrangler reads them from a file named `.dev.vars` in the worker directory, formatted as KEY=VALUE pairs (same as .env). This file must be in .gitignore as it contains plaintext API keys. It is the local equivalent of `wrangler secret put` and must be created manually after cloning the repo. It does not live inside .wrangler/ and must be gitignored explicitly.
comparison: .dev.vars vs wrangler secret put: wrangler secret put stores secrets in Cloudflare's encrypted secret store for deployed workers; .dev.vars provides the same secrets to wrangler dev locally in plaintext and must be manually managed and gitignored.
use_cases: Local development of Cloudflare Workers requiring secrets; Managing ANTHROPIC_API_KEY and similar credentials for wrangler dev; Onboarding developers to a Workers project requiring local secret setup

tool: Lazy client instantiation with @lru_cache
description: Replacing eager client = Anthropic() at module top-level with a get_client() function decorated with @lru_cache(maxsize=1) defers construction until first use. Modules import without an API key, unit tests don't hit the network at import time, and httpx never tries to wire up a SOCKS proxy in sandboxed CI runners that lack socksio. The cached client is shared across the whole process, identical in behavior to the eager version once warmed up.
comparison: Eager module-level instantiation requires API key at import time and causes network/proxy issues in CI; lazy cached version avoids all of these with identical runtime behavior once warmed up.
use_cases: unit testing without network access; sandboxed CI environments; avoiding import-time side effects; shared client across process lifetime

tool: Universal retry wrapper as a single choke point
description: Hoisting exponential-backoff retry into one function (call_with_retry) that every caller routes through means transient 5xx/529s only need to be handled correctly once. Per-module retry duplicates the logic and inevitably drifts; a shared wrapper also lets you layer telemetry, caching, and a cost ledger at the same choke point with zero changes to call sites.
comparison: Per-module retry logic duplicates handling and drifts over time; a single shared wrapper centralizes correctness and allows layering of concerns with zero call-site changes.
use_cases: resilient API clients; centralized telemetry; cost tracking; transient error handling for LLM APIs

tool: Hysteresis-aware tier mapping
description: Hysteresis prevents oscillation at threshold boundaries by using different up-thresholds and down-thresholds. For a Low/Medium/High tier system with break-points at 6 and 8, climbing requires reaching threshold + buffer (e.g. 6.5 and 8.5), and dropping requires falling below threshold - buffer (e.g. 5.5 and 7.5). The result is a sticky current tier where small noise around the boundary doesn't cause flapping. Without hysteresis, a metric averaging exactly 8.0 flips between High and Medium on every recalculation.
comparison: Simple threshold mapping causes flapping at boundaries; hysteresis-aware mapping with symmetric buffers prevents oscillation and produces stable tier assignments.
use_cases: adaptive difficulty systems; performance tier classification; control systems with discrete states; metric-to-tier mapping for noisy signals

tool: Variable shadowing inside a loop body
description: Inside a for loop, writing history = entry.get('confidence_history') or [] rebinds the local name history for every subsequent iteration, clobbering the outer history variable. Python scoping does not isolate the body of a for loop; assignments persist into later iterations. The first iteration works correctly because history still points to the outer list; the bug only fires on iteration 2+, making it appear stochastic. Fix: rename to a non-colliding local such as entry_history.
comparison: Outer variable and loop-body variable sharing the same name causes silent clobbering; unique naming per scope avoids the issue entirely.
use_cases: debugging intermittent loop bugs; Python scoping awareness; code review for variable naming

tool: Module-import-time binding of config values
description: from config import DATA_DIR captures the value at import time, not a reference to the attribute. Later monkeypatching config.DATA_DIR does NOT update the importing module's bound copy. You must monkeypatch.setattr(pipeline.convert, 'DATA_DIR', new_path) for each module that imported it. Derived constants like HISTORY_PATH computed at import time also need to be rebound separately. Mitigation: read DATA_DIR lazily inside functions per-call, or accept per-module rebind in tests.
comparison: Import-time binding creates immutable local copies that ignore later config changes; lazy per-function imports or explicit per-module monkeypatching are the workarounds.
use_cases: test configuration overrides; monkeypatching config in pytest; understanding Python import semantics

tool: SHA-256-keyed response cache for LLM calls
description: A single on-disk cache keyed on hash(model + max_tokens + prompt) automatically deduplicates re-runs of identical API calls. The prompt text itself goes into the key so any prompt edit naturally invalidates without explicit cache-busting logic. LRU eviction via Python's insertion-ordered dicts: pop and re-insert on read to mark as recently-used, drop oldest via next(iter(dict)) when over the cap. An env-var bypass (MINDCI_CACHE_DISABLE=1) forces fresh runs during debugging.
comparison: Hashing prompt-template files separately would over-invalidate on every edit; prompt-text-in-key is the correct granularity for natural invalidation.
use_cases: LLM cost optimization; deduplicating API calls across re-runs; on-disk response caching; debugging with cache bypass

tool: Streamlit session state vs durable disk snapshot
description: Streamlit's st.session_state lives in process memory and dies on browser refresh or app restart. For multi-step flows, snapshot relevant keys to a JSON file on every state mutation and restore at the top of the renderer if no in-memory session exists. Delete on completion. Wrap reads/writes in try/except: pass so persistence failures never block the UI. Pattern uses three helpers: _save, _load, _clear with five call sites: start, submit, skip, next, end.
comparison: Session state alone is volatile and lost on refresh; disk snapshot provides durable best-effort persistence without blocking the UI on failure.
use_cases: multi-step Streamlit flows; mock interview state persistence; multi-question grading workflows; resilient UI state management

tool: Debounced filesystem watcher pattern
description: Editor atomic save sequences fire 2-5 watchdog events in rapid succession. A Timer-based debounce cancels any pending timer on each event and starts a new one for N seconds; only when the timer fires does the callback run with the accumulated set of changed files. ~2.5s is a sensible debounce window. The callback must not run synchronously inside the event handler as the watchdog thread blocks while processing and will miss subsequent events.
comparison: Synchronous in-handler processing blocks the watchdog thread and misses events; Timer-based debounce coalesces rapid events and runs the callback asynchronously.
use_cases: filesystem watchers for editors; event coalescing; CI auto-reload triggers; debouncing rapid file change notifications

tool: Pydantic forward-compatible schema evolution
description: Adding new fields as Optional[T] = None lets existing JSON deserialize without modification. The price is that you can never change a field's semantics, only add new ones. For a personal tool this is acceptable; for production you would want a version field and explicit migrations. When the loader can return either old or new shape, prefer entry.get('field') or default over assuming the key exists.
comparison: Required new fields break old JSON; Optional fields with defaults allow forward-compatible evolution at the cost of semantic immutability of existing fields.
use_cases: schema migration for personal tools; backward-compatible JSON evolution; Pydantic model versioning

tool: Cassette-style integration tests with monkeypatched API
description: For LLM-driven pipelines, integration testing means exercising cross-module wiring (parse, save, reload, process) with the network stubbed at one point rather than running real API calls. Replace the API choke-point function with a canned-response generator; assertions cover whether the right files were written, the right entries were updated, and the right calls were made in the right order. A single fixture stubs the API and assertions are about filesystem state and module interactions, not LLM output quality.
comparison: Real API calls in integration tests are slow, costly, and non-deterministic; monkeypatched canned responses enable fast, deterministic wiring verification.
use_cases: LLM pipeline integration testing; cross-module wiring verification; filesystem state assertions; mocking at the API choke point

tool: Cost telemetry as middleware
description: Wrapping API calls to log response.usage.input_tokens and output_tokens to a daily JSON row gives free spend visibility. Pricing constants for input and output tokens-per-million live in config and are env-overridable. A get_usage_summary(days) helper rolls today and last-N-days totals for the dashboard. Wrap logging in try/except so telemetry failures never abort a real call.
comparison: No telemetry leaves spend invisible; middleware logging adds observability with zero impact on call success due to best-effort error handling.
use_cases: LLM spend tracking; personal API cost dashboards; observability middleware; daily usage rollups

tool: GitHub Actions runtime deprecation cycles
description: GitHub Actions migrates the Node.js runtime under named actions on a schedule (e.g., Node 20 to 24 in June 2026). Pinned major versions (actions/checkout@v4) keep working until the deprecation deadline; upgrading to the next major version is usually a no-op for the workflow contract. Track the deprecation banner in workflow runs and bump the major version when it appears.
comparison: Staying on deprecated runtimes eventually breaks workflows; proactive major version bumps on banner appearance keeps CI stable with minimal effort.
use_cases: CI maintenance; GitHub Actions version management; Node runtime upgrade tracking

tool: Pre-commit hooks vs CI redundancy
description: Pre-commit and CI run the same checks (ruff, format, tests) but at different points in the loop. Pre-commit shifts feedback left to the developer's machine; issues caught before push are ~10x cheaper than issues caught in CI. The two layers together mean pre-commit catches the common case locally while CI is the safety net for forgotten hooks (--no-verify) and the authoritative gate before merge.
comparison: Pre-commit alone has no enforcement guarantee; CI alone gives slow feedback. Together they provide fast local feedback with an authoritative remote gate.
use_cases: developer feedback loop optimization; CI/CD pipeline design; enforcing code quality standards; pre-push validation

tool: Anthropic structured output as parser elimination
description: String-based parsing of LLM output using regex to hunt for markers and splitting on delimiters is the dominant source of skipped-entry warnings. Switching to JSON-schema-constrained structured output via Anthropic's tool_use mode eliminates the parser layer entirely as the API guarantees parseable JSON conforming to the schema. Migration is per-call-site: define the JSON schema, drop the regex parser, replace with json.loads(response).
comparison: Regex-based prose parsing is fragile and a common source of silent data loss; tool_use structured output provides API-guaranteed schema-conformant JSON with no parser needed.
use_cases: LLM output parsing; eliminating regex parsers; structured data extraction from LLMs; reducing silent parse failures

tool: Closed-loop adaptive systems with two compounding loops
description: A closed-loop system has the output of a downstream stage feeding back to update the input of an upstream stage. Two compounding loops: an outer loop where JD analyses drive market frequencies driving topic suggestions driving new notes adapts to external market signal, and an inner loop where mock interview scores drive per-entry auto_confidence driving next-generation difficulty adapts to personal performance. Each cycle the system gets more calibrated to current ability against current market demand. The interface between deterministic (parsers, validators, math) and probabilistic (LLM calls) halves is a single client choke-point that audits and budgets the creative side.
comparison: Single-loop systems adapt along only one axis; two compounding loops allow simultaneous calibration to both external market signal and internal performance signal.
use_cases: adaptive learning systems; personalized study tools; feedback-driven pipeline design; systems with market and performance adaptation

tool: Chrome Manifest V3
description: MV3 replaces background pages with a service_worker entry and requires manifest_version 3. Side panel support needs side_panel in permissions plus a side_panel key pointing to the HTML file. Content scripts that need host access require host_permissions as a separate top-level array — omitting it causes silent failures where the content script loads but cannot read page DOM.
comparison: MV2 used background pages; MV3 uses service workers. host_permissions must be its own top-level key rather than nested inside the permissions array, unlike MV2 where permissions were more unified.
use_cases: Chrome extension development; Content script DOM access; Side panel extensions; MV3 migration

tool: Chrome Unpacked Extension Dev Reload
description: Enable Developer mode at chrome://extensions, then Load unpacked pointing to the project folder. Changes to content scripts and sidebar HTML/JS require clicking the reload icon. Changes to the service worker additionally require restarting it from the extension card's Service Worker link. No build step needed — browser reads files directly from disk.
comparison: Unlike traditional web development where a page refresh suffices, Chrome extensions require explicit reload of the extension card, and service worker changes need an additional restart step beyond a simple reload.
use_cases: Chrome extension development iteration; Debugging content scripts; Service worker development; Zero-build-step extension workflows

tool: Direct API Key vs Proxy Fallback Architecture
description: Marginalia supports two API paths in api.js: a direct path using the user's own Anthropic key stored in chrome.storage.local, and an optional Cloudflare Worker proxy for shared/demo use with KV-backed rate limiting. The client tries the direct key first; if absent it falls back to the proxy URL. chrome.storage.local is not encrypted and is readable by anyone with physical access to the Chrome profile.
comparison: Direct key storage avoids hard-coding keys in the extension and never transmits them except to Anthropic. The proxy fallback enables demo distribution without requiring users to supply a key. Trade-off is storage.local's lack of encryption vs convenience.
use_cases: Chrome extension API key management; Demo distribution; Rate-limited shared API access; Privacy-conscious credential storage

tool: Chrome Web Store Submission
description: Minimum Web Store requirements beyond a working MV3 extension: a PRIVACY_POLICY.md hosted at a public URL, a store listing with a 128x128 icon, at least one 1280x800 screenshot, short and long descriptions, and a single-purpose justification for each permission. The manifest description field (max 132 chars) becomes the Store subtitle verbatim.
comparison: Web Store submission has stricter requirements than simply loading an unpacked extension locally. Privacy policy is mandatory for extensions handling user data, and each permission must be individually justified.
use_cases: Publishing Chrome extensions; Web Store compliance; Extension privacy documentation; Store listing preparation

tool: Chrome Storage API
description: chrome.storage.local persists across browser restarts; chrome.storage.session lives only for the current browser session. Both use callback-style API. In MV3 service workers the Promise-based form also works, but in content scripts and sidebars the callback form is safer. storage.session is cleared on browser close but NOT on extension reload, which can leave stale data during development.
comparison: storage.local vs storage.session differ in persistence scope. Promise-based API works in service workers but callback form has broader compatibility across content scripts and sidebars. Unlike localStorage, chrome.storage APIs are async.
use_cases: Chrome extension state management; Session-scoped data storage; Persistent user preferences; MV3 service worker data access

tool: Rolling Context Window with Dual Eviction
description: RC_Context.addChunk() applies two eviction rules on every insert: chunks older than 10 minutes are dropped, and the array is capped at 10 entries with oldest removed first. Text is truncated to 4000 characters per chunk. Dual eviction is more robust than either rule alone: time-only fails for bursty reading; count-only fails for slow readers who linger on a single article for 30+ minutes.
comparison: Time-only eviction fails during bursty reading sessions. Count-only eviction fails for slow, long reading sessions. Combining both strategies handles all usage patterns more robustly.
use_cases: LLM context management; Reading assistant memory; Bounded token usage; Long-session context windowing

tool: Prototype-based JS Class Pattern inside IIFE
description: Marginalia uses the old-style constructor + prototype pattern wrapped in an IIFE to keep browser scripts framework-free and testable in Node via vm.Script. This avoids ES module syntax requiring a bundler, making window the only external dependency which is easily mocked in tests. If the IIFE is never closed due to file truncation, the vm parser throws SyntaxError at the next meaningful token after the last good line rather than at the actual cut point.
comparison: Compared to ES modules or class syntax, the IIFE+prototype pattern requires no bundler and allows easy mocking of window in Node test environments. ES modules would require a bundler or native module support, adding complexity.
use_cases: Zero-dependency browser scripting; Node-based unit testing of browser code; Framework-free extension development; vm.Script compatibility

tool: vm.createContext for Testing Browser IIFEs in Node
description: Create a sandbox where sandbox.window = sandbox and sandbox.self = sandbox, inject extra globals including Chrome API mocks and document, call vm.createContext(sandbox), then run new vm.Script(src).runInContext(sandbox). Exports like window.RC_Prompts are accessible as sandbox.RC_Prompts after execution. vm line numbers match source file actual lines only if the script was not modified before being passed to vm.Script.
comparison: Compared to jsdom or a headless browser, vm.createContext is a zero-dependency approach requiring no npm installs. It is more lightweight but less realistic than jsdom and cannot handle full DOM rendering.
use_cases: Unit testing browser IIFEs from Node; Zero-dependency test environments; Mocking Chrome APIs in tests; Testing extension logic without a browser

tool: Node Built-in Test Runner (node:test)
description: Node 18+ ships import { describe, it } from 'node:test' and import assert from 'node:assert/strict'. Run a single suite with node --test tests/foo.test.mjs or all suites with node --test tests/*.mjs. Output is TAP-formatted. Test files must be .mjs or the project must have type: module in package.json — mixing CJS require and import fails.
comparison: Compared to Jest or Mocha, node:test requires zero npm installs and no package.json. It is ideal for projects deliberately avoiding npm such as browser extensions. Trade-off is fewer features and less ecosystem support than Jest.
use_cases: Zero-dependency Node testing; Chrome extension testing; Lightweight unit test suites; Projects avoiding npm

tool: Chrome Storage API Mocking in vm Sandbox
description: Pass a plain JS object as chrome in extraGlobals. Back chrome.storage.session with a plain store object and implement get(key, cb) and set(obj, cb) as synchronous callbacks. Create a fresh mock per test suite rather than per test to prevent state bleed between suites while still isolating them from each other.
comparison: Synchronous callback mocks are simpler than async mock implementations and sufficient for testing callback-style Chrome storage API code. Per-suite rather than per-test mocks balance isolation and test setup overhead.
use_cases: Chrome extension unit testing; vm sandbox Chrome API mocking; Storage API behavior testing; Stateful mock management

tool: Visibility-aware setInterval Dwell Timer
description: Store a baseTime (when the interval started) and a baseDwell (accumulated seconds). In tick callback: elapsed = (Date.now() - baseTime) / 1000; dwell = baseDwell + elapsed. On visibilitychange to hidden, clear interval and save current dwell. On return to visible, reseed baseTime and baseDwell and restart. This avoids accumulating phantom dwell while user is in another tab. Pattern applies to any elapsed-time UI where accuracy depends on tab-focus state.
comparison: A naive setInterval without visibility awareness accumulates time even when the tab is hidden, producing inflated dwell metrics. The visibility-aware pattern gives accurate real engagement time.
use_cases: Reading engagement tracking; Session length measurement; Tab-aware countdown timers; Accurate dwell time analytics

tool: Marker-based Response Parsing with Sentence-boundary Fallback
description: Primary parse path uses case-insensitive regex to find [OBSERVATION], [INSIGHT], [QUESTION] markers in order and slices the string between them. Fallback for conversational replies splits on sentence boundaries: 1 sentence = observation only; 2 = obs + question; 3 = obs/insight/question; 4+ = obs/middle joined/last question. Markers must appear in order for the primary path to activate — out-of-order markers trigger the fallback.
comparison: Marker-based parsing is precise but brittle if the LLM produces out-of-order or missing markers. The sentence-boundary fallback degrades gracefully without failing the UI, trading precision for robustness.
use_cases: LLM structured response parsing; Graceful degradation in AI responses; Chat UI response formatting; Conversational AI output handling

tool: 3x3 Persona Matrix with Orthogonal Axes
description: Two independent choices — extraction strategy (readability/viewport/structured) x behavior strategy (mitigate/embrace/erase) — combine multiplicatively to produce 9 distinct system prompts. Extraction controls what content the model sees; behavior controls how it interprets and presents engagement signals. Orthogonality ensures each of the 9 cells has a unique combination of data and tone. If axes were correlated, fewer than 9 meaningfully different prompts would result.
comparison: Correlated axes produce redundant prompt combinations and effectively fewer than 9 distinct personas. Orthogonal axes maximize the distinctiveness of each combination, making the matrix design principle essential for meaningful variety.
use_cases: LLM prompt engineering; Persona-based AI assistants; Multi-dimensional system prompt design; Content-type adaptive AI responses

tool: Prompt Quality Guards: Insight Restatement + Question Tension Rules
description: Two explicit guards woven into all 9 system prompts. Insight guard: do not quote or paraphrase the article directly — draw an inference the article itself does not make. Question rule: the question must not have a correct answer — it should create productive tension. Both stated as prohibitions. Negative constraints outperform positive instructions for preventing specific failure modes because they close a door the model might otherwise push open.
comparison: Negative constraints ('do not X') are more reliable than positive instructions ('do Y instead') for constraining LLM output to prevent specific failure modes. Positive instructions leave room for the model to interpret broadly.
use_cases: LLM output quality control; Preventing LLM response repetition; Generating open-ended questions; System prompt engineering for AI assistants

tool: Turn-aware Prompting with Follow-up Suffix
description: On turns 2+ the system prompt is appended with FOLLOWUP_SUFFIX instructing the model not to repeat or re-summarise the article and to respond directly to the user's message. The suffix is appended rather than replacing the system prompt so persona and quality guards remain active. The suffix is only applied when _apiHistory.length > 1, allowing the first turn to follow the full structured format while follow-ups shift to natural conversation.
comparison: Without the follow-up suffix, the model re-anchors to the article on every turn producing repetitive openers. Appending rather than replacing the system prompt preserves all other prompt engineering while adding turn-specific behavior.
use_cases: Multi-turn LLM conversation design; Preventing repetitive AI responses; Context-aware system prompt engineering; Conversational AI persona maintenance

tool: Article-type Injection via URL/Title/Text Heuristics
description: _detectContentType(chunk) returns one of 8 labels (academic, reference, saved, opinion, explainer, research, news, article) based on URL pattern matching, title keyword matching, and short text excerpt scan in that priority order. The label is injected as a Content-type tag in the user message so the model can calibrate framing without needing a separate system prompt per content type.
comparison: Injecting content type in the user message rather than the system prompt keeps the 9-prompt matrix intact while still giving the model the framing signal. Adding it to the system prompt would require expanding the matrix or maintaining separate prompt sets.
use_cases: Adaptive LLM framing by content type; URL-based content classification; Dynamic user message context injection; Multi-type reading assistant

tool: Direction-aware Dwell Timer UX
description: In skim mode the timer counts down from the 30s neutral threshold showing time until reclassification. In all other modes it counts up showing elapsed engagement time. Countdown is computed as remaining = Math.max(0, SKIM_THRESHOLD_S - dwell); once it hits 0 it switches to counting up from the threshold. This surfaces classification logic without exposing raw numbers or explainer text.
comparison: A uniform count-up timer would not give skim-mode users actionable information. The countdown direction change communicates the classification threshold implicitly through UX behavior rather than explicit UI labels.
use_cases: Reading engagement UX; Implicit classification feedback; Dwell-based behavior signaling; Timer direction as information design

tool: Passive Local Feedback Telemetry without a Backend
description: Two feedback signals accumulate in chrome.storage.local and are never sent anywhere. rcHighlightStats counts stars per section revealing which part users find most valuable. rcSessionStats records persona, sessions, totalTurns, maxTurns per persona — mean turn depth (totalTurns/sessions) is the clearest signal for comparing persona engagement. Both are inspectable via chrome.storage.local.get. Zero privacy risk and zero backend cost.
comparison: Compared to server-side telemetry, local-only storage eliminates privacy risk and backend infrastructure cost. Trade-off is that data is not aggregatable across users and is only available to the developer via manual inspection on their own device.
use_cases: Privacy-preserving usage analytics; Extension engagement measurement; Persona performance comparison; Zero-backend telemetry

tool: Write Tool Silent Truncation on Multi-byte UTF-8 Characters
description: The Write tool can silently truncate a file mid-line when encountering multi-byte UTF-8 characters such as em dash or almost-equal sign in certain positions. The tool reports no error and the file appears to write but output is cut short at or just after the problematic character. The fix is to write through bash (fs.writeFileSync on the Linux mount path). The Edit tool can also report success while the change never lands due to Windows/Linux mount sync lag. Always verify with wc -l in bash rather than re-reading via the Read tool which may return a stale cache.
comparison: The Write/Edit/Read file tools operate through a layer that may cache or mishandle multi-byte characters, while bash operates directly on the mount path with correct UTF-8 handling. Bash is the authoritative source of truth for file state.
use_cases: Agentic file writing workflows; Debugging silent write failures; UTF-8 safe file operations; Cross-environment file verification

tool: Windows vs Linux Mount Path Mismatch in Agentic Environments
description: File tools (Read/Write/Edit) operate on Windows paths while bash commands operate on Linux mount paths. Both refer to the same underlying files via a mount, but changes made via Edit may not be visible immediately in bash due to sync latency. When bash and file tools disagree about file content, bash is right — it reads directly from the mount without going through any caching layer. Always use bash cat or wc -l as the authoritative check after any write.
comparison: File tools use Windows paths and go through a caching layer that may lag behind actual file state. Bash uses Linux mount paths and reads directly from the underlying filesystem, making it the authoritative verification method.
use_cases: Agentic coding environments; Cross-OS file operation debugging; Write verification in WSL-like setups; Diagnosing file sync latency

tool: Binary-search Diagnosis of vm SyntaxError
description: When vm.Script throws a generic SyntaxError without a useful line number, binary-search the source: slice to lines.slice(0, mid), append a synthetic closing token (})(), and catch. Errors indicating normal incompleteness (Unexpected end, Expected, missing) mean the cut is too early; 'Invalid or unexpected token' means the bad content is in the current half. Most reliable when root cause is file truncation (an unclosed IIFE) rather than a malformed expression, as appending })() can produce misleading errors if the partial source ends mid-string or mid-regex.
comparison: Compared to reading hundreds of lines manually, binary search narrows the root cause to a specific line efficiently. The technique is less reliable for malformed expressions than for truncation because the synthetic closing token interacts unpredictably with partial strings or regexes.
use_cases: Debugging vm.Script SyntaxErrors; Diagnosing truncated browser IIFE files; Narrowing large file syntax errors; Node vm debugging workflows

tool: Lazy Anthropic client with @lru_cache
description: Replacing eager client = Anthropic() at module top-level with a get_client() function decorated with @lru_cache(maxsize=1) defers construction until first use. Modules import without an API key, unit tests don't hit the network at import time, and httpx never tries SOCKS proxy setup in sandboxed CI runners.
comparison: Eager instantiation vs lazy cached instantiation. Eager fails in offline/test environments; lazy is identical in behavior once warmed up.
use_cases: Unit testing without API keys; CI runners without network access; Sandboxed environments lacking socksio

tool: Universal retry wrapper (call_with_retry)
description: Hoisting exponential-backoff retry into one function that every caller routes through means transient 5xx/529s only need to be handled correctly once. Also enables layering telemetry, caching, and cost ledger at the same choke point.
comparison: Per-module retry duplicates logic and drifts; a shared wrapper compounds benefits with every new pipeline function.
use_cases: Resilient LLM API calls; Cost telemetry aggregation; LRU response caching

tool: Centralized config with env-overridable constants
description: Centralizing volatile constants (model name, pricing, token caps, paths) in one config module means a model upgrade or budget tweak is a one-line change. The Sonnet 4.5 to 4.6 migration was a single-file edit because of this decision.
comparison: Grep-and-replace across codebase vs single-line config change.
use_cases: Model version upgrades; Pricing updates; Token cap adjustments; Path migrations

tool: Three-way JD x Resume x KB bucketing
description: Single-axis comparisons miss the most actionable signal. Adding a third axis yields four semantically meaningful buckets: lead-with (all three intersect), exposure (resume + JD without KB), hidden-asset (KB + JD without resume), and true-gap (JD only). The exposure category predicts which interview questions you'll fumble.
comparison: Two-axis (JD vs KB) produces one gap list; three-axis produces four actionable buckets with distinct recommended actions.
use_cases: Interview preparation; Resume gap analysis; Study prioritization; Career signal analysis

tool: Backward-compatible API extension via optional kwargs
description: Adding a new parameter as an optional kwarg with None default and branching on its presence keeps original behavior when absent. run_gap_analysis(jd, kb) extended to run_gap_analysis(jd, kb, resume_claims=None) without touching existing call sites or tests.
comparison: None default communicates feature opt-in rather than empty input (False or []).
use_cases: Adding features to existing functions; Refactoring without breaking callers; Incremental API evolution

tool: Prompt-tuning for downstream parseability
description: Constraining the LLM output format in the prompt (e.g., requiring - [ ] markdown checkboxes) makes the parser a regex one-liner instead of a fragile markdown parser. Format constraints and verbosity allowances must be balanced.
comparison: Complex post-hoc parsing vs prompt-constrained trivial parsing.
use_cases: Weekly plan generation; Actionable task extraction; Any structured LLM output that feeds downstream code

tool: Per-call-site model tier routing
description: Routing specific call sites to a cheaper/faster tier (Haiku) while leaving high-reasoning sites on the flagship (Sonnet) via a model= parameter. Structured extraction is safe on Haiku; generative writing, grading, and multi-step reasoning are not.
comparison: Single model everywhere vs tiered routing. Wrong tier on high-reasoning sites silently degrades quality.
use_cases: Cost optimization; Resume parsing; Schema-conforming JSON extraction; Grading and scenario generation

tool: Reader-bridge pattern for URL ingestion (Jina Reader)
description: Services like Jina Reader take any public URL, strip boilerplate, and return clean markdown ready for an LLM. One stdlib urllib.request.urlopen call, no new dependency. Make the endpoint env-overridable to swap to self-hosted alternatives.
comparison: Custom HTML scraper with JS handling vs single API call to reader service.
use_cases: LLM-ready web content ingestion; Research note capture; Article summarization

tool: AnkiConnect JSON-RPC with graceful degradation
description: AnkiConnect exposes a JSON-RPC API at localhost:8765. Always design as best-effort: probe is_available() once per session, push when available, fall through to CSV export when not. Cache the availability probe in session state.
comparison: Hard dependency on Anki vs graceful fallback to CSV. User without Anki gets same experience as before.
use_cases: Flashcard creation from study notes; Spaced repetition integration; Offline-first study tools

tool: Heuristic file-extension detection for code blobs
description: Detecting language from code content by scanning first ~300 chars for distinctive tokens. Terraform: resource/provider/variable. YAML: apiVersion/kind. Python: def/import. JSON: leading { or [. Fallback: .txt. Rank checks by specificity.
comparison: Strict language detection vs good-enough heuristic. Downloads open in correct editor mode ~90% of the time.
use_cases: Code download buttons; Editor mode detection; File type classification

tool: Cross-modal session-state hand-off in Streamlit
description: Modals communicate through st.session_state. Pattern: write seed value to target key, set active_modal, call st.rerun(). Receiving modal renders pre-populated. Compose features by chaining modals through state rather than cramming everything into one screen.
comparison: Direct modal communication (impossible in Streamlit) vs state-mediated hand-off.
use_cases: Resume check to convert modal hand-off; Multi-step workflows; Context-preserving navigation

tool: Cache key hygiene for LLM response caching
description: Hash everything that changes the response: model name, max_tokens, full prompt text. Hash nothing else. The cache key should answer whether this exact request would produce the same API response. Per-user state like temperature or system prompt variants must go in the key.
comparison: Including prompt template files over-invalidates (every edit nukes all entries). Including only prompt text provides natural invalidation.
use_cases: LLM call deduplication; Cost reduction on repeated queries; Debug-friendly cache invalidation

tool: Optional artifact carve-outs (right-sized feature scope)
description: When a feature request implies a major new artifact, enumerate use cases and ask which truly require the new artifact vs which can be handled by existing primitives. Most value slots into existing primitives. Build only the use case that requires the new slot.
comparison: Building the full artifact with all enabled use cases vs carving out only the one that genuinely needs a new stored object.
use_cases: System design scoping; Feature prioritization; Architecture decisions

tool: Documentation tiering (OVERVIEW.md vs README.md)
description: Two distinct docs for distinct audiences: friendly OVERVIEW.md (90-second tour) and README.md (technical reference). Front door points at reference for depth-seekers; reference assumes the visitor has already decided to use the project.
comparison: Single monolithic README vs tiered docs. Single doc either overwhelms new visitors or under-serves developers.
use_cases: Open source project documentation; Internal tool onboarding; Developer reference

tool: Centralized config as upgrade insurance
description: Centralizing volatile constants costs an afternoon of refactoring once. The payoff is every subsequent change being one line. Sonnet 4.5 to 4.6 migration was a single-file edit. Identify constants most likely to change in the next year and centralize those first.
comparison: Scattered constants requiring grep-and-replace vs centralized single-line changes.
use_cases: Model version management; Pricing updates; Path and environment configuration

tool: Three-tier substring matching with minimum token length
description: A single-tier substring check fails on cases like AWS Lambda vs Lambda cold start. Fix is a tiered cascade: (1) substring either direction, (2) token-level overlap for tokens >= 4 chars. The 4-char threshold excludes high-noise tokens like AWS, CI, DB that would false-match.
comparison: Single-tier substring vs cascaded matching. Without min-token-length filter, common short tokens become match magnets.
use_cases: Skill matching across job descriptions; Resume to KB coverage checks; Fuzzy concept deduplication

tool: Closed-loop gap-resolution UX
description: When showing a user a problem or gap, the highest-impact UI offers a one-click action to start resolving it without losing context. Listing missing resume claims is informational; pre-filling a Convert form for one of them is behavior-changing. Friction between seeing a problem and acting on it is where most users drop off.
comparison: Informational display vs actionable display with one-click resolution.
use_cases: Gap analysis results; Error correction flows; Any problem-display UI

tool: LRU eviction via Python insertion-ordered dicts
description: Since Python 3.7 dicts preserve insertion order. On read, pop and reinsert to mark most recently used. On overflow, pop(next(iter(d))) removes the oldest entry. No collections.OrderedDict needed. Pattern is two helpers (touch_on_read, evict_when_full) plus a cap constant.
comparison: collections.OrderedDict vs plain dict with insertion-order semantics. Plain dict is sufficient since Python 3.7.
use_cases: LLM response caching; Any bounded LRU cache; Session state management

tool: Best-effort I/O for telemetry and cache layers
description: Telemetry, caching, and side-channel I/O should never fail the real operation they support. Wrap reads/writes in try/except: pass. The same rule does NOT apply to business logic. Best-effort applies to the side channel, not the main channel.
comparison: Propagating side-channel errors vs swallowing them. Swallowing in the main API path masks real failures.
use_cases: Usage logging; Cache writes; Session snapshots; Telemetry collection

tool: Docker build as CI regression-catcher
description: Running Python tests in CI doesn't catch Dockerfile regressions. One additional docker build step in GitHub Actions catches all such failures with no registry push, no image tagging, no extra setup. Five lines of YAML, zero ongoing cost.
comparison: pytest-only CI vs pytest + docker build CI. Docker build catches Dockerfile regressions invisible to pytest.
use_cases: CI pipeline hardening; Dockerfile regression detection; Container-based deployments

tool: Wave 1 vs Wave 2 feature splitting
description: When a backlog mixes clean features with features that have unresolved design conflicts, split into waves. Wave 1: build only items with no design questions. Wave 2: schedule design conversations first, then code. Shipping Wave 1 isolates design discussions from urgency.
comparison: Building all features simultaneously vs staged delivery. Deciding while building reshapes APIs mid-implementation.
use_cases: Sprint planning; Backlog grooming; Architecture-first development

tool: Auto-discovery folders as extension points
description: Instead of registering new items in a config file, glob a designated folder and render whatever is there.
