The reminder_prompts pattern: drop a .md or .txt into the folder and it shows up as a new tab on next render. Zero code change to extend. Great for trusted local extensions, dangerous for untrusted user uploads.
comparison: Config-file registration vs filesystem glob discovery. Config requires code change; glob requires only file drop.
use_cases: Sidebar prompt tabs; Plugin systems; Dynamic menu generation

tool: Single-file CLI subcommand pattern with argparse
description: argparse add_subparsers() with per-subcommand set_defaults(func=cmd_fn) gives a clean dispatcher in main() via args.func(args). New subcommands require only a cmd_xxx function and a registration line. Defer expensive imports to command body, not module load, to keep CLI startup fast.
comparison: Monolithic argument parsing vs subcommand dispatch. Subcommand pattern scales cleanly with new commands.
use_cases: Developer CLI tools; Pipeline management commands; Cache and data management scripts

tool: Hysteresis-aware confidence tier mapping
description: Hysteresis prevents oscillation at threshold boundaries by using different up-thresholds and down-thresholds. For Low/Medium/High with break-points at 6 and 8, climbing requires threshold + 0.5 buffer and dropping requires threshold - 0.5 buffer. Without hysteresis, a metric averaging exactly 8.0 flips between tiers on every recalculation.
comparison: Simple threshold crossing vs hysteresis-buffered threshold crossing. Simple thresholds cause flapping at boundaries.
use_cases: Confidence tier assignment; Adaptive difficulty systems; Any continuous-to-discrete mapping

tool: Variable shadowing inside a loop body (Python scope bug)
description: Inside for entry in kb, writing history = entry.get(...) rebinds the local name history for every subsequent iteration, clobbering the outer history used by recent_scores. The first iteration works correctly; the bug fires on iteration 2+ making it look stochastic. Rename to entry_history to avoid collision.
comparison: Reusing outer variable name vs non-colliding local name. Python for-loop bodies do not create a new scope.
use_cases: Code review; Debugging stochastic-looking bugs; Python scope education

tool: Module-import-time binding of config values
description: from config import DATA_DIR captures the value at import time, not a reference to the attribute. Later monkeypatching config.DATA_DIR does NOT update the importing module's bound copy. Must monkeypatch.setattr per module. Derived constants computed at import time also need separate rebinding in tests.
comparison: Module-level import binding vs lazy per-function import. Module-level is faster but harder to monkeypatch in tests.
use_cases: Test configuration; CI environment overrides; Path configuration management

tool: SHA-256-keyed LLM response cache
description: A single on-disk cache keyed on hash(model + max_tokens + prompt) automatically deduplicates re-runs of identical API calls. Prompt text in the key provides natural invalidation on edits. LRU eviction via insertion-ordered dicts. Add MINDCI_CACHE_DISABLE=1 env var for forcing fresh runs.
comparison: Hashing prompt template files over-invalidates (every edit nukes all entries). Prompt text in key is the correct design.
use_cases: LLM call cost reduction; Development iteration speed; Reproducible pipeline runs

tool: Streamlit session state vs durable disk snapshot
description: st.session_state dies on browser refresh or app restart. For multi-step flows, snapshot relevant keys to a JSON file on every state mutation, restore at renderer top if no in-memory session exists, delete on completion. Wrap reads/writes in try/except so persistence failures never block the UI.
comparison: In-memory only state vs disk-backed state. In-memory state is lost on refresh; disk-backed state survives.
use_cases: Mock interview sessions; Multi-step grading flows; Refresh-survivable UI state

tool: Debounced filesystem watcher pattern
description: Editor atomic save sequences fire 2-5 watchdog events in rapid succession. Timer-based debounce: on each event cancel any pending timer and start a new one for N seconds; callback runs only when timer fires. ~2.5s is a sensible debounce window. Do not run callback synchronously inside the event handler or the watchdog thread blocks and misses subsequent events.
comparison: Immediate callback on each event vs debounced callback. Immediate causes multiple pipeline runs per save.
use_cases: File watch pipelines; Live reload systems; Editor integration

tool: Pydantic forward-compatible schema evolution
description: Adding new fields as Optional[T] = None lets existing JSON deserialize without modification. Cannot change field semantics, only add new ones. For production, add a version field and explicit migrations. Prefer entry.get('field') or default over assuming the key exists when loader can return either old or new shape.
comparison: Required new fields break existing data; Optional fields allow gradual migration.
use_cases: KB entry schema evolution; Config file versioning; API response schema migration

tool: Cassette-style integration tests with monkeypatched API
description: For LLM-driven pipelines, integration means exercising cross-module wiring with the network stubbed at one point. Replace the API choke-point with a canned-response generator. Assertions cover whether the right files were written, the right entries were updated, and the right calls were made in the right order.
comparison: Real API calls vs stubbed API with filesystem assertions. Real calls are slow, costly, and non-deterministic.
use_cases: Pipeline integration testing; CI without API keys; Cross-module wiring validation

tool: Cost telemetry as middleware
description: Wrapping API calls to log response.usage.input_tokens and output_tokens to a daily JSON row gives free spend visibility. Pricing constants are env-overridable. get_usage_summary(days) rolls today plus last-N-days totals. Wrap logging in try/except so telemetry failures never abort a real call.
comparison: No telemetry vs lightweight middleware telemetry. Middleware approach adds zero latency to the critical path.
use_cases: LLM spend tracking; Dashboard cost display; Budget alerting

tool: Closed-loop adaptive systems with two compounding loops
description: A closed-loop system has downstream output feeding back to update upstream input. Two loops: outer (JD analyses to market frequencies to topic suggestions to new notes) adapts to external market signal; inner (mock interview scores to auto_confidence to next difficulty) adapts to personal performance. Each cycle the system gets more calibrated.
comparison: Static study system vs adaptive closed-loop system. Static systems do not improve targeting over time.
use_cases: Personalized learning systems; Adaptive difficulty tuning; Market-signal-driven study planning

error: AD Lambda architectural decisions required justification and documentation
root_cause: Complex system with many interdependent decisions across AWS services, security, cost, and reliability domains lacking a single reference
fix: Documented 20+ architectural decisions with rationale, trade-offs, and gotchas covering Bedrock API usage, Lambda patterns, networking, security, and testing
concept: Direct boto3 Bedrock calls over LangChain, decoupled async notify Lambda, mock LDAP via env flag, dual confidence thresholds (0.8 onboard / 0.95 offboard), SSM TTL cache, Simple AD over Managed Microsoft AD, SQS DLQ on async Lambda, CloudWatch alarm on DLQ, API Gateway throttling, resource-scoped IAM, exponential backoff on LDAP with no retry on auth errors, VPC isolation with interface endpoints, Secrets Manager over env vars, DynamoDB TTL on audit records, Lambda REQUEST authorizer, S3 remote Terraform state, conn.response over conn.entries for ldap3, moto-backed integration tests with module-level client rewiring

tool: Direct Bedrock API calls over LangChain
description: Three Claude calls (NL parsing, group assignment with confidence scoring, notification generation) are discrete, well-scoped prompts with structured JSON outputs. LangChain adds abstraction without value, makes debugging harder, and introduces an extra dependency. Direct boto3 calls give full visibility into every prompt and response.
comparison: LangChain abstraction vs direct boto3. LangChain is valuable for complex chains; unnecessary for discrete well-scoped prompts.
use_cases: AD provisioning Lambda; Any LLM integration with structured JSON output; Systems making real directory or infrastructure changes

tool: Decoupled async Lambda invocation for SNS notifications
description: SNS notification handled by a dedicated Lambda invoked with InvocationType=Event so a transient SNS failure never blocks, retries, or extends the main onboarding response. Each function has a single testable responsibility. Without a DLQ, failures are silent.
comparison: Synchronous notification in main Lambda vs async decoupled notification Lambda. Synchronous couples failure modes.
use_cases: Async notification delivery; Decoupled side-effect processing; Reliability isolation

tool: Mock LDAP layer via USE_MOCK_LDAP env flag
description: A USE_MOCK_LDAP=true environment variable bypasses all real LDAP connections and logs what would have been provisioned to CloudWatch. Enables full Claude pipeline in CI and demos without a live directory. Returns deterministic responses so handler logic is fully testable. Controlled via Terraform variable.
comparison: Live LDAP in all environments vs mock flag. Live LDAP makes CI expensive, slow, and environment-dependent.
use_cases: CI pipeline testing; Demo environments; Local development

tool: Separate SSM parameters for onboard vs offboard confidence thresholds
description: Two independent SSM parameters (/ad-lambda/confidence-threshold at 0.8 and /ad-lambda/offboard-confidence-threshold at 0.95) allow independent tuning without redeployment. The bar for irreversible destructive actions (disabling accounts) is categorically different from reversible provisioning actions. Each has its own 5-minute TTL cache.
comparison: Shared threshold vs independent thresholds. Shared threshold cannot differentiate risk levels between reversible and irreversible actions.
use_cases: Human-in-the-loop AI systems; Risk-tiered automation; Ops-tunable confidence gates

tool: SSM Parameter Store with module-level TTL cache in Lambda
description: Reading SSM on every invocation adds latency and cost. A module-level dict caches values with a loaded_at timestamp; if time elapsed exceeds 300 seconds, the next invocation fetches fresh. Config changes propagate within 5 minutes without redeployment. Tests must manually reset the cache dict between cases.
comparison: Per-invocation SSM read vs TTL-cached module-level read. Per-invocation adds ~50-100ms latency and SSM API costs.
use_cases: Lambda configuration management; Feature flags without redeployment; Ops-tunable thresholds

tool: AWS Simple AD over Managed Microsoft AD
description: Simple AD (Samba 4, ~$36-40/month Small) chosen over Managed Microsoft AD Standard (~$87-140/month). Simple AD lacks trust relationships, MFA integration, and PowerShell AD module support but all three required LDAP operations (user create, group membership modify, account disable via userAccountControl) work identically.
comparison: Simple AD at ~$40/month vs Managed Microsoft AD at ~$87-140/month. Switch only if trust relationships or MFA are needed.
use_cases: LDAP-based provisioning; Cost-optimized directory services; Portfolios and demos

tool: SQS dead-letter queue on async Lambda
description: InvocationType=Event means Lambda retries a failed invocation twice then silently discards it. A dead_letter_config pointing to SQS captures the payload after retries so no notification is permanently lost. Requires sqs:SendMessage IAM permission and SQS VPC interface endpoint for VPC-resident Lambdas.
comparison: No DLQ vs SQS DLQ. Without DLQ, silent failures are undetectable and payloads are permanently lost.
use_cases: Async Lambda reliability; Notification delivery guarantees; Audit trails for failed events

tool: CloudWatch alarm on DLQ ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible
description: A DLQ with no alarm is only useful if someone checks it manually. CloudWatch alarm with threshold=0, GreaterThanThreshold, period=60, evaluation_periods=1 fires within 60 seconds of first failure. treat_missing_data=notBreaching keeps alarm green when queue is empty. Both alarm and OK transitions notify existing IT SNS topic.
comparison: Manual DLQ inspection vs automated alarm. Manual inspection means failures go undetected until someone looks.
use_cases: DLQ monitoring; Failure detection SLAs; On-call alerting

tool: API Gateway HTTP API throttling
description: default_route_settings on the $default stage sets burst limit of 10 req/s and sustained rate of 5 req/s. Protects downstream Bedrock and LDAP from runaway callers even with a valid API key. Throttling applies after authorizer validates the key. Exceeding the limit returns 429 before any Lambda is invoked.
comparison: No throttling vs gateway-level throttling. Without throttling, a runaway client can exhaust Bedrock quotas and LDAP connection pools.
use_cases: API abuse prevention; Downstream service protection; Cost control

tool: Resource-ARN-scoped IAM policies
description: Every IAM statement uses resource-specific ARNs: Secrets Manager, DynamoDB, SNS, SSM, Lambda, and Bedrock all scoped to exact ARNs. No wildcards remain. Bedrock foundation model ARNs have a double colon (no account ID segment): arn:aws:bedrock:region::foundation-model/id.
comparison: Wildcard IAM policies vs resource-specific ARNs. Wildcards violate least-privilege and expand blast radius.
use_cases: Production AWS deployments; Security compliance; Least-privilege enforcement

tool: Exponential backoff retry on LDAP with auth-error exemption
description: LDAP connections retry up to 3 times with delays of 1s, 2s, 4s. Authentication errors (invalid credentials, unwillingtoperform) are not retried because a wrong password will not fix itself and retrying risks account lockout. Retry settings are env-overridable.
comparison: Retry all errors vs retry only transient errors. Retrying auth errors risks lockout and wastes time.
use_cases: LDAP provisioning reliability; Directory service integration; Any integration where auth errors must not be retried

tool: VPC isolation with interface endpoints for all AWS service calls
description: All Lambda functions run inside a private VPC with no internet gateway. Every AWS service call goes through VPC interface endpoints and never leaves the AWS backbone. LDAP traffic is entirely private. Trade-off is approximately $7-10/month per interface endpoint.
comparison: Public endpoints vs VPC interface endpoints. Public endpoints expose traffic to the internet; interface endpoints keep all traffic on AWS backbone.
use_cases: Security-critical AWS deployments; Compliance requirements; Financial and directory service workloads

tool: Secrets Manager over Lambda environment variables for credentials
description: LDAP and Azure credentials fetched at invocation time from Secrets Manager rather than injected as Lambda environment variables. Environment variables are visible in Lambda console and Terraform state; Secrets Manager values are not. Lambda role scoped to specific secret ARNs.
comparison: Environment variable injection vs Secrets Manager. Env vars are visible in console and state files; Secrets Manager values are encrypted and access-controlled.
use_cases: Credential management; Security compliance; Any Lambda handling passwords or API keys

tool: DynamoDB TTL on audit records
description: Every audit record includes a ttl attribute set to time.time() + 90 days. DynamoDB TTL automatically deletes expired items without consuming write capacity. Table uses PAY_PER_REQUEST billing so there is no capacity to provision or over-provision.
comparison: Manual cleanup vs TTL-based automatic expiry. Manual cleanup requires cron jobs and operational overhead.
use_cases: Audit log management; Cost control for time-bounded data; Compliance with data retention policies

tool: Lambda REQUEST authorizer for API key validation
description: A dedicated authorizer_function Lambda validates the x-api-key header on every request before API Gateway routes it. Key injected as env var via Terraform sensitive variable and GitHub Actions secret. The authorizer itself does not need VPC access; it only reads its own env var.
comparison: API Gateway built-in API keys vs custom Lambda authorizer. Custom authorizer allows arbitrary validation logic and key storage.
use_cases: API authentication; Request gating; Multi-tenant key validation

tool: S3 remote state for Terraform CI/CD
description: Terraform state stored in S3 via -backend-config at terraform init so state is shared between plan (PR) and apply (merge to main) jobs. Without remote state, each CI runner starts with empty local state and apply would try to recreate all resources. S3 bucket must exist before first terraform init.
comparison: Local state vs remote S3 state. Local state breaks multi-runner CI; remote state enables plan/apply separation.
use_cases: Terraform CI/CD pipelines; Multi-developer Terraform workflows; Plan/apply separation

tool: conn.response over conn.entries for ldap3 group search
description: After conn.search(), conn.entries returns ldap3 Entry objects not subscriptable with bracket notation. conn.response returns raw dicts where e[attributes][cn] works as expected. Response list also includes referral and result-done entries so filter to e.get(type) == searchResEntry before extracting values. Bug is invisible in unit tests that mock the connection.
comparison: conn.entries ORM-style access vs conn.response raw dict access. entries appear to work in mocks but fail against live directory.
use_cases: LDAP group membership queries; Directory service integration; ldap3 library usage

tool: Cloudflare KV TTL
description: Cloudflare KV's native TTL is a fixed expiry set at write time — it does not reset when the key is read or overwritten unless you explicitly pass a new TTL. To implement sliding expiry, you must store a lastActivity timestamp in the value and rewrite the key with a fresh TTL on every access.
comparison: Sliding expiry vs fixed expiry: fixed TTL is set once at write time; sliding requires explicit rewrite with new expirationTtl on every access.
use_cases: Session expiry after inactivity; Token or trial expiry windows; Any KV-backed feature requiring sliding expiration

tool: Three-namespace KV pattern for entitlements
description: Separating entitlement data into three distinct KV bindings (FREE_TRIAL, TOKEN_BALANCE, UNLIMITED) rather than one namespace makes access patterns self-documenting, allows independent retention policies per type, and prevents key collisions.
comparison: Three-namespace pattern vs single-namespace with prefixes: prefixes create implicit coupling; separate bindings are independently configurable.
use_cases: SaaS entitlement modeling; Multi-tier access control; Independent KV retention policies per feature

tool: Chrome Web Store Payments API
description: Google discontinued the Chrome Web Store Payments API. Extensions can no longer use the built-in google.payments.inapp.buy() flow. Any monetised extension must use a third-party provider and handle purchase verification independently.
comparison: CWS Payments (deprecated) vs third-party providers (Stripe, Gumroad, Lemon Squeezy): CWS API is dead; third-party requires independent verification.
use_cases: Chrome extension monetisation; In-extension purchase flows

tool: chrome.runtime.id
description: chrome.runtime.id is fixed for a given extension published on the Chrome Web Store — it matches the CWS listing ID and does not change across installs or updates. It cannot be spoofed by end users. The main abuse vector it does not cover is developers side-loading a modified copy with a different ID.
comparison: HMAC-signing the extension ID adds complexity without meaningfully blocking realistic threats for a Web Store extension.
use_cases: Extension identity verification; Abuse prevention; Entitlement keying

tool: chrome.identity.launchWebAuthFlow
description: launchWebAuthFlow opens a controlled popup and resolves when the page redirects to chrome.identity.getRedirectURL(). It was designed for OAuth flows, not payments. Payment providers do not redirect to that URL by default.
comparison: launchWebAuthFlow (OAuth-designed) vs chrome.tabs.create + polling: tabs.create is the correct pattern for payment flows.
use_cases: OAuth flows in Chrome extensions; Not suitable for payment checkouts

tool: HMAC-SHA256 with Web Crypto API (SubtleCrypto)
description: Cloudflare Workers use the Web Crypto API, not Node's crypto module. To compute HMAC-SHA256: import the secret key with crypto.subtle.importKey, then call crypto.subtle.sign. The result is an ArrayBuffer that must be hex-encoded for string comparison. Use timing-safe comparison to prevent timing attacks.
comparison: Web Crypto API (Cloudflare Workers) vs Node crypto module: Web Crypto is async and uses ArrayBuffers; Node crypto is synchronous with Buffer.
use_cases: Webhook signature verification; HMAC-based authentication in Cloudflare Workers

tool: Timing-safe string comparison
description: Regular string comparison short-circuits on the first mismatched character, leaking information via timing. Timing-safe comparison XORs all corresponding byte values and checks the result is zero — it always takes the same time regardless of where strings diverge.
comparison: === comparison (unsafe, short-circuits) vs XOR accumulation (safe, constant time).
use_cases: HMAC signature verification; Any secret comparison in security-sensitive contexts

tool: Optimistic deduction + correction pattern for token billing
description: Deduct a flat estimate upfront to block concurrent over-spending. Once the response returns with actual usage, perform a correction write: correctedBalance = currentBalance + flatDeduction - actualTokens. This refunds over-estimates and charges under-estimates without holding a lock across the API call.
comparison: Optimistic deduction vs locked deduction: locked deduction holds state across async API call; optimistic deduction avoids locking with a correction step.
use_cases: Token-based billing for proxied LLM APIs; Pay-per-use billing with unknown upfront costs

tool: Fail-open vs fail-closed for infrastructure failures
description: When KV is unavailable mid-request, fail open (allow the request, risk brief untracked access) or fail closed (return error, risk blocking paying users). Subscription and trial products generally prefer fail-open.
comparison: Fail-open (availability-first) vs fail-closed (security-first): fail-open preferred for subscription products to avoid blocking customers during outages.
use_cases: KV-backed entitlement checks; Any dependency failure handling in paid services

tool: Server-side-only KV writes for billing security
description: Removing client-callable deduction endpoints and making all KV writes happen in the proxy worker means the trusted server controls all balance state. The extension only reads what the server returns via response headers — it has no write authority over billing data.
comparison: Client-callable deduction (spoofable) vs server-side-only deduction (trusted): client calls can be forged; server-side writes are authoritative.
use_cases: Token billing for browser extensions; Any client-server billing architecture where spoofing is a risk

tool: Gumroad webhook authentication
description: Gumroad signs webhook POST bodies with HMAC-SHA256 using a seller-specific secret delivered in the X-Gumroad-Signature header. The body is application/x-www-form-urlencoded, not JSON. Parse with new URLSearchParams(body).
comparison: Gumroad webhook (URL-encoded) vs typical JSON webhooks: must use URLSearchParams not JSON.parse.
use_cases: Gumroad purchase verification; Extension license activation on purchase

tool: Payment callback polling pattern in Chrome extensions
description: Chrome extensions cannot intercept redirects from external payment pages. The standard pattern is: open the payment URL in a new tab, then poll a lightweight /check-purchase endpoint from background.js at a fixed interval. Poll every 3–5 seconds, time out after 10 minutes, stop if user closes the tab.
comparison: Redirect interception (not possible in extensions) vs polling pattern (standard workaround).
use_cases: Stripe/Gumroad checkout in Chrome extensions; Any external payment flow from an extension

tool: Activity-based vs firstSeen-based trial reset rules
description: Resetting a trial based on firstSeen punishes users who signed up and forgot. Resetting based on lastActivity targets genuinely lapsed users. Active users, including exhausted ones who kept retrying, never qualify because each attempt updates lastActivity.
comparison: firstSeen-based reset (punishes forgetful users) vs lastActivity-based reset (targets genuinely lapsed users).
use_cases: Free trial abuse prevention; SaaS trial lifecycle management

tool: Typed errors for UI-specific error handling
description: Attaching a .code property to thrown errors lets UI layers respond specifically without parsing message strings. The catch block can branch on err.code to show different CTAs without duplicating string matching logic.
comparison: Message string parsing (brittle, coupled) vs .code property (structured, decoupled).
use_cases: Differentiating upgrade vs buy-tokens CTAs; Any multi-branch error UI in JavaScript

tool: Client-sent headers as informational-only hints
description: Any header sent by the client can be forged. Headers like X-Request-Type should only be used for logging and analytics — never for access control or billing logic. The server must derive entitlement state from its own trusted storage.
comparison: Client-supplied headers (untrusted) vs server-derived state (trusted): never use client headers for authorization.
use_cases: Request logging and analytics; Debugging and observability

tool: Token counting in streaming API responses (SSE)
description: When Anthropic returns a streaming response, the usage object appears in the final message_delta event, not in every chunk. A Cloudflare Worker proxy must buffer the stream to extract this event before performing accurate token deduction. Non-streaming and streaming responses require different parsing paths.
comparison: Non-streaming (usage in top-level body) vs streaming (usage in final SSE event): requires two distinct parsing paths.
use_cases: Token billing for streaming LLM APIs; Cloudflare Worker SSE proxy with usage tracking

tool: Write Tool Silent Truncation
description: The Write tool silently truncates files beyond roughly 191 lines (~7 KB) — returns success but the file on disk is incomplete. The truncation always happens at the same byte boundary. The only reliable workaround for large files is to write them via python3 inside a bash call.
comparison: Write tool (silently truncates at ~7KB) vs bash python3 write (reliable for large files).
use_cases: Writing large generated files in AI coding tools; File output reliability in sandboxed environments

tool: Testing ESM Cloudflare Workers in Node with vm.createContext
description: Cloudflare Workers use ESM (export default), which Node's vm module cannot evaluate directly. Fix via source transform: replace 'export default' with 'self.workerExport =' before passing to vm.Script. All Fetch API globals must be injected into the sandbox explicitly.
comparison: Direct vm evaluation (fails on ESM) vs regex source transform + sandbox injection (works).
use_cases: Unit testing Cloudflare Workers without wrangler; Node-based test harnesses for Worker code

tool: Mutable Fetch Proxy Pattern for Worker Tests
description: When testing a worker loaded once into a vm sandbox, individual tests cannot swap the fetch mock by reloading or reassigning sandbox.fetch. Solution: a mutable proxy — inject fetch as an arrow function wrapping a variable, then reassign that variable per test.
comparison: Direct fetch injection (captures value at injection time, not reassignable) vs mutable proxy pattern (reassignable per test).
use_cases: Per-test fetch mocking in vm-based Worker tests; Any scenario where a sandbox captures a reference at eval time

tool: Per-User KV Entitlement Model
description: Three separate KV namespaces (FREE_TRIAL, TOKEN_BALANCE, UNLIMITED) each keyed by extension ID. Priority checked in order: UNLIMITED → TOKEN_BALANCE → FREE_TRIAL → reject. A user can hold multiple entitlements simultaneously; the highest-tier wins. Separating namespaces avoids read-modify-write races between tiers.
comparison: Global daily counter (single shared limit) vs per-user per-tier namespaces (independent, race-free, priority-ordered).
use_cases: Multi-tier SaaS entitlement; Extension license management; Pay-as-you-go + subscription hybrid billing

tool: Fail-Open KV Policy in Cloudflare Workers
description: Every KV read is wrapped in try/catch. On failure, the worker allows the request through and sets X-Entitlement-Source: fallback. A KV outage degrades to 'everyone gets through' rather than 'everyone is blocked'. The policy must be documented to prevent well-meaning code reviewers from removing the bare catch blocks.
comparison: Fail-closed (blocks all users on outage) vs fail-open (allows all users on outage): fail-open preferred for paid user experience.
use_cases: Entitlement checks in Cloudflare Workers; Any paid-service dependency with availability requirements

tool: Optimistic Token Deduction with Post-Response Correction
description: Deduct a flat estimate (TOKENS_PER_REQUEST) before forwarding to Anthropic. After response, correct the balance using in-memory values: correctedBalance = tokensRemaining + tokensDeducted - actualTokens. Avoids a second KV read. Correction write happens in ctx.waitUntil().
comparison: Locked deduction (requires second KV read) vs optimistic deduction with in-memory correction (single write, no second read).
use_cases: Token billing for LLM proxy workers; Post-response balance reconciliation

tool: SSE Stream tee() for Dual Consumption in Cloudflare Workers
description: A ReadableStream can only be consumed once. response.body.tee() returns [branchA, branchB]. Branch A is forwarded to the client; Branch B is consumed in ctx.waitUntil() to extract token usage. Backpressure on branch B does not stall branch A.
comparison: Single stream consumption (can't both forward and parse) vs tee() dual consumption (forward + parse simultaneously).
use_cases: Token usage extraction from streaming LLM responses; Any Cloudflare Worker needing to both proxy and inspect a stream

tool: ctx.waitUntil() — Extending Cloudflare Worker Lifetime
description: Once a Cloudflare Worker's fetch handler returns a Response, the runtime may terminate the isolate. ctx.waitUntil(promise) keeps the isolate alive until the promise settles. Correct pattern for fire-and-forget side effects like post-response KV writes. Errors must be caught with .catch() as waitUntil does not surface rejections.
comparison: Async work after response (lost on termination) vs ctx.waitUntil (guaranteed to complete).
use_cases: Post-response KV balance correction; Logging and analytics after streaming response; Any async side effect after a Cloudflare Worker response

tool: HMAC-SHA256 Webhook Verification with SubtleCrypto
description: Cloudflare Workers use Web Crypto API exclusively. Pattern: importKey → sign → ArrayBuffer → hex string → timing-safe comparison vs header value. Both importKey and sign are async — forgetting await produces [object Promise] which never matches.
comparison: Node crypto (sync, Buffer-based) vs SubtleCrypto (async, ArrayBuffer-based): both produce same HMAC but require different code patterns.
use_cases: Gumroad webhook verification; Any webhook HMAC verification in Cloudflare Workers

tool: Timing-Safe String Comparison (XOR Accumulation)
description: Standard === short-circuits on first differing character, leaking timing information. Safe pattern: accumulate XOR differences across full string without early exit. Length check must be done first; length itself is not secret for HMAC hex output.
comparison: === (short-circuits, unsafe) vs XOR accumulation (constant time, safe).
use_cases: HMAC webhook signature verification; Any secret token comparison

tool: Gumroad Webhook Payload Format
description: Gumroad sends webhook POST bodies as application/x-www-form-urlencoded, not JSON. Custom fields use bracket notation: custom_fields[extension_id]. Parse with new URLSearchParams(await request.text()). Bracket characters are literal parts of the key name.
comparison: JSON.parse (throws on Gumroad webhooks) vs URLSearchParams (correct parser).
use_cases: Gumroad purchase webhook handling; Extension license activation

tool: 90-Day Inactivity Reset Logic for Free Trials
description: Reset rule requires both conditions: (1) used < total AND (2) lastActivity older than 90 days. Exhausted users are never reset regardless of inactivity. lastActivity is always updated on every request, even for exhausted users, to close the dormancy loophole.
comparison: firstSeen-based reset (exploitable) vs lastActivity-based with exhaustion guard (abuse-resistant).
use_cases: Free trial lifecycle management; Abuse prevention for credit-based trials

tool: git show HEAD:<path> for File Recovery When checkout is Blocked
description: git checkout HEAD -- <file> can be blocked by filesystem permissions in sandboxed environments. Workaround: git show HEAD:<relative-path> > /tmp/restore.js && cp /tmp/restore.js <destination>. git show reads from the git object database without touching the working tree.
comparison: git checkout (writes to working tree, can be permission-blocked) vs git show + cp (reads object DB, bypasses permission check).
use_cases: File recovery in sandboxed or restricted environments; Restoring corrupted working tree files

tool: wrangler .dev.vars — Local Secrets File
description: When running wrangler dev locally, secrets are read from a .dev.vars file in the worker directory, formatted as KEY=VALUE pairs. This file must be gitignored — it is the local equivalent of wrangler secret put and contains plaintext API keys. Not created automatically.
comparison: .wrangler/ (build cache, commonly gitignored) vs .dev.vars (separate secrets file, must be explicitly gitignored).
use_cases: Local Cloudflare Worker development with secrets; Team onboarding for Worker projects

tool: Unicode box-drawing chars causing silent file truncation in AI edit tools
description: The Edit and Write tools silently truncate files when old_string or new_string contains certain Unicode characters (box-drawing chars U+2500, em dashes U+2014). No error is produced; the file is left broken at the byte position of the first problematic character. The bug triggers on OLD content too, not only inserted content.
comparison: Normal ASCII content (safe) vs Unicode box-drawing/em-dash content (triggers silent truncation).
use_cases: Debugging silent file corruption in AI coding tools; Safe file repair in AI-assisted development

tool: CRLF vs LF — Windows files and Python byte-level repair
description: Windows text files use CRLF; Python on Linux defaults to LF. When repairing a Windows file with Python byte-level append, writing \n produces mixed line endings. Always use binary mode and explicit b"\r\n" to be deterministic across platforms.
comparison: Text mode Python write (platform-dependent line endings) vs binary mode with explicit b"\r\n" (deterministic).
use_cases: Cross-platform file repair scripts; Python byte-level file manipulation on Windows-origin files

tool: Python byte-level file repair — anchor-and-truncate pattern
description: Repair procedure for silently truncated files: (1) open in rb, (2) find last known-good ASCII anchor with bytes.find()/rfind(), (3) open in wb and write bytes up to and including anchor, (4) append correct tail as bytestring literal. Anchor must be entirely ASCII to avoid re-triggering truncation.
comparison: Text-mode repair (may re-trigger truncation bug) vs binary anchor-and-truncate (safe, encoding-preserving).
use_cases: Repairing AI tool-corrupted source files; Any byte-level file surgery on corrupted content

tool: MV3 service worker as the only safe origin for Anthropic API calls
description: In Chrome MV3, fetch requests to Anthropic's API must originate from the service worker, not sidebar or content-script contexts. Page contexts are blocked by CORS and Anthropic's browser-detection. The service worker still requires the header 'anthropic-dangerous-direct-browser-access: true' because it emits an Origin: chrome-extension://[id] header.
comparison: Sidebar/content-script fetch (CORS-blocked, browser-detected) vs service worker fetch (bypasses CORS for host_permissions URLs).
use_cases: Claude API calls from Chrome extensions; Any third-party API requiring non-browser origin from an extension

tool: chrome.runtime.sendMessage — return true to keep channel open for async response
description: The chrome.runtime.onMessage listener must return true synchronously if the handler will call sendResponse asynchronously. If the listener returns undefined or false, Chrome closes the message channel immediately. Returning a Promise does not keep the channel open — only the literal true from the callback itself works.
comparison: Returning Promise (channel closes) vs returning literal true (channel stays open for async sendResponse).
use_cases: Async message handling in MV3 extensions; Background fetch on behalf of sidebar

tool: Sidebar-side vs content-script-side module placement in Chrome extensions
description: Detection modules that need access to sidebar-side state (rolling context window, session trajectory) must be loaded in sidebar.html, not injected as content scripts. Content scripts can only communicate via message passing and have no direct access to sidebar variables.
comparison: Content script (page execution context, message-passing only) vs sidebar script (direct access to sidebar state).
use_cases: Module placement decisions in MV3 extension architecture; Stateful detection modules in Chrome extensions

tool: Stateful IIFE module scope for cross-poll persistence in the sidebar
description: An IIFE creates a private scope whose variables persist for the lifetime of the containing page. Variables declared at IIFE top level persist across invocations of exported functions, functioning as module-level state. IIFE state resets when the sidebar is closed and reopened.
comparison: Global variables (namespace pollution) vs IIFE scope (private, persistent within page lifetime).
use_cases: Domain tracking across polling cycles; Any stateful sidebar module needing cross-call memory

tool: Multi-layer suggestion fatigue prevention
description: Preventing recurring UI nudges from becoming noise requires multiple independent gates: minimum-evidence threshold, per-type in-session cooldown, session-level cross-type cooldown, and source-switch early return. No single gate is sufficient — each catches a different failure mode.
comparison: Single gate (insufficient) vs multi-layer gates (comprehensive fatigue prevention).
use_cases: Adaptive suggestion UI in reading tools; Any system with recurring nudges that must avoid noise

tool: Already-at-target guard — defensive programming for logically redundant conditions
description: For current mismatch cases the already-at-target check is logically redundant but worth adding: it handles future suggestion types that may lack mutual exclusivity, catches cross-session edge cases, and makes intent explicit. Implementation: check if all config keys in the suggestion already match current config.
comparison: Implicit safety (relies on current case properties) vs explicit guard (robust to future additions).
use_cases: Suggestion application idempotency; Defensive guards in extensible rule-based systems

tool: chrome.storage.session vs chrome.storage.local — lifecycle and appropriate use
description: chrome.storage.session persists for the current browser session and clears on browser close. chrome.storage.local persists indefinitely. Rolling context window belongs in session storage (stale context from previous session is misleading); user config and highlights belong in local storage (deliberate long-lived choices). Service workers can be killed and restarted within a session, requiring hydration from session storage on restart.
comparison: session storage (cleared on browser close, appropriate for transient state) vs local storage (persists across restarts, appropriate for user preferences).
use_cases: Rolling context window storage; Service worker state hydration after restart; Separating transient vs persistent extension state

tool: SOFT_RESET_CONTEXT — coordinating state wipes across extension components
description: When sidebar detects a source-domain switch, it sends SOFT_RESET_CONTEXT → background calls RC_Context.clear() (wipes in-memory array and session storage) → sidebar clears its own _latestChunks and skips the current poll cycle's mismatch check. Soft reset preserves user config, highlights, and chat history.
comparison: Hard reset (wipes everything) vs soft reset (wipes only transient context window).
use_cases: Domain switch handling in reading extensions; Coordinated state resets across extension components

tool: Detecting domain switches with stateful sidebar polling
description: source_switch module tracks _lastSeenDomain across polls. Each poll extracts hostname from most recent chunk URL, compares to stored value, updates stored value, returns switched:true if different. First call always returns switched:false. www. prefix is stripped before comparison to avoid spurious switches.
comparison: Stateless detection (false positives on every poll) vs stateful IIFE tracking (accurate cross-poll comparison).
use_cases: Source-domain switch detection in browser extensions; Any polling-based change detection requiring previous-state memory

tool: chrome.contextMenus.removeAll() before create() — preventing duplicate ID errors on SW restart
description: Service workers can be killed and restarted between onInstalled events. Calling chrome.contextMenus.create() with the same ID after restart throws a duplicate-ID error. Fix: call removeAll() inside onInstalled before creating items. removeAll() is async/callback-based so create() must be nested inside its callback.
comparison: create() without removeAll (duplicate ID error on SW restart) vs removeAll then create in callback (idempotent).
use_cases: Context menu setup in MV3 service workers; Any idempotent resource registration in service workers

tool: Service worker kill/restart lifecycle — implications for in-memory state
description: Chrome MV3 service workers are ephemeral — killed after ~30 seconds of inactivity, restarted on next event. In-memory state is lost on kill. State needing to survive must be persisted to chrome.storage.session (survives SW restarts within browser session) or chrome.storage.local (survives browser restarts). _hydratePromise must be awaited before any handler uses in-memory state.
comparison: In-memory state (lost on SW kill) vs storage-backed state (survives SW restart).
use_cases: Persistent state in MV3 service workers; Rolling context window hydration after SW restart

tool: Partial config writes with a delta object
description: chrome.storage.local.set() merges into the existing store rather than overwriting. Build a delta object containing only changed keys and pass that to set(). Keys with undefined values will corrupt stored data, so each key must be explicitly checked before inclusion.
comparison: Full config rewrite (overwrites unrelated keys) vs delta write (surgical, preserves untouched keys).
use_cases: Applying partial configuration updates in Chrome extensions; Any storage update where only a subset of keys changes

tool: Patching in-memory config immediately after a successful storage write
description: _config in sidebar.js is loaded once at startup and not automatically updated when storage changes. After APPLY_SUGGESTION, patch _config in-memory inside the sendResponse success callback, then call _renderStrategyBadge() to reflect the change in UI without a reload. Patch must happen after confirmed write to avoid in-memory/storage desync.
comparison: Waiting for next reload (stale config causes spurious mismatch detection) vs immediate in-memory patch (consistent state).
use_cases: Config state management in Chrome extension sidebars; Post-write UI updates without page reload

tool: The _none sentinel pattern in pure-function detectors
description: Define a local _none object representing the nothing-to-report result. Every early-return path returns _none or a shallow copy. Prevents callers from null-checking return values, ensures consistent return shape across all exit paths, and makes early returns self-documenting.
comparison: Returning null (ambiguous, requires null checks) vs _none sentinel (explicit, consistent shape).
use_cases: Pure-function detectors with multiple early-exit conditions; Any function with a no-op result that must be distinguished from error

tool: Suggestion registry as pure data — no side effects in the data layer
description: suggestions.js is a pure data module: SUGGESTIONS is a plain object, lookup functions only read and return. No storage writes, DOM manipulation, or chrome.runtime calls. All side effects are owned by sidebar.js. Enables isolated testing with no mocks and registry extension without touching behavioral code.
comparison: Data layer with side effects (coupled, hard to test) vs pure data layer (isolated, mockless testable).
use_cases: Rule-based suggestion systems; Any architecture requiring separation of data and behavior

tool: Mirrored domain lists across modules — coupling risk
description: REFERENCE_DOMAINS in suggestions.js and KNOWN_DOMAINS in structured.js enumerate the same domain set as two independent constants. If one is updated without the other, behavior becomes inconsistent. Ideal fix is a shared source of truth but content scripts and sidebar scripts can't share modules without a build step. Practical mitigation: a unit test asserting both lists contain the same entries.
comparison: Single shared source of truth (requires build step) vs mirrored constants with test-enforced parity (no-build workaround).
use_cases: Domain-aware extraction and suggestion logic in browser extensions; Any multi-context codebase with shared enumeration constants

tool: No-build browser-side test harness for sidebar-dependent modules
description: Sidebar-side modules depend on window globals and cannot be tested in Node without complex mocking. Solution: standalone HTML file loading real library scripts via relative script tags, inline test helpers, assertions run on page load, results rendered as pass/fail DOM cards. No build step, no test runner, no npm. Script load order must mirror sidebar.html exactly.
comparison: Node test runner (requires mocking window globals) vs browser HTML harness (real environment, no build, no mocks needed).
use_cases: Testing Chrome extension sidebar modules without a build pipeline; Browser-native unit testing for extension code

tool: Edit and Write tools (AI edit tools)
description: The Edit and Write tools silently truncate files when either the old_string or the new_string contains certain Unicode characters — specifically box-drawing chars (U+2500, '──') and em dashes (U+2014, '—'). The truncation affects the target file at the byte position of the first problematic character, regardless of whether that character is in the content being inserted or in the surrounding existing content that was matched. The bug triggers on the OLD content too, not only on what is being inserted.
comparison: Standard file write tools vs byte-level Python repair; normal edit tools fail silently while raw byte manipulation avoids the truncation bug entirely.
use_cases: Repairing files corrupted by silent truncation; Working with files containing Unicode box-drawing or em-dash characters; Byte-level file patching when AI edit tools are unreliable

tool: Python file I/O (binary mode)
comparison: Text mode open() vs binary mode open(); text mode is non-deterministic across platforms while binary mode with explicit b'\r\n' is always deterministic.
use_cases: Cross-platform file repair scripts; Handling Windows-origin source files on Linux; Byte-level append operations requiring consistent line endings

tool: Python byte-level file repair (anchor-and-truncate pattern)
description: When a file is corrupted by silent truncation, the safe repair procedure is: (1) open in 'rb' mode and load all bytes, (2) find the last known-good ASCII anchor string using bytes.find() or bytes.rfind(), (3) open in 'wb' mode and write bytes only up to and including the anchor, (4) append the correct tail as a bytestring literal. The anchor must be entirely ASCII to avoid re-triggering truncation bugs.
comparison: AI Edit/Write tools vs Python byte-level repair; Edit/Write tools can cause truncation while the anchor-and-truncate pattern avoids re-triggering the bug and preserves original encoding.
use_cases: Repairing files corrupted by Unicode-triggered truncation; Safe file patching without re-triggering encoding bugs; Restoring partially written files to a known-good state

tool: Chrome MV3 service worker fetch (Anthropic API)
description: In Chrome Manifest V3, fetch requests to Anthropic's API must originate from the service worker (background.js), not from sidebar or content-script contexts. Page contexts are blocked by both CORS preflight and Anthropic's server-side browser-detection check. The service worker bypasses CORS for URLs listed in host_permissions, but still requires the header 'anthropic-dangerous-direct-browser-access': 'true' because the service worker emits an Origin: chrome-extension://[id] header.
comparison: Sidebar/content-script fetch vs service worker fetch; sidebar fetch is blocked by CORS and server-side detection while service worker fetch succeeds with proper host_permissions and required header.
use_cases: Making Anthropic API calls from Chrome MV3 extensions; Routing API requests through background.js as a proxy for sidebar; Bypassing CORS restrictions in extension contexts

tool: chrome.runtime.onMessage (return true pattern)
description: The chrome.runtime.onMessage listener must return the literal true synchronously if the handler is going to call sendResponse asynchronously. If the listener returns undefined, false, or a Promise, Chrome closes the message channel immediately and any later sendResponse call is a no-op. The return true must come from the onMessage callback itself, not from an inner async function.
comparison: Returning undefined/false/Promise vs returning literal true; the former closes the channel immediately while the latter keeps it open for async response.
use_cases: Async message handling in Chrome MV3 extensions; Keeping message channels open for awaited operations like API calls; Preventing silent 'no response' bugs in extension messaging

tool: Chrome extension module placement (sidebar vs content script)
description: Detection modules that need access to the rolling context window, session trajectory, or other sidebar-side state must be loaded in sidebar.html via script tags, not injected as content scripts. Content scripts run in the page's execution context and can only communicate via message passing with no direct access to sidebar variables or DOM. Rule: if the module consumes sidebar state, load it in the sidebar; if it produces data for the sidebar, inject it as a content script.
comparison: Content script injection vs sidebar script loading; content scripts have no direct sidebar state access while sidebar scripts share the sidebar execution context directly.
use_cases: Structuring Chrome extension modules that consume sidebar state; Deciding where to load detection modules like source_switch.js or mismatch_detector.js; Avoiding message-passing overhead for modules that need direct state access

tool: JavaScript IIFE module pattern (stateful closure)
description: An immediately invoked function expression (IIFE) creates a private scope whose variables persist in memory for the lifetime of the containing page. Variables declared at IIFE scope (like _lastSeenDomain) persist across every invocation of exported functions, functioning as module-level state without global namespace pollution. IIFE state resets whenever the sidebar is closed and reopened because the HTML page is reloaded.
comparison: IIFE closure state vs global variables vs external storage; IIFEs avoid namespace pollution unlike globals and avoid storage I/O unlike chrome.storage, but reset on page reload unlike persistent storage.
use_cases: Cross-poll state persistence within a sidebar session; Stateful domain tracking without external storage; Module encapsulation in no-build browser extension environments

tool: Multi-layer suggestion fatigue prevention system
description: Preventing recurring UI nudges from becoming noise requires multiple independent gates each catching a different failure mode: a minimum-evidence threshold (MIN_CHUNKS=3), a per-type in-session cooldown keyed by mismatch type, a session-level cross-type cooldown (30 min), and a source-switch early return that skips the mismatch check for the affected poll cycle. No single gate is sufficient alone.
comparison: Single-gate vs multi-gate fatigue prevention; a single gate misses failure modes that only the other gates catch, such as two different types firing back-to-back or the same type firing again after cooldown with stale conditions.
use_cases: Preventing UI suggestion fatigue in reading assistant extensions; Designing layered rate-limiting for notification systems; Balancing signal and noise in behavioral mismatch detection

tool: 'Already at target' guard (defensive programming pattern)
description: For the current two mismatch cases the 'already at target' check is logically redundant because detection conditions are mutually exclusive with suggestion targets. The guard is still worth adding because future suggestion types may not have this mutual exclusivity, it catches cross-session edge cases where cooldown has expired and behavior reverted but config was already corrected, and it makes intent explicit in code. Implementation: _alreadyAtTarget checks Object.keys(suggestion.config).every(key => config[key] === suggestion.config[key]).
comparison: Implicit safety via mutual exclusivity vs explicit guard; implicit safety is fragile the moment a new case breaks the assumption while an explicit guard remains valid regardless of future additions.
use_cases: Defensive programming in suggestion/recommendation systems; Guarding against edge cases in config-patching workflows; Making implicit safety properties explicit in code

tool: chrome.storage.session vs chrome.storage.local
description: chrome.storage.session persists for the duration of the current browser session and is cleared when the browser closes. chrome.storage.local persists indefinitely across browser restarts. The rolling context window belongs in session storage because stale reading context from a previous session is misleading. User config and highlights belong in local storage. The service worker can be killed and restarted within the same session, so _hydratePromise re-reads session storage into memory on every SW startup.
comparison: session storage vs local storage; session storage auto-expires with the browser session making it appropriate for transient context, while local storage persists indefinitely making it appropriate for user preferences and highlights.
use_cases: Deciding storage tier for different extension data types; Surviving service worker kill/restart cycles without losing context; Hydrating in-memory state from persistent storage on SW startup

tool: SOFT_RESET_CONTEXT messaging pattern (Chrome extension)
description: When the sidebar detects a source-domain switch it sends a SOFT_RESET_CONTEXT message to background.js, which calls RC_Context.clear() wiping both in-memory array and chrome.storage.session. Simultaneously the sidebar clears its own _latestChunks array and returns early from _pollContext() to skip that cycle's mismatch check. The two-sided reset ensures neither component retains stale pre-switch chunks. A soft reset leaves user config, highlights, and chat history untouched.
comparison: Soft reset vs hard reset; soft reset clears only the transient context window while hard reset would wipe all extension state including config and highlights.
use_cases: Coordinating state wipes across extension components on domain switch; Implementing message-based state management across sidebar and service worker; Preventing stale context from polluting post-switch analysis

tool: Stateful sidebar polling for domain switch detection
description: The sidebar polls the context window every 15 seconds via GET_CONTEXT messages. The source_switch module tracks _lastSeenDomain across calls: each poll extracts the hostname from the most recent chunk's URL, compares to the stored value, updates it, and returns switched:true if they differ. The first call always returns switched:false. Empty chunk arrays and failed chunks are treated as no-change to avoid false positives. The 'www.' prefix is stripped before comparison.
comparison: Stateful poll-based detection vs event-driven detection; poll-based detection works within the existing 15-second polling loop without requiring additional event hooks, but introduces up to 15-second detection latency.
use_cases: Detecting navigation to a new domain in a reading assistant; Triggering context resets on source switches; Avoiding false positives from www vs non-www URL variants

tool: chrome.contextMenus.removeAll() before create()
description: The service worker can be killed and restarted between onInstalled events. If a context menu item was already registered before restart and chrome.contextMenus.create() is called with the same ID, Chrome throws a duplicate-ID error. The fix is to call chrome.contextMenus.removeAll() inside onInstalled before creating any items. removeAll() is asynchronous so the create() call must be nested inside its callback, not placed after it.
comparison: Direct create() vs removeAll() then create(); direct create() throws on duplicate IDs after SW restart while removeAll() first makes the operation idempotent.
use_cases: Making context menu registration idempotent across SW restarts; Preventing duplicate-ID errors in MV3 extensions; Safe context menu initialization in onInstalled handlers

tool: Chrome MV3 service worker kill/restart lifecycle
description: Chrome MV3 service workers are ephemeral — Chrome may kill them after ~30 seconds of inactivity and restart them on the next event. Any in-memory state is lost on kill. State that needs to survive across SW activations must be persisted to chrome.storage.session or chrome.storage.local. A GET_CONTEXT message arriving immediately after SW restart before hydration completes would see an empty in-memory array. Awaiting _hydratePromise before calling getChunks() prevents this race.
comparison: In-memory state vs storage-backed state in service workers; in-memory state is lost on kill while storage-backed state survives restarts, with the trade-off of requiring async hydration on each startup.
use_cases: Designing resilient state management for MV3 extensions; Preventing race conditions between SW hydration and incoming messages; Deciding which data must be storage-backed vs can be in-memory

tool: chrome.storage.local.set() delta/partial write pattern
description: chrome.storage.local.set() merges the provided object into the existing store rather than overwriting it entirely. This means only changed keys need to be written as a delta object. handleApplySuggestion() builds a delta from only the keys present in the payload, leaving other config keys untouched. If the payload includes a key with an undefined value, chrome.storage.local.set() will write undefined for that key, so each key must be explicitly checked before adding to the delta.
comparison: Full config write vs delta write; full config write risks overwriting keys that should be unchanged while delta write only touches the keys that actually changed.
use_cases: Applying partial config updates without touching unrelated settings; Implementing suggestion application that targets only specific config keys; Preventing accidental config corruption when writing strategy updates

tool: In-memory config patch after storage write (sidebar.js pattern)
description: When a user applies a suggestion, background.js writes the new strategy to chrome.storage.local, but _config in sidebar.js is a local object loaded once at startup and is not automatically updated when storage changes. Without an explicit patch, the sidebar continues detecting mismatches based on stale config until the next page reload. The patch must happen inside the success callback of the APPLY_SUGGESTION message, not before it, to avoid in-memory/storage desync on write failure.
comparison: Relying on storage change events vs explicit in-memory patch; storage change events add complexity and latency while an explicit patch in the success callback is immediate and co-located with the write.
use_cases: Keeping sidebar config in sync after suggestion application; Immediate UI feedback after strategy change without page reload; Preventing mismatch detection from firing on already-corrected config

tool: _none sentinel pattern for pure-function detectors
description: Both detectSwitch() and detectMismatch() define a local _none object at the top of the function body representing the 'nothing to report' result. Every early-return path returns _none or a shallow copy via Object.assign. This prevents callers from null-checking return values, ensures return shape is always consistent regardless of exit path, and makes early returns self-documenting — returning _none communicates 'conditions not met' rather than the ambiguous null.
comparison: Returning null vs returning a _none sentinel; null requires null-checks at every call site and is ambiguous in meaning while _none is self-documenting and provides a consistent return shape.
use_cases: Designing pure-function detectors with multiple early-return paths; Avoiding null-check boilerplate in callers of detection functions; Making intent explicit when no action is needed

tool: Suggestion registry as pure data module (suggestions.js)
description: suggestions.js is a pure data module: the SUGGESTIONS registry is a plain object of suggestion descriptors and the two lookup functions only read from it and return a value or null. No storage writes, DOM manipulation, or chrome.runtime calls occur in this module. All side effects are owned by sidebar.js. This separation means suggestion logic can be tested in isolation with no mocks and the registry can be extended without touching behavioral code.
comparison: Pure data module vs mixed data/behavior module; pure data modules are testable in isolation without mocks while mixed modules require mocking all side-effect dependencies.
use_cases: Designing testable suggestion/recommendation registries; Separating data layer from presentation layer in extension architecture; Enabling registry extension without modifying behavioral code

tool: Mirrored domain lists across modules (coupling risk pattern)
description: REFERENCE_DOMAINS in suggestions.js and KNOWN_DOMAINS in structured.js both enumerate the same set of platforms and are maintained independently. If a domain is added to one list and not the other, the extension will extract with the structured strategy but not surface the reference_mode suggestion or vice versa — a subtle inconsistency. The ideal fix is a single shared source of truth but content scripts and sidebar scripts cannot share a module directly without a build step. Practical mitigation: a unit test that asserts both lists contain the same entries.
comparison: Duplicated domain lists vs shared source of truth; duplication is simpler without a bundler but creates a documentation contract rather than an enforced one, while a shared module requires a build step.
use_cases: Managing shared constants across extension contexts without a bundler; Detecting coupling risks between independently maintained lists; Mitigating divergence risk with cross-list unit tests

tool: No-build browser-side test harness for sidebar-dependent modules
description: Sidebar-side detection modules depend on window globals and cannot be tested in Node without complex mocking. The solution is a standalone HTML file that loads real library scripts via relative script tags, defines test helpers inline, and runs assertions on page load with results rendered as pass/fail cards in the DOM. No build step, no test runner, no npm required. Script load order matters exactly as in sidebar.html. Tests run in the real browser environment but cannot run in CI without a headless browser.
comparison: Node-based test runner with mocks vs no-build browser-side harness; Node requires complex mocking of browser globals while the browser harness runs against the real environment with no mocking but requires a browser or headless browser to execute.
use_cases: Testing Chrome extension sidebar modules without a build pipeline; Validating detection module behavior in the real browser environment; Creating lightweight regression tests for no-build extension projects

tool: ldap3 conn.entries vs conn.response
description: conn.entries returns a list of ldap3 Entry objects with an ORM-style interface (entry.cn.value), not plain dicts. Subscripting them with ["attributes"]["cn"] raises a TypeError at runtime against a real directory. conn.response is the raw list of dicts where entry["attributes"]["cn"] works as expected. Filter conn.response to e.get("type") == "searchResEntry" to strip referrals and the result-done control entry.
comparison: conn.entries provides ORM-style access; conn.response provides raw dict access. The bug is invisible in unit tests that mock the LDAP connection and only surfaces against a live directory.
use_cases: LDAP directory queries in Python; AWS directory integration; Filtering searchResEntry results from LDAP responses

tool: moto e2e tests — rewiring module-level boto3 clients
description: boto3.client() called at module import time binds to real AWS before @mock_aws activates. To fix this, reassign the module's client attributes in setUp inside the moto context. SSM caches also survive between tests and must be manually busted. AWS_EC2_METADATA_DISABLED=true must be set before importing any module that calls boto3.client() at the top level, otherwise the import hangs in CI trying to reach the EC2 metadata endpoint.
comparison: Module-level boto3 clients bind before moto context is active; reassigning in setUp ensures they bind within the mock context.
use_cases: Moto-based AWS unit testing; CI pipeline test isolation; Testing Lambda functions with mocked AWS services

tool: DynamoDB item key names must match KeySchema
description: The audit log helper wrote items with key request_id matching the table's KeySchema, but tests asserted record["username"] causing a KeyError that only appeared when the DynamoDB scan ran against moto. The actual item had request_id, employee_name, timestamp, status, and ttl. Always derive test assertions from the actual write path, not assumptions about naming.
comparison: Assumed field names in tests vs actual field names from write path can diverge silently until running against a real or mocked DynamoDB.
use_cases: DynamoDB integration testing; Moto-based DynamoDB testing; Audit log implementations in AWS

tool: SQS dead-letter queue for async Lambda invocations
description: When a Lambda is invoked with InvocationType="Event" (async), AWS retries it twice on failure before discarding the event. Attaching a dead_letter_config with an SQS queue ARN captures the failed payload after those retries. The Lambda execution role needs sqs:SendMessage on the DLQ ARN, and if the Lambda runs inside a VPC, an SQS VPC interface endpoint is required.
comparison: Without a DLQ, failed async Lambda invocations are silently discarded after two retries. With a DLQ, failed payloads are preserved for inspection and reprocessing.
use_cases: Async Lambda error handling; Reliable event-driven architectures on AWS; VPC-bound Lambda reliability patterns

tool: CloudWatch metric alarm on SQS ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible
description: ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible > 0 with period = 60 and evaluation_periods = 1 gives a 60-second detection window on a DLQ. treat_missing_data = "notBreaching" keeps the alarm green when the queue is empty rather than entering INSUFFICIENT_DATA. Wiring alarm_actions to an SNS topic closes the loop so the same pipeline that sends notifications also alerts on its own failures.
comparison: Without an alarm, DLQ messages can accumulate unnoticed. With the alarm, failures surface within 60 seconds.
use_cases: DLQ monitoring and alerting; SQS observability; CloudWatch alarm configuration for AWS pipelines

tool: API Gateway HTTP API (v2) throttling
description: HTTP API stages support default_route_settings with throttling_burst_limit (token bucket peak) and throttling_rate_limit (sustained req/s). This applies to all routes unless overridden per-route. A valid API key does not bypass throttling — the authorizer runs first, then throttling applies. Exceeding the limit returns a 429 Too Many Requests before the Lambda is ever invoked.
comparison: Per-stage throttling applies globally to all routes; per-route overrides allow finer-grained control. Throttling is enforced after authorization but before Lambda invocation.
use_cases: API Gateway rate limiting with Terraform; Protecting Lambda backends from traffic spikes; Multi-route throttle configuration in AWS HTTP APIs

tool: IAM least-privilege — scoping Bedrock to a specific model ARN
description: Bedrock foundation model ARNs follow the format arn:aws:bedrock:<region>::foundation-model/<model-id> with a double colon (no account ID, as foundation models are AWS-managed resources). Scoping bedrock:InvokeModel to this ARN instead of "*" means the role cannot call any other model, even if new ones are added to the account. The ARN must be updated if the model is switched.
comparison: Using "*" for bedrock:InvokeModel grants access to all foundation models. Scoping to a specific ARN enforces least-privilege and prevents unintended model access.
use_cases: IAM policy least-privilege for Bedrock; Security hardening for AI workloads on AWS; Terraform IAM resource configuration

tool: Resolving AWS account ID in Terraform without hardcoding
description: data "aws_caller_identity" "current" {} resolves the account ID of whoever is running terraform apply. Use data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id in ARNs to avoid hardcoding a 12-digit account number. This is the standard pattern for IAM resource ARNs that include the account ID (Lambda, SNS, DynamoDB, etc.) where a direct resource reference would create a circular dependency.
comparison: Hardcoded account IDs break portability across accounts and environments. Using aws_caller_identity is dynamic and avoids circular dependency issues.
use_cases: Terraform IAM policy authoring; Multi-account AWS deployments; Avoiding circular dependencies in Terraform resource ARNs

tool: Exponential backoff retry pattern for external connections
description: Retry loops should distinguish between transient failures (network timeout, connection refused) and permanent failures (wrong credentials, operation not permitted). The pattern: loop up to N attempts, check str(e).lower() for auth error strings and raise immediately, sleep base * (2 ** (attempt - 1)) between retries, re-raise the last exception after exhausting attempts. Import time inside the function to avoid patching it globally in tests.
comparison: Flat retry intervals waste time and can trigger lockouts on permanent failures. Exponential backoff with failure-type discrimination is more efficient and safer.
use_cases: Resilient LDAP or API connections in Python; Retry logic for transient AWS service errors; Avoiding account lockouts on auth failures

tool: SSM Parameter Store as a runtime-tunable config store with Lambda caching
description: Reading SSM on every Lambda invocation adds 10–50 ms latency and costs $0.05 per 10,000 reads. The standard pattern is to cache the value in a module-level dict with a TTL timestamp. On invocation, check if time.time() - loaded_at > TTL; if stale, fetch and update. A 5-minute TTL means config changes propagate within one cache window without redeployment. Multiple independent parameters should have separate cache dicts. Tests must manually reset the cache dict between test cases.
comparison: No caching: low latency variance but high cost and latency per invocation. TTL-based caching: reduces cost and latency with a bounded propagation delay for config changes.
use_cases: Lambda configuration management via SSM; Runtime-tunable thresholds without redeployment; Cost optimization for SSM reads in high-frequency Lambda functions

tool: Python test patch targets — import namespace matters
description: Patching with @patch("module_a.some_func") patches the name in module_a's namespace. If module_b does from module_a import some_func, the binding in module_b is already made at import time and patching module_a.some_func has no effect on calls inside module_b. You must patch module_b.some_func to affect calls inside module_b. The rule: patch where the name is used, not where it is defined.
comparison: Patching at the definition location does not affect already-bound references in other modules. Patching at the usage location correctly intercepts calls.
use_cases: Python unittest.mock patching strategy; Avoiding silent test patching failures; Testing modules that import functions from other modules

tool: AWS Simple AD vs Managed Microsoft AD
description: Simple AD (~$36–40/month Small) is Samba 4-based and lacks trust relationships, MFA, and PowerShell AD module support. Managed Microsoft AD Standard (~$87–140/month) provides a true Microsoft AD domain controller. For workloads that only perform basic LDAP operations (user create, group membership modify, account disable via userAccountControl), Simple AD is functionally identical at less than half the cost.
comparison: Simple AD: Samba 4-based, cheaper, lacks trusts/MFA/PowerShell AD. Managed Microsoft AD: true AD domain controller, more expensive, full feature set. Choose based on whether missing features are actually needed.
use_cases: Cost-optimized LDAP workloads on AWS; Basic user and group management via LDAP; Architecture decisions for AWS directory services

tool: GitHub Actions — Node.js runtime deprecation for actions
description: GitHub Actions runs each uses: action on a Node.js runtime. When GitHub deprecates a Node version (e.g. Node 20 to Node 24 cutover June 2026), actions pinned to older major versions emit warnings and will eventually fail. The fix is to bump the action version tag: actions/checkout@v4 to @v5, actions/setup-python@v5 to @v6. Check the action's releases page to confirm the new tag ships with the required Node runtime.
comparison: Older action version tags use deprecated Node runtimes and will fail after cutover dates. Newer version tags ship with updated Node runtimes and remain supported.
use_cases: CI/CD pipeline maintenance; GitHub Actions version management; Proactive deprecation handling for automated workflows

error: None explicitly stated
root_cause: Template-substitution approach creates invisible coupling between prompt layers
fix: Adopted append-only layer composition in buildSystemPrompt(): base template → content layer → trajectory layer → follow-up suffix → structural invariant, where no layer may modify or delete prior layer content
concept: Append-only prompt layer composition ensures predictability and auditability by concatenating layers in strict order, isolating each layer's contribution and guaranteeing the structural invariant always appears last

error: Low-confidence classifier output injecting wrong content-type layer into system prompt
root_cause: The content-type layer was being appended regardless of classifier confidence, meaning a weak or incorrect classification would contaminate the base template with a bad instruction
fix: Added a confidence gate inside `buildSystemPrompt()` that only appends the content-type layer when the classifier returns `confidence > 0.7`; below that threshold the layer is silently skipped and a fallback log entry is written to `chrome.storage.local` under `rcClassifierFallback`
concept: Confidence-gated layer injection — the consumer of a classifier result (not the classifier itself) decides whether to act on the output based on the confidence score, keeping the classifier stateless and the gating logic centralized in the prompt-building step

error: N/A
root_cause: Need to classify content into coarse types without model inference or network calls
fix: Implemented classifyContent(text) using regex pattern matching with keyword-density scoring, normalised per 100 words, with confidence clamped to [0.35, 0.95] and a margin bonus for unambiguous wins
concept: Keyword-density content classification scores five content types (argumentative, instructional, narrative, reflective, reference) by counting regex pattern matches and normalising per 100 words; density normalisation makes the metric length-agnostic and the margin bonus rewards clear winners without overfitting

error: Trajectory classification emitting false signals on single-chunk or minimal sessions
root_cause: Insufficient data points cause meaningless trend detection; a single 'skim' or 'deep' tag cannot represent a true session trajectory
fix: Require a minimum of 3 chunks before emitting any trajectory; return 'insufficient' status when below this threshold
concept: getTrajectory(chunks) classifies reading sessions as 'declining' | 'rising' | 'stable' | 'insufficient'. Declining requires 2+ consecutive skim tags at the tail of the window and takes priority over rising. Rising requires >50% of chunks tagged deep. Terminal skim behavior is treated as the highest-signal event, indicating active loss of user interest in the current moment.

error: Response format inconsistency breaking UI parser that splits on bracket markers [OBSERVATION] / [INSIGHT] / [QUESTION]
root_cause: Format constraints were not guaranteed to be present in every composed prompt path; templates could silently omit them through edits or branching logic in buildSystemPrompt()
fix: Append the structural invariant as the final string in buildSystemPrompt() unconditionally, after every other layer including the follow-up suffix, so it cannot be repositioned or omitted by any code path
concept: Structural invariant as final override in prompt composition; placing format constraints last maximises recency weight in the model's attention and centralises authorship of format rules rather than duplicating them across nine base templates

error: Prompt token count exceeds 400-token soft budget
