root_cause: Composed prompt length surpasses the warn threshold estimated via Math.ceil(prompt.length / 4) character-to-token heuristic
fix: Developer tunes templates to reduce prompt length; buildSystemPrompt() emits console.warn but never throws, truncates, or modifies the prompt
concept: Token budget enforcement as a monitoring signal rather than a fatal error — preserves structural invariant and context integrity while alerting developers to oversized prompts

error: Scripts reading globals like `window.RC_Context` receive `undefined` when load order is incorrect
root_cause: MV3 content scripts and sidebar scripts do not use ES modules, so there is no automatic dependency resolution — load order in manifest.json or sidebar.html is the sole mechanism for ensuring dependencies are available
fix: List all lib/ files explicitly in manifest.json (content scripts) or sidebar.html (sidebar) in strict dependency order, ensuring each file appears after the files whose globals it consumes
concept: Chrome Extension MV3 script load order as a manual dependency graph; each file exposes its API on a namespaced global (window.RC_*) to reduce collision risk with third-party scripts on arbitrary host pages and to make the dependency graph visible by reading the source list

error: Direct fetch() calls to api.anthropic.com from content scripts or sidebar pages are blocked by the browser's CORS policy
root_cause: MV3 content scripts run in the page's security context, subject to the page's CSP and the API's CORS policy; only the service worker holds the host_permissions grant and the anthropic-dangerous-direct-browser-access header
fix: Move all Anthropic API calls to background.js (service worker); use message-passing pattern where sidebar.js sends chrome.runtime.sendMessage({ type: 'CALL_CLAUDE', ... }) and background.js handles the request and returns the response via sendResponse
concept: In MV3 browser extensions, the service worker runs in an extension context with its own origin and elevated permissions, making it the only valid context for reaching external APIs restricted by CORS

error: Fallback logging throws when chrome API is undefined outside extension context
root_cause: chrome.storage.local is unavailable in plain browser tab test harness where the chrome API is not defined
fix: Wrap the chrome.storage.local write in a try/catch so it silently fails outside the extension context without breaking the test harness
concept: When classifier confidence falls below the gate threshold, buildSystemPrompt() writes { fallback: true, confidence, contentType, ts } to chrome.storage.local under rcClassifierFallback. Fallback events are invisible to the user but persisted across sessions and inspectable from the service worker DevTools console.

error: LLM output is non-deterministic and hard to assert programmatically
root_cause: LLM responses cannot be validated with regex or diff-based assertions for nuanced requirements
fix: Each test case is a directory with input.json (full parameter set) and criteria.md (5 human-review pass/fail criteria); harness builds system prompt from input.json and human checks off criteria
concept: LLM regression testing using input+criteria pairs where human-review criteria capture nuanced requirements that automated assertions cannot; criteria count fixed at 5 per case to balance coverage and readability; harness UI provides toggle buttons per criterion to track pass/fail state

error: Build step overhead and complexity when running tests
root_cause: Use of bundlers or npm scripts as a prerequisite to running any test, creating maintenance burden and barriers to entry
fix: Created a plain HTML test harness (test_harness.html) that loads lib/ files via relative paths and embeds test data inline as a JS array, runnable directly in a browser tab with no server, npm, or bundler required
concept: No-build test harness using window.RC_* globals and file:// context; relative paths tie the harness to the project directory structure (tests/prompt_regression/test_harness.html), which is acceptable for a developer-only tool

error: Each call site had to manually remember to call getSystemPrompt, optionally append FOLLOWUP_SUFFIX, and then call callClaude — forgetting any step produced subtly wrong prompts with no error
root_cause: No encapsulation of the classify → getTrajectory → buildSystemPrompt → callClaude pipeline; each of the three sidebar.js call sites duplicated the multi-step sequence manually
fix: Introduced callClaudeWithContext({ messages, chunks, extractionStrategy, behaviorStrategy, isFollowUp }) to run the full pipeline in one call, updating all three call sites from the manual 3-step sequence to this single function
concept: Pipeline encapsulation via callClaudeWithContext; lower-level callClaude remains exported as an escape hatch for callers with unusual needs, making callClaudeWithContext a convenience layer rather than a forced gate

error: Using type='module' in a MV3 content script causes silent load failure in some versions and is unsupported in others
root_cause: Chrome Manifest V3 does not support ES module syntax in content scripts
fix: Assign public APIs to namespaced globals (window.RC_* pattern) and declare files in correct dependency order in the manifest content_scripts array
concept: Chrome Manifest V3 content scripts cannot use ES module syntax (import/export). The project solves this by assigning each lib/ file's public API to a namespaced global (e.g., window.RC_Context = { ... }) and loading files in dependency order via the manifest content_scripts array. No build step or bundler is used; the load order in manifest.json is self-documenting.

error: MV3 service worker restarts lose in-memory context window data
root_cause: MV3 service workers are ephemeral and spin down after ~30s of inactivity, wiping any in-memory state on restart
fix: Hydrate in-memory array (_chunks) from chrome.storage.session on startup using a one-time promise (_hydratePromise); addChunk() awaits the promise before writing to prevent race conditions where a new chunk arrives before hydration completes
concept: Service worker hydration pattern using chrome.storage.session to persist a rolling context window across SW restarts; session storage is chosen intentionally so context dies with the browser session rather than persisting indefinitely like chrome.storage.local

error: BehaviorStrategy singleton losing accumulated DOM signal state across classify() calls
root_cause: Instantiating a new strategy per classify() call discards all dwell and scroll data accumulated since the last call, producing meaningless zero-baseline signals
fix: Store the strategy as a module-level singleton in lib/behavior/index.js keyed on strategy type; reset state by creating a new instance only when location.href changes for SPA navigation
concept: getBehaviorStrategy() returns a singleton per page load that attaches DOM event listeners (scroll, mousemove, visibilitychange, blur) in its constructor and accumulates signal state across the full session rather than resetting between classify() calls which occur every 10-30 seconds

error: Passive dwell time inflating engagement classification when tab is left open without focus
root_cause: Dwell time accumulated regardless of tab visibility or window focus, and had no upper bound — a tab left open overnight would falsely classify users as deep readers on subsequent pages
fix: MitigateStrategy tracks only active dwell (tab visible AND window focused) and enforces a hard cap of 300 seconds (ACTIVE_DWELL_CAP_S) to prevent overnight inflation into deep territory
concept: Active dwell vs passive dwell: elapsed time should only count when document.visibilityState === 'visible' AND the window has focus; a configurable hard cap (ACTIVE_DWELL_CAP_S = 300s) in mitigate.js keeps all thresholds tunable without touching classification logic

tool: Scroll Velocity via Rolling Time Window
description: Scroll velocity is computed from a 3-second rolling window of recent scroll events (SCROLL_VELOCITY_WINDOW_MS = 3000). Each scroll event appends { px, ts } to an array; on each new event, stale entries older than 3 seconds are discarded before computing px/s. Mousemove events are throttled to 200ms intervals (MOUSEMOVE_THROTTLE_MS) to avoid flooding the scroll array with spurious velocity-zero entries from cursor movement.
comparison: A simple session-average scroll speed would misclassify users who scroll fast initially then slow down to read; the rolling window captures current behaviour rather than the average over the whole session.
use_cases: Distinguishing skimmers from engaged readers by capturing real-time scroll behaviour; feeding scroll velocity into engagement scoring or content recommendation systems where current reading pace matters more than historical average.

error: Readability.js not available as a module without a build step or bundler
root_cause: Project has no build step or bundler, so npm packages cannot be imported directly; Readability.js must be available as a global on the page window
fix: Vendor Readability.js directly into vendor/Readability.js (v0.5.0 extracted via npm pack) and list it first in the manifest content_scripts array so it loads as a global via its UMD wrapper
concept: Mozilla's Readability.js uses a UMD wrapper that allows it to function as a global when loaded via a script tag, without requiring an import or bundler; vendoring pins the exact version, removes network dependency, enables offline use, and insulates the project from npm registry changes

error: Mismatched data lifetime between storage type and conceptual lifetime of data
root_cause: Using a single storage layer (e.g., chrome.storage.local) for both persistent settings and session-scoped reading context conflates data with different intended lifetimes, polluting storage with stale data across sessions
fix: Split storage by conceptual lifetime: use chrome.storage.session for rolling reading context (cleared on browser close) and chrome.storage.local for persistent user settings (extractionStrategy, behaviorStrategy, onboardingComplete)
concept: chrome.storage.session vs chrome.storage.local — session-scoped data should use session storage to match its inherent lifetime, while configuration data that must survive restarts belongs in local storage. The architectural principle is: let data lifetime match conceptual lifetime.

error: N/A
root_cause: N/A
fix: N/A
concept: A 3x3 persona matrix is constructed as the Cartesian product of two onboarding question axes: extraction strategy (readability | viewport | structured) and behavior strategy (mitigate | embrace | erase). The function getSystemPrompt(extraction, behavior) looks up a base template by the resulting key pair, mapping each combination to a named character archetype (e.g. The Analyst, The Enthusiast). Keys are plain strings stored via chrome.storage.local.

error: Template-substitution creates invisible coupling between layers and makes contributions hard to audit.
root_cause: Substitution-based approaches allow layers to overwrite each other, breaking isolation and predictability.
fix: Adopt append-only composition so every layer's contribution is auditable by reading the string top to bottom and each layer can be unit-tested in isolation.
concept: Append-only prompt layer composition: buildSystemPrompt() concatenates layers in strict order (base template → content layer → trajectory layer → follow-up suffix → structural invariant). No layer may modify or delete what a prior layer wrote. Chosen over template-substitution to avoid invisible coupling between layers.

error: Injecting a low-confidence content-type instruction actively degrades the model response.
root_cause: A low-confidence classification is essentially a guess; appending it contaminates the base template with a weak signal.
fix: Gate layer injection on confidence > 0.7 inside buildSystemPrompt(); fall back to the solid base template when confidence is insufficient.
concept: Confidence-gated layer injection: the content-type layer is only appended when classifier returns confidence > 0.7. Below threshold the layer is skipped and a fallback log entry is written to chrome.storage.local under rcClassifierFallback. Gate lives inside buildSystemPrompt(), not in the classifier.

error: Length-variant raw counts make short and long chunks incomparable.
root_cause: Without normalisation, a 2000-word chunk always outscores a 200-word chunk regardless of actual content type signal.
fix: Normalise counts per 100 words so the metric is length-agnostic; add a margin bonus to reward unambiguous wins.
concept: Keyword-density content classification: classifyContent(text) scores five content types by counting regex pattern matches normalised per 100 words. Winner's score fraction plus a margin bonus gives confidence, clamped to [0.35, 0.95]. Heuristic chosen over neural classifier for speed and synchronicity.

error: Single-chunk sessions can emit false trajectory signals.
root_cause: There is insufficient data to establish a trend from one or two chunks.
fix: Emit 'insufficient' when fewer than 3 chunks are present; prioritise terminal declining signal over historical rising majority.
concept: Session trajectory from behavioral tag tail: getTrajectory(chunks) classifies session as declining | rising | stable | insufficient. Declining requires 2+ consecutive skim tags at the tail. Rising requires >50% deep-tagged chunks. Declining takes priority. Minimum 3 chunks before any trajectory is emitted.

error: If format constraints are embedded per-template they can be silently omitted by a template edit, breaking the sidebar parser that splits on bracket markers.
root_cause: Distributed format constraints create nine places where the constraint can be forgotten or misauthored.
fix: Append the invariant unconditionally at the very end of buildSystemPrompt() so it is authored once and can never be repositioned or omitted.
concept: Structural invariant as final override: the format instruction ('Respond in exactly 2-3 sentences…') is always the last string appended unconditionally, after every other layer including the follow-up suffix. Its final position maximises recency weight in the model's attention.

error: Automatic truncation would break the structural invariant or drop context unpredictably.
root_cause: A long composed prompt is a monitoring signal, not a fatal runtime error; treating it as fatal causes worse outcomes than the over-length prompt itself.
fix: Log a warning and let the developer tune templates; never mutate the prompt in response to a budget breach.
concept: Token budget warn-never-throw: buildSystemPrompt() estimates token count as Math.ceil(prompt.length / 4) and logs console.warn if over 400 tokens. It never throws, truncates, or modifies the prompt. Characters ÷ 4 is an acceptable rough heuristic for a soft warning threshold.

error: A file that reads window.RC_Context before context.js is loaded gets undefined with no error.
root_cause: Without a bundler or module system the manifest/HTML load order is the only dependency resolution mechanism.
fix: List files in strict dependency order and namespace all globals under window.RC_* to make the dependency graph visible and auditable.
concept: Chrome extension script load order as dependency graph: MV3 content scripts and sidebar scripts don't use ES modules. Every lib/ file is listed explicitly in manifest.json or sidebar.html in dependency order, each exposing its API on a window.RC_* namespaced global to reduce collision risk with third-party scripts.

error: Direct fetch from content script or sidebar is blocked by the browser's CORS policy.
root_cause: MV3 content scripts run in the page's security context subject to the page's CSP and the API's CORS policy; only the service worker has an elevated extension origin.
fix: Route all Anthropic API calls through the service worker via message-passing: sidebar sends CALL_CLAUDE message, background.js handles it and returns response via sendResponse.
concept: Anthropic API calls must live in the service worker: fetch() calls to api.anthropic.com from content scripts or sidebar pages are blocked by CORS. Only background.js holds host_permissions and the anthropic-dangerous-direct-browser-access header. sidebar.js uses chrome.runtime.sendMessage to delegate API calls to background.js.

error: Without try/catch the test harness throws on every low-confidence case because the chrome API is undefined outside the extension context.
root_cause: The test harness runs in a plain browser tab where window.chrome is not defined, so any chrome.storage write without error handling crashes the harness.
fix: Wrap the storage write in try/catch; silently swallow the error outside the extension context while preserving the logging behaviour inside it.
concept: Silent fallback logging to chrome.storage.local: when classifier confidence is below the gate threshold, buildSystemPrompt() writes {fallback, confidence, contentType, ts} under rcClassifierFallback. The write is wrapped in try/catch so it silently fails in the test harness where the chrome API is undefined.

error: Programmatic assertions cannot capture nuanced qualitative requirements such as 'the observation must not be a plot summary'.
root_cause: LLM output is non-deterministic; identical inputs can produce different valid outputs, making exact-match or diff-based assertions unreliable.
fix: Pair each test input with 5 explicit human-review criteria and a harness UI with per-criterion toggle buttons for pass/fail tracking.
concept: LLM regression test as input + criteria pair: each of the 10 test cases contains input.json (full parameter set) and criteria.md (5 human-review pass/fail criteria). Human review is used because LLM output is non-deterministic and nuanced requirements cannot be captured by regex or diff.

error: A build step is a maintenance burden and a barrier to running tests quickly.
root_cause: Build tooling adds friction; because lib/ files use globals rather than ES modules they are directly loadable from file:// without any transformation pipeline.
fix: Open test_harness.html directly from inside the project directory; no server, npm, or bundler required.
concept: No-build test harness via plain HTML: test_harness.html loads real lib/ files via relative paths, embeds test case data inline as a JS array, and runs entirely in a browser tab. The window.RC_* global pattern means files load cleanly from a file:// context without transformation.

error: Each call site had to remember to call getSystemPrompt, optionally append FOLLOWUP_SUFFIX, and call callClaude; forgetting any step produced subtly wrong prompts with no error.
root_cause: Repeated multi-step sequences at every call site create opportunities for partial or inconsistent execution with no fail-fast signal.
fix: Encapsulate the full pipeline in callClaudeWithContext so the happy path is the only path for common usage, while keeping callClaude exported for edge cases.
concept: callClaudeWithContext as pipeline encapsulation: single function runs classify → getTrajectory → buildSystemPrompt → callClaude. Replaces three manual call sites that each performed a 3-step sequence. Lower-level callClaude remains exported for callers with unusual needs.

error: Using type='module' in a content script causes silent load failure in some MV3 versions.
root_cause: ES module support is not available or reliable in MV3 content scripts; attempting it silently fails rather than throwing a useful error.
fix: Use the window.RC_* global pattern and explicit manifest load ordering as the conventional MV3 workaround, turning the constraint into a self-documenting feature.
concept: MV3 no-ES-modules constraint and the window.RC_* pattern: Chrome MV3 content scripts cannot use ES module syntax. Every lib/ file assigns its public API to a namespaced global and files are loaded in dependency order via the manifest content_scripts array. Load order in manifest.json is self-documenting.

error: Without hydration, every service worker restart would lose the rolling context window because MV3 service workers are ephemeral and spin down after ~30s.
root_cause: MV3 service workers have no persistent in-memory state across restarts; all in-memory arrays are reset on each activation.
fix: Read storage into memory on startup via a one-time hydration promise; gate all writes on that promise to prevent race conditions.
concept: Service worker hydration with chrome.storage.session: context.js reads chrome.storage.session once on startup into _chunks. addChunk() awaits a one-time _hydratePromise before writing so restored data is never clobbered. getChunks() is synchronous because hydration is guaranteed complete before any meaningful API call.

error: Creating a new strategy instance per classify() call discards all dwell and scroll data accumulated since the last call, producing meaningless zero-baseline signals.
root_cause: classify() is called every 10–30 seconds; a fresh instance has no historical signal context and cannot produce meaningful engagement scores.
fix: Store strategy instance as a module-level variable keyed on strategy type; reuse it across classify() calls and reset only on SPA navigation.
concept: BehaviorStrategy singleton with stateful DOM listeners: getBehaviorStrategy() returns a singleton per page load. The strategy attaches scroll, mousemove, visibilitychange, and blur listeners in its constructor and accumulates signal state across the full session. SPA navigation resets state when location.href changes.

error: Passive dwell inflates engagement scores; uncapped dwell causes false deep-reader classification for users who leave a tab open overnight.
root_cause: Time elapsed is not a proxy for reading engagement unless the user is actively attending to the tab.
fix: Gate dwell accumulation on visibility AND focus; apply a hard cap to prevent runaway inflation; expose cap as a tunable constant.
concept: Active dwell vs passive dwell with hard cap: MitigateStrategy tracks elapsed time only when tab is visible AND window has focus. A hard cap of 300 seconds (ACTIVE_DWELL_CAP_S) prevents a tab left open overnight from inflating dwell into deep territory. All thresholds are tunable constants at the top of mitigate.js.

error: Session-average scroll velocity misclassifies a user who skims the opening then slows to read closely.
root_cause: Averaging over the full session smooths out the current behaviour signal that is most relevant to real-time engagement classification.
fix: Use a 3-second rolling window so velocity reflects current behaviour; throttle mousemove events to 200ms to prevent array flooding.
concept: Scroll velocity via rolling 3-second time window: each scroll event appends {px, ts} to an array; stale entries older than 3000ms are discarded on each new event before computing px/s. Mousemove events are throttled to 200ms intervals to avoid spurious velocity-zero entries.

error: Without vendoring the extension depends on npm registry availability and risks breaking if the package changes.
root_cause: The project has no build step or bundler; the pre-built CommonJS UMD file from npm pack works as a global when loaded via script tag.
fix: Vendor the file into source control via npm pack to pin version, enable offline use, and avoid registry changes breaking the extension.
concept: Readability.js vendored as a global: Mozilla Readability.js v0.5.0 is vendored into vendor/Readability.js via npm pack, listed first in manifest content_scripts, and executes as a UMD global. Vendoring pins the exact version, removes network dependency, and allows offline operation.

error: Storing reading context in local storage pollutes persistent storage with stale cross-session data and makes context window semantics unclear.
root_cause: Reading context is inherently session-scoped; yesterday's article chunks are irrelevant and misleading in today's session.
fix: Store context in chrome.storage.session and settings in chrome.storage.local so each data type's storage lifetime matches its conceptual lifetime.
concept: chrome.storage.session vs chrome.storage.local intentional lifetime split: rolling reading chunks stored in session storage (cleared on browser close); settings stored in local storage (survives restarts). Matches data lifetime to conceptual lifetime: settings = forever, reading context = this browser session only.

error: Special-casing persona combinations creates refactoring burden when adding new personas and risks inconsistency between combinations.
root_cause: Non-orthogonal axis design means each new combination may require unique logic rather than composing cleanly from two independent axes.
fix: Define two independent orthogonal axes; generate all combinations as their Cartesian product so new personas require extending only one axis without refactoring the other.
concept: 3×3 persona matrix from two onboarding questions: 9 AI personas are the Cartesian product of extraction strategy (readability | viewport | structured) × behavior strategy (mitigate | embrace | erase). getSystemPrompt(extraction, behavior) looks up the base template by key pair. Keys are strings for natural serialisation to chrome.storage.local.

tool: Windows→Linux mount mtime desync and Python bytecode cache
description: When a file on a Windows filesystem is edited via a tool and then accessed from a Linux mount (WSL or Docker bind-mount), the mtime visible on the Linux side does not update automatically. Python's bytecode cache (__pycache__/*.pyc) compares the source mtime to the .pyc mtime to decide whether to recompile — if the .pyc appears newer, Python silently uses the stale compiled version even though the source changed. The symptom is that code edits have no effect at runtime. importlib.reload() does NOT help — the stale .pyc is loaded before you can reload anything. Fix: run touch <file>.py on the Linux side after editing from Windows to force mtime to now, making the source appear newer than the .pyc and triggering a recompile on the next import.
comparison: Unlike a pure Linux filesystem where mtime updates atomically on write, a Windows-backed mount seen through WSL or Docker bind-mount does not propagate mtime changes to the Linux VFS layer automatically, making Python's cache invalidation logic unreliable in cross-OS workflows.
use_cases: Debugging silent no-effect code changes in WSL or Docker bind-mount dev environments; any workflow where source files are edited on Windows and executed on Linux.

tool: Pydantic v2 field_validator mode='before' for type coercion
description: In Pydantic v2, a @field_validator('field_name', mode='before') runs before the field's declared type is checked. This makes it the right place to coerce values that arrive in the wrong type — e.g., an LLM generating a list [] where a model expects str. The validator receives the raw input value and returns the coerced form; Pydantic then type-checks the returned value normally. Gotcha: if the .pyc for the file containing the model is stale, Pydantic will use the old model definition and the validator will silently not exist. Pattern: if isinstance(v, list): return '; '.join(str(item) for item in v if item) handles both empty lists and populated lists.
comparison: Compared to mode='after', mode='before' validators receive the raw pre-cast value, making them suitable for type coercion rather than post-validation transformation. mode='after' validators see the already-typed value and are better for semantic validation.
use_cases: Handling LLM output schema drift where a list is returned instead of a string; coercing loosely-typed API responses into strict Pydantic models; normalizing heterogeneous input types before model instantiation.

tool: Pydantic v2 model_validator mode='before' for field aliasing and promotion
description: A @model_validator(mode='before') receives the entire raw input dict before any field validators or type checks run. This is the correct hook for field aliasing — promoting an alternate field name to the canonical one when the source omits the required field. Because it runs first, the promoted value flows through all downstream field validators normally. Pattern for LLM schema drift: if not data.get('error') and data.get('concept'): data['error'] = data['concept'] — recovers entries where the model used a non-spec field name as the headline.
comparison: Compared to field_validator which operates on a single field, model_validator mode='before' operates on the entire input dict, making it the right tool for cross-field aliasing and structural normalization before per-field processing begins.
use_cases: Recovering entries from LLM output where the model drifts from the expected schema and uses alternative field names; normalizing data from multiple sources with inconsistent field naming conventions.

tool: JSON null-byte padding and parse error handling
description: When a JSON file is written with a fixed-size buffer or via certain file tools, it can be padded with null bytes (\x00) beyond the actual JSON content. json.load() reads the full file including the padding and raises json.JSONDecodeError: Extra data because it sees content after the closing ] or }. The file is not corrupt — the JSON itself is valid; there is just invisible trailing garbage. Gotcha: content.strip() does NOT remove null bytes — only whitespace characters. Fix: content = f.read().rstrip(b'\x00').rstrip() before passing to json.loads().
comparison: Standard json.load() has no tolerance for trailing non-whitespace content, unlike some parsers that support streaming or lenient modes. The null-byte issue is invisible in most text editors, making it a hard-to-diagnose failure.
use_cases: Reading JSON files written by tools that use fixed-size write buffers; handling JSON files produced by embedded systems or certain file I/O libraries; defensive JSON loading in data pipelines that ingest externally produced files.

tool: Response cache as a no-cost replay and recovery mechanism
description: MindCI caches every API response to data/response_cache.json keyed by SHA-256(model | max_tokens | prompt). When downstream processing fails, the cached responses can be replayed through the fixed pipeline without any new API calls or cost. The cache is LRU-bounded at 1000 entries and persists across sessions, doubling as an audit log and a free recovery path. Gotcha: the cache key includes the full prompt text, so any change to the prompt text including whitespace produces a cache miss — intentional to prevent stale responses after prompt edits, but means prompt debugging always hits the API. Recovery pattern: iterate response_cache.json filtering by timestamp, parse each cached response through the fixed validate_entries(), write valid entries to structured.json with zero API calls.
comparison: Unlike a pure memoization cache focused only on performance, this cache serves a dual purpose as both a cost-saving layer and a recovery audit log, which justifies persisting it across sessions rather than treating it as ephemeral.
use_cases: Recovering a knowledge base after a validation bug silently drops entries without re-incurring API costs; auditing what responses were received for a given prompt; replaying API responses through an updated processing pipeline during development.

tool: _salvage_partial_json — brace-depth walking for truncated LLM output
description: When an LLM response is cut off before the closing ] of a JSON array, standard json.loads() fails on the whole response. The salvage strategy walks the raw string character-by-character tracking brace depth and string/escape state, identifying every fully-formed {...} object even when the outer array is incomplete. Each candidate slice is parsed independently with json.loads(); only syntactically complete objects are kept. This recovers all entries that the LLM finished writing before the context window filled. Gotcha: the brace-depth walker must correctly handle escaped characters inside strings (\\, \") or it will miscount depth and produce invalid slices — the implementation skips two characters on \\ to consume the escaped char. The tradeoff: salvage recovers partial batches silently — always check report['salvage_warning'] after processing a large note.
comparison: Compared to simply failing on truncated JSON, brace-depth walking maximizes data recovery at the cost of implementation complexity and the risk of silent partial recovery if the escape-handling logic has edge cases.
use_cases: Recovering partial LLM JSON output when context window limits truncate the response mid-array; building resilient LLM output parsers that maximize yield from expensive API calls; any pipeline that must handle variable-length structured output from generative models.

tool: parse_and_save_json deduplication by source field
description: Every KB entry carries a source field (the originating filename). On each write, parse_and_save_json loads the existing structured.json, computes the set of source values in the new batch, drops any existing entries whose source matches, then merges retained and new entries. This means re-converting a note replaces all its previous entries atomically while entries from all other notes are preserved untouched. Split sections use _partN suffixes as distinct source names so they accumulate correctly across multi-part conversions. Implication: if you re-convert a note with a different filename or the CLI auto-generates note_part1.txt vs the original note.txt, both source families coexist in the KB — intended for split workflows but a footgun if you rename files.
comparison: Compared to a naive overwrite strategy, source-keyed deduplication allows incremental updates to a shared knowledge base without requiring a full rebuild, but introduces subtle correctness risks when filenames change or differ between runs.
use_cases: Incrementally updating a structured knowledge base when individual notes are revised; managing multi-part note splits without duplicating entries; maintaining a shared KB across many source files with independent update lifecycles.

tool: Batch convert UI total_saved miscounting valid entries
description: In app_dashboard.py, the batch convert loop does total_saved += len(_parsed) where _parsed is the raw output of parse_and_save_json — which returns ALL parsed entries (valid + invalid), not just the valid ones that actually made it into structured.json. The report dict contains the correct counts (valid_count, invalid_count), but the UI success message only uses total_saved. This means '233 entries saved' can appear even when 232 were invalid and only 1 was written to disk. This is a silent data loss path — the UI says success but the KB is nearly empty. Fix pending: change total_saved += len(_parsed) to total_saved += _report['valid_count'] so the success message reflects what was actually persisted.
comparison: The report dict already tracks valid_count and invalid_count correctly; the bug is solely in the UI aggregation layer reading the wrong field, making this a presentation-layer bug with serious observability consequences rather than a core logic error.
use_cases: Any batch processing UI that aggregates counts from a pipeline with validation steps; identifying silent data loss patterns in ETL dashboards; auditing success messaging accuracy in data ingestion tools.

tool: Long-note auto-split via heuristic section detection
description: Notes above SPLIT_THRESHOLD_WORDS (default 500) are routed to detect_note_sections(), which tries five splitting strategies in priority order: explicit section markers and further strategies not captured in the truncated source text. This is a heuristic system designed to break long notes into manageable chunks for LLM processing while preserving semantic boundaries.
comparison: Compared to naive fixed-size chunking, heuristic section detection attempts to split at semantically meaningful boundaries, reducing the risk of cutting mid-topic, though the fallback strategies may produce less clean splits when explicit markers are absent.
use_cases: Preprocessing long study notes or documents before sending to an LLM with context window constraints; building document ingestion pipelines that respect semantic structure; splitting large markdown or plain-text notes into independently processable parts.

tool: Document Chunking Strategy
description: A hierarchical chunking pipeline that splits documents using progressively finer-grained markers: markdown headings (#–####), triple-or-more blank lines, double blank lines (grouped to avoid tiny chunks), and finally a midpoint bisect at the nearest paragraph boundary. The strategy that fired is returned alongside the sections so callers can surface it to the user. Each section is converted independently and given a unique _partN source name to accumulate in the KB without collisions.
comparison: Heading-based splitting is the most semantically clean strategy, preserving topic boundaries; blank-line strategies are fallbacks that work well for structured prose but may lose context; midpoint bisect is a last resort and the least semantically aware, risking mid-thought cuts compared to the other strategies.
use_cases: Splitting large technical notes or documentation into KB-friendly chunks without collisions, processing markdown-heavy study notes, ingesting plain prose documents where heading markers are absent, and surfacing chunking transparency to end users by reporting which strategy fired.

tool: parse_and_save_json / structured.json history versioning
description: Before overwriting data/structured.json, parse_and_save_json copies the current file to data/history/structured_YYYY-MM-DD_HH-MM-SS.json. This creates a full chronological audit trail of every KB state. Recovery from any bad write is a single file copy. The history directory grows unboundedly with no pruning policy, so long-lived projects will accumulate many snapshots. Recovery pattern: find the history file just before a bad conversion via ls -lt data/history/, then copy it back to data/structured.json.
comparison: No explicit alternative tool compared; the approach contrasts with in-place overwrites that provide no recovery path versus this copy-on-write pattern that enables point-in-time rollback.
use_cases: Audit trailing every KB state change, recovering from bad or truncated JSON writes by restoring the most recent valid snapshot, debugging data pipeline regressions by comparing historical structured.json states.

tool: Pydantic v2 @field_validator (multi-field decorator)
description: A single @field_validator decorator can target multiple field names in one call: @field_validator('use_cases', 'comparison', mode='before'). Pydantic v2 calls the same function for each listed field, passing the individual field's value each time. This avoids repeating identical coercion logic across separate validators and keeps the model definition compact. For mode='before' coercions the field ordering in the decorator list has no effect since each field is processed independently, whereas mode='after' validators run post-type-check and order can matter.
comparison: Compared to writing separate @field_validator decorators per field, the multi-field form eliminates duplicated coercion logic and reduces model verbosity while producing identical runtime behavior for mode='before' validators.
use_cases: Applying the same list-to-string or string coercion logic to multiple fields such as use_cases and comparison in a single validator definition, keeping Pydantic model classes concise when several fields share identical pre-processing requirements.

tool: vm.createContext (Node.js vm module)
description: When a JavaScript array is created inside a vm.createContext sandbox (e.g. via a [] literal in the IIFE under test), its prototype chain belongs to the vm context's Array, not the host Node.js Array. In Node 22, assert.deepStrictEqual(vmArray, []) throws 'Values have same structure but are not reference-equal' because deepStrictEqual detects the prototype mismatch even for empty arrays. Arrays produced by calling .filter() or .map() on a host-context array (data passed in from the test) come back as host arrays and compare cleanly — only arrays born entirely inside the vm are affected. Gotcha: assert.deepEqual from node:assert/strict maps to deepStrictEqual, so the strict prototype check is always active even when you write deepEqual.
comparison: Arrays created inside the vm sandbox have a different prototype (vm context Array) than host arrays, causing deepStrictEqual to fail on reference equality even for structurally identical arrays. Arrays derived from host-context arrays via .filter() or .map() retain the host prototype and compare cleanly.
use_cases: Testing code that runs inside Node.js vm sandboxes; asserting on array outputs from vm-executed IIFEs; avoiding false test failures due to prototype mismatches when comparing vm-born arrays to host literals.

tool: Write / Edit file tools
description: The Write and Edit file tools silently truncate output at roughly 28000 bytes — the write appears to succeed (no error returned) but the file on disk is shorter than the content sent. The truncation happens mid-line with no warning, making it hard to detect until the file fails to parse. This affected lib/prompts.js (cut off mid-buildUserMessage) and cloudflare-worker/worker.js (cut off mid-function declaration). Gotcha: The Read tool can show stale cached content from earlier in the same conversation session, making the file look complete when the disk copy is actually truncated. Always verify with wc -l or tail via bash.
comparison: Write and Edit tools are convenient for small files but silently fail for content exceeding ~28 KB, whereas bash heredocs (cat >> file << 'ENDOFFILE') and git restore methods handle large files reliably without truncation.
use_cases: Writing or editing large source files (>25 KB) such as lib/prompts.js or cloudflare-worker/worker.js; recovering truncated files from git history; verifying file integrity after writes using bash commands like wc -l or tail.

tool: bash heredoc append
description: cat >> file << 'ENDOFFILE' appends content to a file verbatim with no shell expansion because of the quoted delimiter. This is the reliable fallback when file tools truncate. Quoting the ENDOFFILE sentinel prevents the shell from expanding $variables or backticks inside the block. For a complete rewrite use a temp file: write with heredoc to /tmp/scratch, then cp /tmp/scratch destination. Gotcha: actual Unicode characters (e.g. U+2026) inside a heredoc may cause the shell to misparse the ENDOFFILE marker if there is an encoding issue, leaving the file with a dangling partial line or a stray byte. Prefer escaped forms inside content rather than raw glyphs. After append: always verify with xxd | tail to check for stray bytes at the end of the file.
comparison: Compared to file tools that may truncate, heredoc append is more reliable for large or verbatim writes. Using a quoted sentinel differs from an unquoted one in that it suppresses all shell expansion inside the block.
use_cases: Appending large blocks of content to files without shell expansion, recovering from file tool truncation issues, performing full file rewrites safely via a temp file intermediary.

tool: git show for file recovery
description: git show HEAD:relative/path/to/file prints the file content at the last commit to stdout. Piping it to a temp file and then copying avoids the git checkout permission error that can occur when the working-tree file is locked by the mounted filesystem (Operation not permitted). The pattern git show HEAD:path > /tmp/copy && cp /tmp/copy path works even on read-only-mounted paths where git's atomic rename strategy fails. Always check git log --oneline -5 first to confirm the last commit actually has the version you want before overwriting.
comparison: Compared to git checkout, using git show piped to a temp file avoids permission errors on locked or read-only-mounted working-tree files where git's atomic rename strategy fails.
use_cases: Recovering truncated or corrupted files from the last git commit, working around filesystem permission errors during checkout, restoring files on read-only-mounted paths.

tool: Cross-session memory — Approach A (article highlight injection)
description: When a user returns to a previously read article, up to three previously starred passages (rcHighlights[] from chrome.storage.local) are injected into buildSystemPrompt as a 'Cross-session context —' block. Highlights are selected most-recent-first using .slice(-3) and truncated to 200 characters each to control token cost. The model is instructed to let them inform its observations and insights without quoting them directly, preventing replay of the user's own words as new content. URL canonicalization strips tracking params (utm_*, fbclid, gclid, ref, source), fragments, and trailing slashes before comparison so variants like article?utm_source=twitter#comments match the stored article.
comparison: Unlike preference-signal approaches that aggregate behavioral statistics over time, highlight injection uses the literal content of prior saved passages as contextual priming, making it more content-specific but also more dependent on the quality and recency of what the user has starred.
use_cases: Returning users revisiting articles they have previously annotated, where prior highlights should subtly shape the assistant's framing without repeating the user's own words back to them.

tool: Cross-session memory — Approach D (preference signal)
description: rcHighlightStats accumulates a count of how many times the user has saved each section type (observation, insight, question) across all sessions. If total saves are >= 3 AND one type holds a strict majority (> 50% of all saves), a single-sentence preference note is appended to the system prompt indicating which section type the user most frequently saves. The strict majority gate (not just plurality) is intentional to prevent early saves from biasing the prompt before sufficient signal exists. A 3/3/0 split intentionally produces dominantSection = null because the gate requires strictly more than 50%, not exactly half. The min-3 threshold prevents noise from firing on the very first interaction.
comparison: Unlike highlight injection (Approach A) which uses literal saved text as context, the preference signal abstracts user behavior into a single categorical preference, making it lower-token and more stable over time but losing the specific semantic content of what was saved.
use_cases: Personalizing system prompts for returning users who have accumulated enough save history to reveal a reliable behavioral preference for a particular section type, without overwhelming the prompt with raw highlight content.

tool: URL canonicalization for cross-session highlight matching
description: Highlights are stored with the URL at save-time, which may differ from the URL at revisit-time (different UTM params, fragments, or a trailing slash). A `_canonicalizeUrl(url)` helper normalises both the stored and current URL before comparison: it uses `new URL()` to parse, deletes known tracking query params, strips the hash, strips the trailing `/`, and lowercases the result. A regex fallback handles malformed URLs that `new URL()` rejects. Gotcha: `new URL()` is not available in all JavaScript environments — in the Node vm sandbox used by `load-script.mjs`, it must be explicitly injected as an extra global (`{ URL: URL }` in the sandbox options), otherwise `_canonicalizeUrl` throws `ReferenceError: URL is not defined`.
comparison: Stored URLs may include UTM params, fragments, or trailing slashes absent at revisit-time; canonicalization normalizes both sides before comparison, unlike naive string equality which would cause missed matches.
use_cases: Matching saved highlights to the current page URL across sessions where URL formatting may vary due to tracking parameters, hash fragments, or trailing slash inconsistencies; also relevant when running URL parsing logic in sandboxed Node vm environments that lack browser globals.

tool: Append-only buildSystemPrompt layer ordering
description: `buildSystemPrompt` composes the final system prompt by string-appending layers in a fixed order. No layer may modify content added by a prior layer. The structural invariant is unconditionally always the last thing appended, making it impossible for any future layer addition to accidentally bury the response-format instruction. Cross-session layers (4a highlights, 4b preference) slot between the trajectory layer and the follow-up suffix, preserving all prior layer semantics without touching them. Rule: when adding new layers, add before `STRUCTURAL_INVARIANT` and after the follow-up suffix check — never reorder the invariant append.
comparison: Compared to a mutable or reorderable composition approach, the append-only fixed-order model provides a stronger safety guarantee that the structural invariant cannot be displaced by future modifications.
use_cases: Composing layered LLM system prompts where response-format instructions must always appear last; enforcing architectural constraints when multiple contributors or future developers may add new prompt layers over time.

tool: vm.createContext (Node.js vm module)
description: vm.createContext creates a minimal sandbox that does not include Node.js globals like URL, fetch, TextEncoder, etc. If the script under test calls new URL(str), it will throw ReferenceError: URL is not defined unless URL is passed in the extraGlobals object when calling loadScript. The chrome mock and any global stubs the script depends on must all be injected explicitly — nothing leaks in from the host Node context automatically. Gotcha: get(keys, cb) {} method shorthand in object literals is parsed as a getter accessor definition in JavaScript, which does not accept parameters. Always use get: function(keys, cb) {} colon syntax in mock objects to avoid SyntaxError: Setter must have exactly one formal parameter.
comparison: Unlike the host Node.js context which has all globals available automatically, vm sandbox contexts start minimal and require explicit injection of every global the script depends on.
use_cases: Testing browser extension scripts or isolated code in Node.js where you need a controlled environment; mocking chrome APIs and other globals for unit tests without polluting the host context.

tool: buildSystemPrompt token budget system
description: buildSystemPrompt estimates prompt token count as characters divided by 4 and logs a console.warn if it exceeds the budget. The budget was raised from 400 to 600 tokens to accommodate new cross-session layers (up to 3 highlights x 200 chars each equaling roughly 150 tokens of headroom, plus the preference sentence). The warning is monitoring-only and never truncates or throws, so raising the threshold is a documentation and observability change rather than a behavioral one. Estimation heuristic: 200 characters equals approximately 50 tokens at the chars/4 rate; three 200-char highlights plus one preference sentence equals roughly 175-200 tokens total.
comparison: The chars/4 heuristic is a rough approximation compared to exact tokenizer counts, but is sufficient for budget monitoring purposes since the warning is non-blocking.
use_cases: Monitoring prompt token cost when adding new prompt layers such as cross-session memory highlights or user preference sentences; estimating token overhead before deploying new prompt architecture changes.

tool: ADR — Approach A + D vs B or C (cross-session memory architecture)
description: Approach B (domain-level summary across all articles on a site) was rejected because it requires aggregating highlights across multiple articles, adding storage query complexity and risking irrelevant context injection. Approach C (per-article context replay — replaying entire prior conversations) was rejected due to token cost and the risk of the model getting stuck in prior conversation frames. Approach A + D together provide a lightweight signal (a few hundred tokens) that personalises the response without constraining it: A anchors to what the user found interesting in the specific article, D nudges section weighting based on long-run preference patterns. Both layers are append-only, read-only during prompt construction, and fail silently when storage is empty, keeping the fast path completely unchanged.
comparison: Approach B adds storage query complexity and injects potentially irrelevant cross-article context; Approach C is token-expensive and risks locking the model into prior conversation frames; Approach A + D is lightweight, non-constraining, and degrades gracefully with no history.
use_cases: Personalising article reading assistants with per-article highlight anchoring and long-run preference weighting, while preserving a clean fast path for first-time or anonymous visitors with no stored history.

tool: Python .pyc cache staleness on Windows→Linux filesystem mounts
description: When a file is modified through a Windows host tool and the Linux side accesses it via a mount, the Linux kernel may not update the file's mtime or the change may not be reflected in cached inode metadata. This causes Python's .pyc validity check (which compares stored mtime + size against the source .py) to conclude the pyc is fresh even when the content has changed. The safe workaround is to re-write the file through the Linux process itself (e.g. cat > file << 'EOF' in bash), which forces the filesystem to issue a real mtime update. Gotcha: inspect.getsource() and direct python -c invocations will see the correct updated code (they compile fresh), but pytest uses its own assertion-rewrite cache keyed on mtime+size and will silently run stale tests.
comparison: inspect.getsource() and python -c compile fresh each time and see updated code correctly, whereas pytest uses a separate assertion-rewrite .pyc cache keyed on mtime+size that can serve stale bytecode silently when mtime is not updated by cross-OS writes.
use_cases: Debugging stale test behavior when editing test files through Windows GUI tools or IDE plugins while running pytest on a Linux filesystem mount; ensuring mtime is properly updated by writing files via bash heredoc across Windows/Linux mount boundaries.

tool: pytest assertion rewrite cache (.cpython-X.Y-pytest-Z.pyc)
description: pytest rewrites assert statements in test files to provide rich failure output. It compiles a separate .pyc named <module>.cpython-<ver>-pytest-<pytest_ver>.pyc in __pycache__/. This cache is validated against the source file's mtime and size — if both match, pytest uses the cached (potentially stale) bytecode without recompiling. This is distinct from the normal Python .pyc and is invisible to python -c invocations. Gotcha: A test can pass when run directly with python -c but fail under pytest if the pytest-specific .pyc was compiled before a code change that did not update the mtime.
comparison: The pytest-specific .pyc rewrite cache is separate from the normal Python .pyc; python -c always compiles fresh and is unaffected by this cache, while pytest silently uses stale bytecode if mtime+size have not changed.
use_cases: Understanding why tests behave differently under pytest versus direct python -c execution; diagnosing silent stale test runs after code edits that did not trigger an mtime update; invalidating the pytest rewrite cache by forcing an mtime refresh on the test file.

tool: _salvage_partial_json — zero-API-call recovery for truncated LLM JSON
description: When an LLM response is cut off mid-array due to token limits, the JSON is invalid but already-completed objects are recoverable. A character-by-character walker tracks brace depth and in-string/escape state to identify complete {...} blocks without relying on a full JSON parser. Each candidate block is attempted with json.loads and successes are collected. The escape skip (i += 2 on \) is critical — without it, backslash-escaped quotes inside strings are misread as string boundaries, corrupting depth tracking. Gotcha: The walker must skip TWO characters on \ (not one), otherwise the escaped character is re-examined and can trigger false string-open/close events.
comparison: Compared to sending the truncated payload back to an LLM for repair, the character-by-character walker requires zero API calls and recovers all fully-formed objects instantly; the LLM repair approach is a last resort only when salvage returns empty.
use_cases: Recovering structured data from truncated LLM JSON responses caused by token limit cutoffs; building a tiered fallback pipeline: direct parse first, then salvage partial objects, then LLM repair as a last resort.

tool: Anthropic API output token cap causing JSON truncation
description: The Anthropic API enforces a hard max_tokens limit on model output. If a structured JSON response would exceed this limit, the response is cut off mid-string, producing invalid JSON. The symptom is an Unterminated string JSON parse error at a character offset near max_tokens * ~4 (bytes per token approximation). The fix is to raise max_tokens for conversion calls (8192 for dense notes) and add a recovery layer for cases that still truncate. Gotcha: The default max_tokens (4096) is often set to match generation tasks — conversion of long notes needs a separate, higher cap.
comparison: Default max_tokens of 4096 is sufficient for typical generation tasks but insufficient for structured JSON conversion of long or dense notes, which may require 8192 or higher to avoid mid-string truncation.
use_cases: Configuring Anthropic API calls for structured JSON output from long notes; distinguishing truncation errors from other malformed-JSON errors using is_truncation = 'Unterminated string' in str(parse_error) to route them to a truncation-aware repair path.

tool: Smarter JSON repair prompt for truncated arrays
description: When a large JSON array is truncated, sending the full malformed payload back to the model wastes tokens because the middle is valid and the model cannot do anything useful with it. A better approach sends only the head (first ~300 chars, for context) and tail (last ~800 chars, where the truncation happened) and asks the model to complete the last incomplete entry as a single JSON object. The repaired object is then wrapped in a list by the caller if needed. Gotcha: The repair path should only activate when _salvage_partial_json fails (returns empty), to avoid an unnecessary API call in the common case.
comparison: Sending only the head and tail of the truncated JSON to the repair model is significantly more token-efficient than sending the full malformed payload, since the valid middle section provides no useful signal for completing the truncated tail.
use_cases: Repairing truncated LLM JSON array responses with minimal token cost; implementing a fallback repair step in a tiered JSON recovery pipeline using snippet = bad_json[:300] + '\n...[middle omitted]...\n' + bad_json[-800:].

tool: KB merge + dedup by source field
description: An append-only or overwrite-only knowledge base design breaks incremental workflows. If the same note is re-converted after editing, new entries should replace old ones for that note rather than duplicate them. The source field (the filename the entry came from) is the natural dedup key. The process is: load existing KB, compute new_sources set, filter out retained entries whose source is in new_sources, merge retained and new valid entries, then write. For split sections, each section gets a unique _partN source name so they accumulate correctly rather than overwriting each other. Gotcha: The previous validate_and_save helper used an overwrite-only design — calling it for each split section meant each section's results wiped the previous section's KB entries.
comparison: An overwrite-only design causes split section results to clobber each other on each write, whereas a merge+dedup approach keyed on the source field allows incremental re-conversion of individual notes without duplicating or losing entries from other notes or other sections of the same note.
use_cases: Building incremental knowledge base pipelines where individual notes can be re-converted and updated without full rebuilds; handling split multi-section notes by assigning unique _partN source names to accumulate section results correctly.

tool: detect_note_sections heuristic — priority ordering and min_chars threshold
description: A five-tier heuristic for splitting long notes into topic sections without an API call. Full description was truncated in the source material.
comparison: Using a local heuristic for section splitting avoids an API call entirely compared to asking an LLM to identify section boundaries, at the cost of relying on structural patterns in the note text.
use_cases: Splitting long technical notes into topic sections prior to structured JSON conversion to avoid hitting API token limits on a single large note.

error: Chunks constructed from short strings may fall below the 150-char min_chars threshold, causing a valid strategy to fall through to midpoint bisect
root_cause: The threshold filter rejects chunks that are technically valid for a given strategy but too short, so the function incorrectly escalates to the next fallback tier
fix: Pad test strings to >=150 chars or pass min_chars=0 when the test is specifically about strategy detection rather than threshold filtering
concept: detect_note_sections uses a tiered fallback: (1) CPM section markers, (2) markdown headings #-####, (3) triple+ blank lines, (4) double blank lines, (5) midpoint bisect at nearest paragraph boundary. Each tier checks min_chars before accepting. Returns a (sections, strategy_label) tuple.

tool: Streamlit multi-file uploader with per-file state isolation
description: st.file_uploader accepts accept_multiple_files=True to return a list of UploadedFile objects. A st.selectbox lets users switch between staged files. Per-note state must be keyed to the currently selected file and cleared on file switch using an index pointer (convert_file_idx) stored in st.session_state, compared against the selected index on each render to trigger state resets on mismatch.
comparison: Without per-file state isolation, state from one file bleeds into another when the user switches files; the index-pointer pattern prevents this by detecting mismatches at the top of the modal body before any stateful widgets are rendered
use_cases: Multi-file note upload workflows in Streamlit dashboards where each file requires independent processing state such as quality check results, split sections, and enrichment data

tool: CLI auto-splitting long notes (mindci.py convert)
description: The CLI convert command classifies notes as short (<=SPLIT_THRESHOLD_WORDS) or long (>threshold). Short notes are batched into a single Claude call. Long notes are split via detect_note_sections and each section is converted independently with a _partN suffix appended to the source name. The watch command inherits this behavior by delegating to cmd_convert. CLI and dashboard share the same parse_and_save_json and merge/dedup logic, producing identical KB state.
comparison: Short notes use the original single-call batch path while long notes use the section-split multi-call path, but both paths converge on the same KB write logic ensuring consistent output regardless of entry point
use_cases: Processing long technical notes via CLI or file watcher where a single API call would be insufficient, with each section accumulating independently in the knowledge base under its own dedup key

tool: ruff I001 — isort import block violations
description: ruff's I001 rule enforces isort-style import ordering and grouping. Common violations include stray blank lines within an import section or orphaned blank lines left after removing a dependency. F401 suppression does not suppress I001, so unused imports that leave formatting irregularities still fail CI. Running ruff check --fix auto-corrects the formatting.
comparison: F401 (unused import) and I001 (import ordering) are independent rules in ruff; suppressing one does not affect the other, unlike some linters where import-related rules are grouped
use_cases: CI pipelines enforcing consistent import formatting in Python projects, especially after dependency removal or refactoring that leaves behind blank lines in import blocks

tool: Copy-on-write KB versioning to data/history/
description: Before any write to data/structured.json, the existing file is copied to data/history/structured_YYYY-MM-DD_HH-MM-SS.json, providing a full audit trail of every KB state without a formal database or git-tracked data files. The version is created from existing entries before merge, so history reflects pre-write state. The write order is: version first, then validate new entries, then dedup, then write.
comparison: Compared to git-tracked data files or a formal database, this approach is simpler to implement and query while still providing point-in-time rollback capability through newest-first sorted versions
use_cases: Lightweight knowledge base versioning where full audit trail and rollback are needed without the overhead of a database or complications of git-tracking large JSON data files

tool: Chrome MV3 Service Worker Restart Race Condition
description: Chrome MV3 service workers are killed after ~30s of inactivity and restarted on demand. When a message arrives immediately after restart, module-level state populated from chrome.storage.session during the previous lifetime hasn't been re-hydrated yet, so synchronous reads return stale or empty data. The fix is to expose the internal hydration promise (_hydratePromise) on the module's public API so the message handler can await it before reading state. This avoids making the state-reading function itself async, which would require every caller to change. Gotcha: chrome.storage.session is cleared on browser restart but survives SW idle-kill, yet the in-memory JS variables that mirror it do not — this mismatch is the root cause. Fix: expose _hydratePromise on the public object, make the one caller (handleGetContext) async, and await RC_Context._hydratePromise before calling getChunks().
comparison: Unlike MV2 background pages which persist in memory, MV3 service workers are ephemeral and lose all in-memory state on idle-kill, even though chrome.storage.session data survives, creating a divergence that does not exist in MV2.
use_cases: Handling messages in Chrome MV3 extensions where service worker state must be re-hydrated from storage before responding; any extension that caches session state in memory across message handling cycles.

tool: Chrome MV3 Async Message Handlers
description: chrome.runtime.onMessage handlers that call sendResponse asynchronously must either return true to keep the channel open or be declared async. Changing a handler to async function is safe and does not break the return true pattern — the runtime only cares that the channel stays open until sendResponse is called. Mixing async handlers with sendResponse is the idiomatic MV3 pattern for awaiting internal state before replying. Gotcha: forgetting to return true in a non-async handler that calls sendResponse inside a promise callback causes the port to close before the reply arrives and the caller receives undefined.
comparison: In MV2, background pages had more flexibility with persistent connections; in MV3, the async handler plus return true pattern is the required idiomatic approach to keep message channels open during asynchronous operations.
use_cases: Any Chrome MV3 extension message handler that needs to await asynchronous operations such as storage reads, fetch calls, or internal promise resolution before sending a response back to the caller.

tool: 429 Error Shape Mismatch — Proxy String vs Anthropic Object
description: When two different backends both return HTTP 429, their error body shapes can differ, breaking a single parsing path. Anthropic's API returns { error: { type: '...', message: '...' } } (nested object), while a Cloudflare Worker proxy typically returns { error: 'insufficient_tokens' } (flat string). Code that checks body.error.message works for Anthropic but silently gets undefined for the proxy string, swallowing the specific error code. The fix is to branch on which call path is active before parsing. Gotcha: typeof body.error === 'string' vs typeof body.error === 'object' is the reliable discriminator — don't try to infer from the HTTP status alone. Check storedKey (direct path) vs proxy path, parse body.error.message for direct, parse body.error as string for proxy, throw named code (DEMO_LIMIT_REACHED / INSUFFICIENT_TOKENS).
comparison: Anthropic direct API uses a nested error object { error: { type, message } } while Cloudflare Worker proxy uses a flat string { error: 'insufficient_tokens' }; discriminating by typeof body.error is more reliable than inferring from HTTP status alone
use_cases: Handling divergent error body shapes from multiple 429-returning backends in a Chrome extension; routing error parsing logic based on call path (storedKey direct vs proxy); throwing named error codes to avoid swallowing specific error conditions

tool: Named Error Codes vs Generic HTTP Status Propagation
description: Propagating raw HTTP status codes to UI copy requires a switch on numbers and makes copy fragile when a status is reused for multiple conditions (e.g., 429 for rate limit vs. trial exhausted vs. token depletion). Named string error codes (RATE_LIMITED, DEMO_LIMIT_REACHED, INSUFFICIENT_TOKENS) thrown at the API layer and caught by the UI layer allow each condition to have distinct user-facing copy, independent of HTTP semantics. Each code maps exactly one-to-one to an ERROR_COPY entry. Flow: parse error, throw new Error('CODE: message'), UI splits on ':', looks up ERROR_COPY[code], renders correct string.
comparison: Raw HTTP status codes are fragile for UI copy because a single status like 429 can represent multiple distinct conditions; named string error codes provide a stable one-to-one mapping to user-facing copy entries regardless of HTTP semantics
use_cases: Decoupling UI error copy from HTTP status codes in API-consuming applications; mapping multiple distinct 429 sub-conditions to separate user-facing messages; building a maintainable ERROR_COPY lookup table keyed by named string codes

tool: SubtleCrypto (Web Crypto API) — HMAC-SHA256 Webhook Verification
description: Gumroad and most webhook providers sign their POST body with a shared secret using HMAC-SHA256 and include the hex digest in a request header. To verify: import the secret as a CryptoKey via crypto.subtle.importKey, compute crypto.subtle.sign('HMAC', key, bodyBytes), hex-encode the result, and compare to the header value. The comparison must be timing-safe (constant-time) to prevent timing oracle attacks. SubtleCrypto does not provide this natively, so a manual byte-by-byte XOR loop is required. Using === string comparison leaks timing information proportional to the length of the matching prefix. Gotcha: If GUMROAD_WEBHOOK_SECRET is not set in the Worker environment, importKey receives the string 'undefined', silently creating a valid key for the wrong secret, making every request with the wrong signature appear valid. Guard: if (!env.GUMROAD_WEBHOOK_SECRET) return 500 → import key → sign body → timing-safe compare.
comparison: Native === string comparison is fast but timing-unsafe; a manual XOR byte loop is slower but constant-time and required for security-sensitive comparisons like HMAC digest verification.
use_cases: Verifying signed webhooks from providers like Gumroad inside a Cloudflare Worker, preventing forgery of webhook payloads by third parties who do not possess the shared secret.

tool: Cloudflare KV — Webhook Replay Attack Prevention via Idempotency Keys
description: A valid signed webhook can be replayed by a malicious actor or re-delivered by the provider's retry logic, causing duplicate purchases to be credited. The standard defense is idempotency: store the unique sale or event ID in a KV store after the first successful processing and reject any subsequent request carrying the same ID with a 200 response, not 4xx. Returning 200 on duplicates is intentional — returning 4xx causes the provider to keep retrying, compounding the problem. Gotcha: KV read errors during the idempotency check should fail open (allow the request) to avoid blocking legitimate purchases during a KV outage; log the error but do not reject. Flow: Read PROCESSED_SALES.get('sale:<saleId>') → if found return 200 with { duplicate: true } → process → write PROCESSED_SALES.put('sale:<saleId>', '1', { expirationTtl: 86400 * 365 }).
comparison: Failing closed on KV errors (rejecting the request) is safer from a duplication standpoint but risks blocking all legitimate purchases during an outage; failing open prioritizes availability over strict deduplication and is preferred for purchase flows.
use_cases: Preventing duplicate purchase credits in Cloudflare Worker webhook handlers when providers implement automatic retry logic or when a signed webhook is intentionally replayed by a malicious actor.

tool: Environment Variable Guard — Hard Fail vs Silent Degradation
description: When a Cloudflare Worker depends on a secret (e.g., GUMROAD_WEBHOOK_SECRET), the absence of the binding causes env.GUMROAD_WEBHOOK_SECRET to be undefined at runtime. JavaScript string coercion means 'undefined' becomes a valid (wrong) key value, silently accepting all requests or creating a different HMAC than intended. The correct behavior is to hard-fail at the top of the handler: check the env var is truthy before doing any crypto, and return 500 with a server_misconfiguration body. This surfaces the configuration error immediately during deployment testing rather than in production. Gotcha: wrangler secret put stores secrets correctly, but if wrangler.toml KV bindings reference a namespace ID that was never created, the binding is undefined at runtime with no startup error.
comparison: Hard-fail on missing env var (return 500 immediately) vs silent degradation (undefined coerced to string 'undefined', wrong HMAC computed, all requests silently accepted or rejected incorrectly)
use_cases: Securing Cloudflare Worker webhook handlers that depend on secrets for HMAC verification; deployment smoke-testing to catch missing bindings before production traffic hits the handler

tool: Delta-Based Analytics Writes to Avoid Over-Counting
description: When a function is called multiple times during a session and each call writes a cumulative total to storage, the total inflates unless the write is idempotent. The pattern for session-scoped counters is to track a _lastRecordedValue in memory alongside the live counter, compute delta = current - lastRecorded, write only the delta to storage, then update _lastRecorded = current. This is resilient to repeated calls and does not require locking because JS is single-threaded. The session marker (sessions++) should fire only when the counter transitions from 0 to 1 (first turn), not on every write. Gotcha: storing the accumulated total directly (totalTurns += _sessionTurnCount) in a handler called on every turn produces triangular-number inflation: a 3-turn session writes 1+2+3=6 instead of 3.
comparison: Delta-based writes (delta = _sessionTurnCount - _lastRecordedTurns, then entry.totalTurns += delta) vs direct cumulative writes (totalTurns += _sessionTurnCount on every call), where the latter causes triangular-number inflation
use_cases: Session-scoped analytics counters in Chrome extensions or similar single-threaded JS environments where a write handler is invoked multiple times per session and storage must reflect accurate totals without over-counting

tool: Chrome Extension Content Script Error Handling
description: A content script catch block that only logs and returns leaves the sidebar in its last known state with no indication that extraction failed. The sidebar has no independent timeout or health-check — it waits for a READING_CHUNK message. If none arrives, the UI appears frozen (the 'Discuss this' button stays visible but the conversation never starts). Every early-return path in the extraction pipeline, including both catch blocks and quality-gate failures, must call sendFailureChunk() before returning so the sidebar can render its fallback CTA instead of staying silent. Gotcha: The original catch blocks around getStrategy() and strategy.extract() logged the error but silently returned — the sidebar received nothing and the user saw no feedback.
comparison: A catch block that only logs and returns silently leaves the UI frozen, whereas one that calls sendFailureChunk() before returning allows the sidebar to render a fallback CTA and give the user visible feedback.
use_cases: Any Chrome extension content script that communicates extraction or processing results to a sidebar or background UI, particularly where the UI depends on receiving a message to transition out of a loading or pending state.

tool: Cloudflare Worker KV Fail-Open Policy
description: In a paid proxy flow, KV stores hold entitlement state (free trial count, token balance, unlimited flag). If a KV read throws — due to a Cloudflare outage or misconfigured binding — rejecting the user request would make the entire product unavailable whenever KV is degraded. Fail-open means: wrap every KV read in try/catch, log the error, and allow the request to proceed as if the user has a valid entitlement. Set a response header (X-Entitlement-Source: fallback) so you can detect and monitor fail-open events without breaking the user experience. Gotcha: Fail-open on KV read does NOT mean fail-open on KV write — failed writes after a successful Anthropic response should still be logged, but the user already got their response so there is nothing to roll back.
comparison: Fail-open on KV read prioritizes availability over strict entitlement enforcement, whereas a fail-closed approach would reject requests during KV degradation and make the product entirely unavailable; fail-open on reads does not extend to writes, which are logged on failure but not retried.
use_cases: Cloudflare Worker-based paid proxy services where entitlement state is stored in KV and resilience to KV outages or misconfigured bindings is required without sacrificing user experience.

tool: SSE Stream Tee + ctx.waitUntil() for Optimistic Deduction Correction
description: When proxying a streaming Anthropic response, the actual token count is only known at the end of the stream (in `message_delta` usage events). To avoid blocking the stream, deduct a flat estimate up front (optimistic deduction), forward the live stream to the client immediately via branch A of a ReadableStream tee, and consume branch B in a ctx.waitUntil() background task that parses the SSE events to get actual usage. After the stream completes, correct the balance to estimated - actual. ctx.waitUntil() keeps the Worker alive after the response is sent so the correction can finish without blocking the client. Gotcha: Only ctx.waitUntil() keeps the Worker alive post-response. A bare setTimeout or unawaited promise would be killed when the response completes. Pattern: stream.tee() → branch A piped to new Response(...) → branch B piped to ctx.waitUntil(parseAndCorrect(branchB))
