comparison: Using ctx.waitUntil() vs bare setTimeout or unawaited promise: ctx.waitUntil() guarantees the Worker stays alive post-response until the background task completes, while setTimeout and unawaited promises are killed as soon as the response is sent.
use_cases: Proxying streaming LLM responses where token usage is only known at stream end, enabling optimistic balance deduction with post-stream correction without blocking the client stream.

tool: chrome.storage.session vs chrome.storage.local in MV3
description: chrome.storage.local persists across browser restarts and extension reloads, making it the right store for user config, API keys, highlights, and accumulated analytics. chrome.storage.session is scoped to the browser session: it survives service worker idle-kill and restart within the same browser session but is cleared on browser close, making it the right store for ephemeral in-session data like a rolling context window. Gotcha: Service worker restart is not the same as browser restart. The SW is killed after ~30s idle but session storage survives, whereas in-memory JS module variables do not survive SW kill and must be re-read from session storage on restart.
comparison: chrome.storage.local persists indefinitely across browser restarts and SW lifecycle events, while chrome.storage.session persists only within the current browser session and survives SW idle-kills but is wiped on browser close.
use_cases: Using chrome.storage.local for durable user data such as API keys and highlights in a Chrome MV3 extension, and chrome.storage.session for ephemeral per-session data like a rolling reading context window that should reset on fresh browser launch.

tool: Exposing Internal Promises on Module Public APIs
description: When a module performs async initialization (e.g., reading from storage) and stores the result in module-level variables, callers that run immediately after the module loads may read before init completes. The clean fix — without making all getters async — is to expose the initialization promise itself as a named export (e.g., `_hydratePromise`). Callers that need to guarantee init is complete can `await` it once before their first read; callers that only run after init (e.g., message handlers triggered by user action) can skip the await. The underscore prefix signals it's an internal implementation detail, not a primary API surface. Example: `const _hydratePromise = chrome.storage.session.get(...).then(data => { _chunks = data... })` → `self.RC_Context = { ..., _hydratePromise }` → caller: `await RC_Context._hydratePromise; RC_Context.getChunks()`
comparison: Compared to making all getters async, exposing the init promise is less invasive — callers opt into awaiting only when necessary, avoiding async overhead on every read.
use_cases: Useful in Chrome extension background scripts or service workers where module-level async hydration from storage must complete before certain callers read shared state, without requiring every accessor to be async.

tool: Structured Three-Part AI Response Parsing with Fallback
description: Asking the model to emit explicit section markers (`[OBSERVATION]`, `[INSIGHT]`, `[QUESTION]`) makes splitting a response into UI sections deterministic — scan for the markers, extract the text between them, render each section independently. The fallback for responses that don't contain markers (short follow-up turns) is sentence-boundary splitting: split on `. ` and distribute sentences across the three slots. Two quality guards woven into every system prompt prevent the two most common failure modes: the insight restating the article verbatim, and the question having a correct answer. Gotcha: marker-based parsing breaks if the model wraps markers in markdown formatting (e.g., `**[OBSERVATION]**`); prompts must explicitly forbid markdown inside the three-part block.
comparison: Compared to free-form response parsing or regex-only approaches, explicit section markers provide deterministic splitting, while sentence-boundary fallback gracefully handles short turns where markers are absent.
use_cases: Applicable in Chrome extensions or AI-powered reading tools that need to render structured model responses into distinct UI sections, particularly where consistent formatting across multi-turn conversations is required.

tool: Session-Scoped State Reset on New Conversation
description: In-memory variables that accumulate session state (_sessionTurnCount, _lastRecordedTurns, _sessionPersona, _conversationLog) must be explicitly reset at the start of each new conversation, not just on page load. If the user clicks 'Discuss this' again mid-session (starting a new exchange on the same page), the state from the previous conversation carries over and corrupts counters and analytics. The reset point is the _handleDiscussCta function — the one user action that definitively starts a new conversation — not the page navigation event. Gotcha: Page navigation triggers a new READING_CHUNK but does not necessarily trigger a new conversation (the user may not click 'Discuss this'). Reset on user intent, not on chunk arrival.
comparison: Resetting on page load or chunk arrival is insufficient compared to resetting on explicit user intent via _handleDiscussCta, because the user may navigate pages without starting a new conversation, leaving stale state to corrupt subsequent sessions.
use_cases: Chrome extension conversation lifecycle management, analytics counter integrity across multiple conversations on the same page, avoiding state bleed in single-page or chunk-based reading applications where multiple conversations can occur without full page reloads.

tool: Cloudflare KV TTL
description: Cloudflare KV's native TTL is a fixed expiry set at write time — it does not reset when the key is read or overwritten unless you explicitly pass a new TTL. To implement sliding expiry (e.g. 'expire after 30 days of inactivity'), you must store a lastActivity timestamp in the value and rewrite the key with a fresh TTL on every access. Each write must recalculate and pass a new expirationTtl.
comparison: Sliding TTL (common expectation) resets on read or write; Cloudflare KV TTL is absolute and only updates when a new TTL is explicitly passed on write.
use_cases: Session expiry based on inactivity windows; user trial periods that should lapse only after genuine dormancy; any key that should survive as long as the user remains active.

tool: Three-namespace KV Pattern
description: Separating entitlement data into three distinct KV bindings (FREE_TRIAL, TOKEN_BALANCE, UNLIMITED) rather than one namespace makes access patterns self-documenting, allows independent retention policies per type, and prevents key collisions. Each binding is declared separately in wrangler.toml and injected as env.FREE_TRIAL etc.
comparison: Single namespace with prefixes works but creates implicit coupling; three-namespace pattern isolates concerns and allows independent policy per entitlement type.
use_cases: SaaS entitlement management; tiered subscription access control; billing systems where different credit types have different lifecycles.

tool: Chrome Web Store Payments API
description: Google discontinued the Chrome Web Store Payments API. Extensions can no longer use the built-in google.payments.inapp.buy() flow. Any monetised extension must use a third-party provider (Stripe, Gumroad, Lemon Squeezy) and handle purchase verification independently. Referencing the old CWS API in new code will produce runtime errors.
comparison: Old CWS Payments API was built-in and simple but is now deprecated; third-party providers require independent verification but are the only viable path.
use_cases: Monetising Chrome extensions; in-app purchases for browser extensions; recurring subscription billing for extension users.

tool: chrome.runtime.id
description: chrome.runtime.id is fixed for a given extension published on the Chrome Web Store — it matches the CWS listing ID and does not change across installs or updates on the same browser profile. It cannot be spoofed by end users. The main abuse vector it does not cover is developers side-loading a modified copy with a different ID.
comparison: HMAC-signing the extension ID adds complexity without meaningfully blocking realistic threats for a Web Store extension; plain runtime.id is sufficient for most anti-abuse use cases.
use_cases: Identifying extension instances for entitlement checks; associating purchases with a specific extension install; lightweight abuse prevention for Web Store extensions.

tool: chrome.identity.launchWebAuthFlow
description: launchWebAuthFlow opens a controlled popup and resolves when the page redirects to chrome.identity.getRedirectURL(). It was designed for OAuth flows. Payment providers do not redirect to that URL by default, so using it for Stripe or Gumroad checkouts requires the provider to support arbitrary redirect URIs — which most hosted checkouts don't. For payments, the correct pattern is chrome.tabs.create, then poll a check endpoint and resolve on confirmation.
comparison: launchWebAuthFlow is purpose-built for OAuth and works cleanly for identity; it is not suitable for payment flows that lack redirect URI control.
use_cases: OAuth login flows in Chrome extensions; any flow where the provider supports chrome.identity.getRedirectURL() as a redirect target.

tool: HMAC-SHA256 via Web Crypto API (SubtleCrypto)
description: Cloudflare Workers use the Web Crypto API, not Node's crypto module. To compute HMAC-SHA256: import the secret key with crypto.subtle.importKey, then call crypto.subtle.sign with algorithm {name:'HMAC', hash:'SHA-256'}. The result is an ArrayBuffer that must be hex-encoded for string comparison. Timing-safe comparison must be used instead of === to prevent timing attacks.
comparison: Node crypto module has a synchronous createHmac interface; Web Crypto API is fully async and Promise-based, requiring await on both importKey and sign.
use_cases: Webhook signature verification in Cloudflare Workers; request authentication between services; any HMAC-based integrity check in a Workers environment.

tool: Timing-Safe String Comparison
description: Regular string comparison short-circuits on the first mismatched character, leaking information about how many leading characters are correct via response timing. Timing-safe comparison XORs all corresponding byte values and checks the result is zero, always taking the same time regardless of where strings diverge. Both strings must be the same length before byte comparison; length checks do not need to be timing-safe because the correct length is not secret.
comparison: Standard === comparison is fast but vulnerable to timing side-channel attacks; XOR accumulation comparison is constant-time but requires pre-checked equal lengths.
use_cases: HMAC signature verification; API key comparison; any server-side secret comparison where an attacker could measure response latency.

tool: Optimistic Deduction and Correction Pattern
description: When a proxied API call's cost is unknown until the response arrives, deduct a flat estimate upfront to block concurrent over-spending. Once the response returns with actual usage, perform a correction write: correctedBalance = currentBalance + flatDeduction - actualTokens. This refunds over-estimates and charges under-estimates without holding a lock across the API call. If the correction write fails after a successful API response, the user's balance is slightly inflated — fail open rather than charging users for infrastructure errors.
comparison: Locking the balance across the full API call prevents over-spending but introduces latency and complexity; optimistic deduction with correction is lock-free and faster at the cost of possible minor balance drift on infrastructure failure.
use_cases: Token-based billing for LLM API proxies; any metered usage system where cost is only known after the operation completes; concurrent request billing without distributed locks.

tool: Fail-Open vs Fail-Closed for KV Failures
description: When a dependency like KV is unavailable mid-request, fail open (allow the request, risk brief untracked access) or fail closed (return error, risk blocking paying users). Subscription and trial products generally prefer fail-open — a short untracked window costs less than blocking customers during an outage. Every KV read should be wrapped in try/catch; on error, log and attach X-Entitlement-Source: fallback header and allow the request through.
comparison: Fail-closed guarantees no untracked access but blocks legitimate paying users during outages; fail-open maintains availability at the cost of potential brief unmetered usage.
use_cases: Entitlement checks in SaaS proxies; rate limiting middleware; any access control layer where uptime is more critical than perfect metering accuracy.

tool: Server-Side-Only KV Writes
description: If the extension can call a /deduct-tokens endpoint directly, any user can forge requests to underpay. Removing that endpoint and making all KV writes happen in the proxy worker means the trusted server controls all balance state. The extension only reads what the server returns via response headers — it has no write authority over billing data.
comparison: Client-callable deduction endpoints are simpler to implement but trivially spoofable; server-side-only writes require routing all billable traffic through the proxy but eliminate client spoofing entirely.
use_cases: Token billing for browser extension AI proxies; any metered API where the client must not be trusted to report its own usage; server-authoritative billing architectures.

tool: Gumroad Webhook Authentication
description: Gumroad signs webhook POST bodies with HMAC-SHA256 using a seller-specific secret, delivered in the X-Gumroad-Signature header. Custom fields set at checkout are included in the payload. The secret is stored as a Cloudflare Worker secret via wrangler secret put. Gumroad sends the webhook body as application/x-www-form-urlencoded, not JSON — parse with new URLSearchParams(body), not JSON.parse.
comparison: Gumroad webhooks use form-encoded bodies unlike most modern providers that use JSON; custom fields use bracket notation keys and must be accessed with literal bracket strings.
use_cases: Purchase verification for Gumroad-sold Chrome extensions; provisioning entitlements after a Gumroad sale; linking a purchase to a specific extension install via custom fields.

tool: Payment Callback Polling Pattern
description: Chrome extensions cannot intercept redirects from external payment pages, so the standard pattern is to open the payment URL in a new tab, then poll a lightweight /check-purchase endpoint from background.js at a fixed interval. Poll every 3–5 seconds, time out after 10 minutes, and stop immediately if the user closes the payment tab via chrome.tabs.onRemoved. Do not send PURCHASE_TIMEOUT when the user closes the tab manually — only timeout after the full polling window elapses.
comparison: launchWebAuthFlow handles redirect interception natively for OAuth but does not work for payment providers; tab-plus-polling is more universal but requires a server-side confirmation endpoint.
use_cases: Post-payment entitlement activation in Chrome extensions; confirming Gumroad or Stripe purchases from a background service worker; any flow where an external page must signal completion back to an extension.

tool: Activity-Based vs firstSeen-Based Trial Reset
description: Resetting a trial based on firstSeen punishes users who signed up and forgot. Resetting based on lastActivity targets genuinely lapsed users — someone inactive for 90 days is unlikely to be an active abuser. Active users, including those who hit the limit and kept retrying, never qualify because each attempt updates lastActivity. lastActivity must be updated on every request, including from exhausted trial users, to ensure the 90-day window never opens for someone actively trying to game the limit.
comparison: firstSeen-based reset is simpler but penalises casual users and is easier to game; lastActivity-based reset is more fair and closes the dormancy abuse loophole.
use_cases: Free trial expiry policies for SaaS products; recycling dormant trial accounts; abuse-resistant credit reset logic.

tool: Typed Errors for UI-Specific Handling
description: Attaching a .code property to thrown errors lets UI layers respond specifically without parsing message strings. The catch block in sidebar.js can branch on err.code to show different CTAs (upgrade vs buy tokens) without duplicating string matching logic or tightly coupling the API layer to UI copy. Pattern: throw Object.assign(new Error(msg), { code: 'TRIAL_EXHAUSTED' }).
comparison: Parsing error message strings in the UI is fragile and couples UI to API wording; typed error codes decouple UI presentation from API internals and are refactor-safe.
use_cases: Multi-state error UIs in browser extensions; distinguishing trial exhaustion from token exhaustion from network errors; any layered architecture where errors must cross module boundaries cleanly.

tool: Client-Sent Headers as Informational-Only
description: Any header sent by the client (like X-Request-Type: trial) can be forged. These headers should only be used for logging and analytics on the server — never for access control or billing logic. The server must derive entitlement state from its own trusted storage, not from what the client claims about itself.
comparison: Using client headers for access control is simple but trivially bypassable; server-side entitlement derivation requires more infrastructure but cannot be spoofed.
use_cases: Request tagging for analytics pipelines; debug logging in proxy workers; any scenario where client context is useful for observability but must not influence security decisions.

tool: Token Counting in Streaming SSE Responses
description: When Anthropic returns a streaming response via Server-Sent Events, the usage object with input_tokens and output_tokens appears in the final message_delta event — not in every chunk. A Cloudflare Worker proxy must buffer the stream to extract this event before performing accurate token deduction. If buffering exceeds the Worker's memory budget, fall back to flat deduction and log the degradation. Non-streaming responses have usage in the top-level body; streaming responses have it in the last SSE event — these require different parsing paths in the same worker.
comparison: Non-streaming responses provide usage in the top-level JSON body; streaming SSE responses require buffering all chunks and parsing the final message_delta event to find usage data.
use_cases: Accurate token billing for proxied Anthropic streaming requests; metered LLM API proxies; any worker that must extract metadata from the tail of a streamed response.

tool: Write Tool Silent Truncation Workaround
description: The Write tool silently truncates files beyond roughly 191 lines (~7 KB) — it returns success but the file on disk is incomplete. The truncation always happens at the same byte boundary, making it a hard limit rather than flakiness. The only reliable workaround for large files is to write them via python3 inside a bash call: open the path, call f.write(content), close. The tool reports success even when it truncates, so the failure only surfaces when subsequent reads or vm.Script evals fail with SyntaxError: Unexpected end of input.
comparison: Write tool is convenient for small files but silently corrupts large ones; python3 bash write is more verbose but reliable for any file size.
use_cases: Writing large generated files in Cowork or Claude Code environments; any automated file generation exceeding ~150 lines; workaround for sandboxed tooling with hidden size limits.

tool: Testing ESM Cloudflare Workers with vm.createContext
description: Cloudflare Workers use ESM syntax (export default { async fetch() {} }) which Node's vm module cannot evaluate directly. The fix is a source transform: replace 'export default ' with 'self.workerExport = ' via regex before passing the source to vm.Script. The sandbox object is assigned to both self and window so the assignment is captured. All Fetch API globals must be injected into the sandbox explicitly from Node 18+ globalThis. The regex must use the multiline flag and anchor with ^ to only match a real top-level export statement.
comparison: Running Workers in Miniflare provides a full runtime environment but is heavier; vm.createContext is lightweight and fast but requires manual global injection and a source transform.
use_cases: Unit testing Cloudflare Workers in Node without Miniflare; fast CI test suites for worker logic; testing worker fetch handlers with mocked KV and fetch dependencies.

tool: Mutable Fetch Proxy Pattern for Worker Tests
description: When testing a worker loaded once into a vm sandbox, individual tests cannot swap the fetch mock by reloading the worker or reassigning sandbox.fetch because the worker captured the original reference at eval time. The solution is a mutable proxy: define 'let fetchProxy = defaultMock' in the test file, inject 'fetch: (...args) => fetchProxy(...args)' into the sandbox, then assign fetchProxy inside each test. The indirection means every call goes through the current value of fetchProxy at call time. Injecting fetch: fetchProxy directly (not wrapped) captures the value at injection time and makes per-test reassignment ineffective.
comparison: Reloading the worker per test is clean but expensive; mutable proxy pattern is lightweight and allows per-test mock swapping without re-evaluation.
use_cases: Per-test fetch mocking in vm-sandboxed worker test suites; simulating different Anthropic API responses per test case; any test environment where a shared sandbox must support per-test dependency injection.

tool: Per-User KV Entitlement Model
description: The per-user model uses three separate KV namespaces — FREE_TRIAL, TOKEN_BALANCE, UNLIMITED — each keyed by extension ID. Priority is checked in order: UNLIMITED first, then TOKEN_BALANCE, then FREE_TRIAL, then reject. A user can hold multiple entitlements simultaneously; the highest-tier one wins. Separating namespaces rather than storing all fields on one key keeps reads cheap and avoids read-modify-write races between tiers.
comparison: Global daily counter is simple but cannot distinguish users or tiers; per-user namespace model is more complex but supports tiered entitlements, independent policies, and cheap priority-ordered reads.
use_cases: Tiered SaaS access control; multi-plan subscription management; entitlement systems where users may hold concurrent plan types.

tool: Optimistic Token Deduction with Post-Response Correction (KV Memory Optimisation)
description: The worker deducts a flat estimate (default 2500 tokens) from the token balance before forwarding to Anthropic. After the Anthropic response streams back, the balance is corrected using: correctedBalance = tokensRemaining + tokensDeducted - actualTokens. tokensRemaining and tokensDeducted are already in memory from the initial entitlement check, so the correction avoids a second KV read. The correction write happens in ctx.waitUntil() — if the worker is terminated early the correction is lost and the user keeps the optimistic over-credited balance.
comparison: Two KV reads (before and after) would be accurate but costly; in-memory correction from already-loaded values is faster and avoids a round-trip at the cost of relying on ctx.waitUntil reliability.
use_cases: Token billing in LLM proxy workers; any metered system where the billable quantity is only known after the operation completes; high-throughput workers where minimising KV reads matters.

tool: ReadableStream tee() for Dual Consumption
description: A ReadableStream can only be consumed once. To both forward the Anthropic SSE stream to the client and parse it for token usage, call response.body.tee() which returns [branchA, branchB]. Branch A is passed directly to the client response. Branch B is consumed in a ctx.waitUntil() promise that reads chunks and extracts usage events. The two branches are internally buffered by the runtime — backpressure on branch B does not stall branch A delivery to the client. If branch B is slow or errors, the buffer can grow unboundedly, so branch B errors must always be handled.
comparison: Buffering the entire response body before forwarding is simpler but adds latency; tee() allows simultaneous forwarding and parsing with no added client latency.
use_cases: Streaming LLM proxy workers that need to extract metadata from SSE responses; any worker that must both forward and inspect a streaming response body.

tool: ctx.waitUntil() for Post-Response Async Work
description: Once a Cloudflare Worker's fetch handler returns a Response, the runtime may terminate the isolate. Any async work that needs to run after the response is sent must be registered with ctx.waitUntil(promise). The runtime keeps the isolate alive until all waitUntil promises settle. waitUntil does not catch errors — if the promise rejects silently the KV write is lost with no user-visible indication. Always add a .catch() to the promise passed to waitUntil.
comparison: Performing async work before returning the response keeps it in the main request path but adds latency; ctx.waitUntil allows responding immediately and doing bookkeeping after, at the cost of losing work if the promise rejects silently.
use_cases: Post-response KV balance corrections; fire-and-forget analytics writes; any side effect that should not block the client response in a Cloudflare Worker.

tool: HMAC-SHA256 Webhook Verification with SubtleCrypto
description: Cloudflare Workers have no Node crypto module. Webhook HMAC verification uses the Web Crypto API exclusively. The pattern is: crypto.subtle.importKey with raw secret and HMAC/SHA-256 algorithm, then crypto.subtle.sign, then convert the ArrayBuffer to a lowercase hex string via Array.from(new Uint8Array(buf)).map(b => b.toString(16).padStart(2,'0')).join(''), then compare against the webhook header value using timing-safe comparison. Both importKey and sign are async — forgetting await on either produces a [object Promise] string that will never match.
comparison: Node crypto.createHmac is synchronous and simpler; SubtleCrypto is async and more verbose but is the only available option in Cloudflare Workers.
use_cases: Gumroad webhook signature verification; any webhook HMAC validation in a Cloudflare Worker; request integrity checks between trusted services in a Workers environment.

tool: Timing-Safe Comparison via XOR Accumulation
description: Standard string equality short-circuits on the first differing character, leaking information about how many leading characters match via response time differences. The safe pattern accumulates XOR differences across the full string without early exit: let result = 0; for each index, result |= a.charCodeAt(i) ^ b.charCodeAt(i); return result === 0. Length must be verified first; rejecting on length mismatch is safe because HMAC hex output length is not secret.
comparison: === is O(1) best case but leaks timing information; XOR accumulation is O(n) always and constant-time, preventing timing side-channel attacks.
use_cases: HMAC webhook signature comparison; API key validation; any server-side secret comparison exposed to potential timing measurement.

tool: Gumroad Webhook Payload Format
description: Gumroad sends webhook POST bodies as application/x-www-form-urlencoded, not JSON. Custom product fields use bracket notation in the form key: custom_fields[extension_id]. Parse with new URLSearchParams(await request.text()) then read with params.get('custom_fields[extension_id]') — the bracket characters are literal parts of the key name. The product permalink is available as params.get('permalink') and is the canonical way to identify which product was purchased. Trying to parse with request.json() will throw.
comparison: Most modern webhook providers use JSON bodies; Gumroad uses form-encoded bodies with bracket-notation custom field keys, requiring URLSearchParams parsing instead of JSON.parse.
use_cases: Gumroad purchase webhook handlers; provisioning entitlements from Gumroad sales events; extracting custom checkout fields like extension ID from Gumroad payloads.

tool: 90-Day Inactivity Reset Logic for Free Trials
description: The free trial reset rule requires two independent conditions: used < total (user is not exhausted) AND lastActivity is older than 90 days. Exhausted users are never reset regardless of inactivity — this prevents abuse where a user could deliberately go dormant to recycle free credits. lastActivity is always updated on every request, even for exhausted users, so the inactivity window is measured from the most recent attempt. Updating lastActivity for exhausted users closes the loophole of an exhausted user waiting 90 days and then immediately qualifying for reset.
comparison: Resetting all users after 90 days of inactivity regardless of exhaustion state is simpler but exploitable; requiring non-exhaustion as an additional condition closes the deliberate dormancy abuse vector.
use_cases: Free trial credit recycling policies; abuse-resistant trial reset logic; inactivity-based account lifecycle management.

tool: git show HEAD:<path> for File Recovery
description: git checkout HEAD -- <file> overwrites the working tree copy directly, which can be blocked by filesystem permissions in sandboxed environments. The workaround is to pipe the blob content to stdout first, then copy it: git show HEAD:<relative-path> > /tmp/restore.js && cp /tmp/restore.js <destination>. The git show command reads the object from the git database without touching the working tree, bypassing the permission check on the destination path.
comparison: git checkout HEAD -- <file> is the standard recovery command but modifies the working tree directly and can be blocked by permissions; git show pipes content without touching the destination until the explicit cp step.
use_cases: File recovery in sandboxed CI or development environments; restoring accidentally modified files when checkout is permission-blocked; extracting historical file versions without checkout.

tool: wrangler .dev.vars for Local Secrets
description: When running wrangler dev locally, secrets like ANTHROPIC_API_KEY are not available from deployed KV bindings. Wrangler reads them from a file named .dev.vars in the worker directory, formatted as KEY=VALUE pairs identical to .env format. This file must be in .gitignore as it contains plaintext API keys. It is the local equivalent of wrangler secret put and is not created automatically — developers must create it manually after cloning the repo.
comparison: wrangler secret put stores secrets in Cloudflare's encrypted secret store for deployed workers; .dev.vars is the local-only equivalent that is read only by wrangler dev and must never be committed.
use_cases: Local development of Cloudflare Workers that depend on API keys; onboarding new developers to a Workers project; separating local secrets from deployed secret management.

tool: .wrangler/ vs .dev.vars gitignore
description: The .wrangler/ build cache directory is commonly gitignored, but .dev.vars is a separate file that does not live inside .wrangler/ and must be gitignored explicitly.
comparison: Unlike .wrangler/ which may be covered by a directory-level gitignore rule, .dev.vars requires its own explicit entry
use_cases: Cloudflare Workers projects using local environment variable files that must not be committed to version control

tool: Edit/Write tools — Unicode truncation bug
description: The Edit and Write tools silently truncate files when old_string or new_string contains certain Unicode characters such as box-drawing chars (U+2500) or em dashes (U+2014). The file is left in a broken half-written state at the byte position of the first problematic character, with no error message. The bug triggers even on the OLD content being matched, not only on inserted content.
comparison: Standard text editors handle these Unicode characters without truncation; the Edit/Write tool environment is uniquely vulnerable at the byte level
use_cases: Debugging silent file corruption in AI-assisted editing workflows; repairing partially written source files using Python byte-level open(path, 'rb') anchor-and-truncate patterns

tool: CRLF vs LF — Windows file byte-level repair in Python
description: Windows text files use CRLF line endings while Python on Linux defaults to LF. When repairing a Windows file with Python byte-level append, writing bare \n produces mixed line endings that can break downstream parsers or show as ^M characters. Opening with text mode may silently translate line endings in a platform-dependent way.
comparison: Text-mode file opens in Python handle line endings automatically but unpredictably across platforms; binary mode with explicit b'\r\n' is deterministic
use_cases: Repairing Windows-origin source files on Linux CI systems; byte-level file patching scripts that must produce valid Windows line endings

tool: Python byte-level file repair — anchor-and-truncate pattern
description: Safe file repair procedure for silent-truncation corruption: (1) open in 'rb' and load all bytes, (2) find the last known-good ASCII-only anchor using bytes.find() or bytes.rfind(), (3) open in 'wb' and write bytes up to and including the anchor, (4) append the correct tail as a bytestring literal. The anchor must be entirely ASCII to avoid re-triggering Unicode truncation.
comparison: Text-mode repair risks re-triggering the truncation bug on any Unicode character in the match string; binary-mode anchor-and-truncate avoids this entirely
use_cases: Recovering files corrupted by AI edit tool Unicode truncation; any scenario requiring deterministic byte-level file patching without a build pipeline

tool: MV3 service worker as sole origin for Anthropic API calls
description: In Chrome Manifest V3, fetch requests to Anthropic's API must originate from the service worker (background.js). Browser page and content-script contexts are blocked by CORS preflight and by Anthropic's server-side browser-detection check. The service worker bypasses CORS for URLs in host_permissions but still requires the header 'anthropic-dangerous-direct-browser-access: true' because it emits an Origin: chrome-extension://[id] header that the server treats as a browser context.
comparison: Content scripts and sidebar page contexts are rejected regardless of API key validity; service worker is the only viable fetch origin in MV3
use_cases: Chrome MV3 extensions that call the Anthropic API directly; any extension architecture needing to proxy AI API calls from sidebar to background

tool: chrome.runtime.sendMessage — return true for async response
description: The chrome.runtime.onMessage listener must synchronously return the literal true from the callback itself (not from an inner async function, and not a Promise) if sendResponse will be called asynchronously. If the listener returns undefined or false, Chrome closes the message channel immediately and any later sendResponse is a no-op.
comparison: Returning a Promise from the onMessage handler does not keep the channel open, unlike Node.js-style async patterns; only the literal boolean true works
use_cases: Any MV3 extension handler that awaits an async operation before calling sendResponse; CALL_CLAUDE message routing from sidebar to background

tool: Sidebar-side vs content-script-side module placement in Chrome extensions
description: Detection modules that consume sidebar state (rolling context window, session trajectory) must be loaded in sidebar.html via script tags, not injected as content scripts. Content scripts run in the page execution context and can only reach sidebar variables via message passing. Rule of thumb: if a module consumes sidebar state, load it in the sidebar; if it produces data for the sidebar, inject it as a content script.
comparison: Content scripts have isolated execution context with no direct variable access to sidebar; sidebar scripts share the same JS heap as other sidebar-loaded scripts
use_cases: Architecting Chrome MV3 extensions with stateful detection modules; placing source_switch.js, mismatch_detector.js, and suggestions.js correctly in sidebar.html

tool: Stateful IIFE module scope for cross-poll persistence
description: An IIFE creates a private scope whose variables persist in memory for the lifetime of the containing page. Variables like _lastSeenDomain declared at IIFE scope persist across every invocation of exported functions, enabling module-level state without global namespace pollution. IIFE state resets when the sidebar is closed and reopened because the HTML page reloads and the script re-executes from scratch.
comparison: Global variables pollute the window namespace and risk collision; module-level let/const in ES modules requires a bundler; IIFEs provide encapsulation with no build step
use_cases: Stateful domain tracking across sequential poll cycles in a Chrome extension sidebar; any no-build browser module needing persistent-across-calls but scoped state

tool: Multi-layer suggestion fatigue prevention
description: Preventing recurring UI nudges from becoming noise requires multiple independent gates each targeting a different failure mode: a minimum-evidence threshold (MIN_CHUNKS=3), a per-type in-session cooldown keyed by mismatch type, a session-level cross-type cooldown (30 min), and a source-switch early return that skips the mismatch check entirely for the affected poll cycle. No single gate is sufficient because each catches a distinct failure scenario.
comparison: A single global cooldown prevents same-type repeat but allows two different types to fire back-to-back; per-type gates alone allow same-type recurrence after cooldown with stale conditions
use_cases: Chrome extension UX systems surfacing adaptive reading suggestions; any notification or nudge system where multiple suggestion types compete for user attention

tool: Already-at-target guard — defensive programming for logically redundant conditions
description: For the current two mismatch cases the already-at-target check is logically redundant due to mutual exclusivity of detection conditions and suggestion targets. The guard is still added because future suggestion types may not share this property, it catches cross-session edge cases where cooldown has expired and config was already corrected in a prior session, and it makes intent explicit rather than relying on an implicit property of the current case set.
comparison: Implicit safety relies on a property of the current case set and breaks the moment a new case is added; an explicit guard remains correct regardless of future additions
use_cases: Any suggestion or action system where the set of cases may grow over time; defensive programming in stateful extension components with cross-session side effects

tool: chrome.storage.session vs chrome.storage.local — lifecycle and use
description: chrome.storage.session persists for the current browser session and is cleared on browser close. chrome.storage.local persists indefinitely across restarts. The rolling context window belongs in session storage because stale prior-session context is misleading. User config and highlights belong in local storage. The service worker can be killed and restarted within a session, so _hydratePromise re-reads session storage on every SW startup and all async handlers await it before using in-memory state.
comparison: session storage is automatically invalidated on browser close making it self-cleaning for transient data; local storage requires explicit cleanup and is appropriate only for intentional long-lived data
use_cases: Persisting rolling reading context across SW kill/restart cycles; storing user config and highlights durably across browser restarts in MV3 extensions

tool: SOFT_RESET_CONTEXT — coordinating state wipes across extension components
description: When the sidebar detects a source-domain switch it sends a SOFT_RESET_CONTEXT message to background.js which calls RC_Context.clear() wiping both in-memory array and chrome.storage.session. The sidebar simultaneously clears its own _latestChunks and returns early from _pollContext() to skip that cycle's mismatch check. Soft reset leaves user config, highlights, and chat history untouched — only the transient context window is cleared.
comparison: A hard reset would wipe all extension state including config and highlights; soft reset scopes destruction to only the transient reading context
use_cases: Handling domain switches in a Chrome reading assistant extension; any multi-component extension architecture requiring coordinated partial state invalidation

tool: Detecting domain switches with stateful sidebar polling
description: The source_switch module tracks _lastSeenDomain in IIFE scope across 15-second GET_CONTEXT polls. Each poll extracts the hostname from the most recent chunk URL, compares to the stored value, updates it, and returns switched:true if they differ. The first call always returns switched:false. Empty arrays and failed chunks are treated as no-change. The www. prefix is stripped before comparison to prevent spurious switches between www and non-www variants of the same domain.
comparison: Polling-based domain detection requires explicit stale-state handling on sidebar reload; an event-based approach via chrome.tabs.onUpdated would fire immediately but requires additional permissions
use_cases: Source-switch detection in a Chrome reading assistant; any sidebar polling architecture needing to identify when the user has moved to a different site

tool: chrome.contextMenus.removeAll() before create() — preventing duplicate ID errors on SW restart
description: Service workers can be killed and restarted between onInstalled events. Calling chrome.contextMenus.create() with a previously registered ID throws a duplicate-ID error. The fix is to call chrome.contextMenus.removeAll() inside onInstalled before creating items to make create() idempotent. removeAll() is asynchronous so the create() call must be nested inside its callback, not placed sequentially after it.
comparison: Wrapping create() in a try/catch is a common but fragile alternative; removeAll() before create() is idempotent and does not depend on knowing which IDs already exist
use_cases: Any MV3 extension that registers context menu items in onInstalled; making SW restart safe without tracking previously registered menu IDs

tool: Service worker kill/restart lifecycle — implications for in-memory state
description: Chrome MV3 service workers are ephemeral and may be killed after ~30 seconds of inactivity. All module-scope in-memory state is lost on kill. State that must survive SW restarts within a session should use chrome.storage.session; state that must survive browser restarts should use chrome.storage.local. RC_Context uses session storage and _hydratePromise re-reads it on every SW startup before any handler runs to prevent a race where GET_CONTEXT returns an empty array before hydration.
comparison: In-memory module-scope variables are fast but ephemeral; storage-backed state adds async overhead but survives the SW lifecycle
use_cases: Any MV3 extension maintaining rolling state across SW activations; designing hydration promises to guard against stale reads immediately after SW restart

tool: Partial config writes with a delta object
description: chrome.storage.local.set() merges the provided object into the existing store rather than overwriting it entirely. handleApplySuggestion() builds a delta from only the keys present in the suggestion payload, leaving unrelated config keys untouched. Each key is explicitly checked before being added to the delta because passing a key with an undefined value will write undefined into storage and corrupt the stored value.
comparison: Writing the full config object on every update risks overwriting keys that were not intended to change and requires reading the full config first; delta writes are safer and cheaper
use_cases: Applying partial configuration changes from suggestion chips in a Chrome extension; any storage update pattern where only a subset of stored keys should be modified

tool: Patching in-memory config immediately after successful storage write
description: When a suggestion is applied, background.js writes the new strategy to chrome.storage.local but the sidebar's _config object loaded at startup is not automatically updated. Without an explicit patch the sidebar continues detecting mismatches based on stale config until reload. The patch must happen inside the success callback of the APPLY_SUGGESTION message response, not before it, to keep in-memory and stored state in sync if the write fails.
comparison: Relying on a storage change listener (onChanged) would also work but adds latency and an extra message cycle; patching directly in the response callback is synchronous and immediate
use_cases: Keeping sidebar UI and mismatch detection in sync after user applies a config-changing suggestion; any MV3 extension pattern where local state must mirror storage writes

tool: _none sentinel pattern in pure-function detectors
description: Both detectSwitch() and detectMismatch() define a local _none object at the top of the function body representing the nothing-to-report result. Every early-return path returns _none or a shallow copy via Object.assign. This ensures a consistent return shape from all exit paths, eliminates null checks for callers, and makes early returns self-documenting — returning _none explicitly communicates conditions not met rather than the ambiguous null.
comparison: Returning null requires callers to null-check before property access; returning a typed sentinel object allows uniform destructuring and makes intent explicit
use_cases: Pure detector functions in Chrome extension sidebar modules; any function with multiple early-return paths that must guarantee a consistent return shape

tool: Suggestion registry as pure data — no side effects in the data layer
description: suggestions.js is a pure data module where the SUGGESTIONS registry is a plain object of descriptors and the lookup functions only read from it. No storage writes, DOM manipulation, or chrome.runtime calls occur in this layer. All side effects are owned by sidebar.js. This separation allows the suggestion logic to be tested in isolation with no mocks and the registry to be extended without touching behavioral code.
comparison: Mixing suggestion lookup with side effects (storage writes, DOM updates) couples the data layer to the runtime environment and makes unit testing require full extension context
use_cases: Building testable suggestion systems in Chrome extensions; any architecture separating data lookup from presentation and side-effect execution

tool: Mirrored domain lists across modules — coupling risk
description: REFERENCE_DOMAINS in suggestions.js and KNOWN_DOMAINS in structured.js both enumerate the same set of platforms and are maintained independently. A domain added to one but not the other creates a subtle inconsistency where extraction uses the structured strategy but no reference_mode suggestion is surfaced (or vice versa). Without a bundler, the practical mitigation is a unit test that imports both lists and asserts they contain the same entries. The comment in suggestions.js is a documentation contract, not an enforced one.
comparison: A single shared module imported by both files would enforce consistency at the cost of requiring a build step; duplicated constants are zero-build but require discipline to keep synchronized
use_cases: No-build Chrome extension architectures with domain lists used in multiple contexts; any project where the same logical constant must exist in two separate execution contexts

tool: No-build browser-side test harness for sidebar-dependent modules
description: Sidebar-side detection modules depend on window globals and cannot be tested in Node without complex mocking. A standalone HTML test file loads the real library scripts via relative script tags, defines test helpers inline, and runs assertions on page load with results rendered as pass/fail DOM cards. No build step, test runner, or npm is required. Script load order must mirror sidebar.html exactly or dependent globals will be undefined when modules initialize.
comparison: Node-based test runners require mocking of window globals, chrome APIs, and DOM; the browser-based harness runs in the real environment but cannot be used in headless CI without a browser driver
use_cases: Testing Chrome extension sidebar modules with real browser globals and no build infrastructure; rapid iterative testing of IIFE-based detection modules during development

tool: Kubernetes Cluster Architecture
description: Visual breakdown of Kubernetes cluster components across control plane and worker nodes. Control plane includes kube-apiserver (port 6443), etcd (port 2379), controller-manager, and kube-scheduler. Worker nodes include kubelet (node agent), containerd (runtime), kube-proxy (iptables/ipvs), and Pods. API requests flow over HTTPS from the control plane to worker nodes via the apiserver.
comparison: Control plane node manages cluster state and scheduling while worker nodes run actual workloads. etcd stores cluster state, kube-apiserver is the central communication hub, whereas kubelet on each worker node acts as the local agent executing instructions from the apiserver.
use_cases: Used as a mental map when troubleshooting cluster outages to identify which layer a failure originates from — whether it is a control plane component like etcd or apiserver, a node-level agent like kubelet, the container runtime like containerd, or network routing via kube-proxy.

error: Kubernetes cluster outage with API server potentially overloaded
root_cause: Aggressive remediation actions such as mass pod deletions, draining many nodes, or restarting critical services can amplify an ongoing outage
fix: Observe first, gather evidence, isolate the failing layer, then make targeted changes only after confirmation
concept: Safe incident response methodology for Kubernetes cluster outages — prioritize observation and evidence gathering before taking action to avoid worsening the situation

error: Cluster outage with unknown blast radius
root_cause: Unclear whether issue originates from control plane (centralized) or nodes/network (distributed)
fix: Before running any command, determine scope: check if whole cluster or specific nodes are affected, whether impact is namespace-scoped or cluster-wide, whether internal or external traffic is disrupted, and whether pods are failing or only API requests
concept: Blast radius assessment in Kubernetes outages — distinguishing control plane issues from node/network issues by scoping the impact across cluster, namespace, traffic type, and workload behavior

error: kubectl intermittently failing during cluster outage
root_cause: Multiple potential causes including API server unhealthy state, etcd health or latency issues, control plane resource exhaustion, worker node health problems, node resource pressure, kubelet or containerd failures, and network or CNI or kube-proxy issues
fix: Follow investigation flow: check API server health with kubectl get --raw='/readyz?verbose', check etcd with etcdctl endpoint status, check control plane resource use with kubectl top pod -n kube-system, check worker nodes with kubectl get/describe nodes, check node resource pressure with top/free -h/iostat/dmesg, verify kubelet and containerd with systemctl status, inspect CNI pod status and conntrack and tcpdump for network issues
concept: Systematic Kubernetes cluster outage investigation flow covering the full stack from API server down to network layer

error: API server unresponsive or degraded during cluster outage
root_cause: Potential API saturation, etcd slowness, webhook failures, or authentication bottlenecks causing subsystem failures in kube-apiserver
fix: Run 'kubectl get --raw='/readyz?verbose'' to send a direct HTTP request to the API server's built-in health endpoint and identify which subsystem is failing via verbose per-subsystem health output
concept: kube-apiserver exposes a /readyz health endpoint that supports verbose mode to surface per-subsystem readiness; healthy state shows fast response and all checks OK, while bad state shows long delays, timeouts, 500 errors, or subsystem failures

error: Cluster outage or degraded control plane performance
root_cause: High resource usage on control plane components such as kube-apiserver saturation, etcd disk/CPU pressure, runaway reconciliation loops in kube-controller-manager, or scheduling backlog in kube-scheduler
fix: Run 'kubectl top pod -n kube-system' to identify which control plane pod is consuming excessive CPU or memory, then investigate the specific component (e.g., reduce API request volume, fix etcd disk I/O, resolve controller reconciliation loops, or address scheduler backlog)
concept: The kube-system namespace hosts Kubernetes core infrastructure. Metrics Server collects stats from kubelets to enable resource usage visibility. Each control plane component has distinct failure modes: apiserver can be saturated by requests, etcd is sensitive to disk and CPU pressure, controller-manager can enter runaway loops, and scheduler can develop backlogs.

error: Cluster outage requiring control plane pod status inspection
root_cause: Unknown control plane component failure; diagnosis required by inspecting pod states across kube-system namespace
fix: Run `kubectl get pods -n kube-system -o wide` to list all infrastructure pods, their current state, and which node they are running on; identify failed or unhealthy components from the output
concept: Control plane components (etcd, kube-apiserver, kube-scheduler, kube-controller-manager, CoreDNS, kube-proxy, CNI agents) all run as pods in the kube-system namespace; the -o wide flag exposes node placement and pod IP for deeper triage
